The process remains untouched. - **Output:** Device IP, MAC, vendor OUI, firmware version, protocol, role, communication patterns. - **Limitations:** Cannot detect silent (dormant) assets that are powered on but not communicating. #### **Technique 2: Active Network Scanning (Controlled)** - **How it works:** Uses OT-aware scanners (e.g., **Tenable.ot**, **Forescout eyeInspect**) that send low-impact, **protocol-specific queries** (e.g., Modbus "Read Coils" instead of SYN scans). - **Why restricted:** Even "safe" active queries can cause **denial-of-service on legacy RTUs** or trigger safety interlocks in DCS systems. - **Best practice:** Always perform active scanning during **planned maintenance windows** and from a **Purdue Level 3.5 / DMZ** position. #### **Technique 3: Agent-Based Discovery** - **How it works:** A lightweight software agent is installed on each endpoint (HMI, Engineering Station, Windows Server) to report asset metadata to a central manager. - **Why limited in OT:** Many OT devices (PLCs, RTUs, sensors) **cannot host agents** — they run proprietary RTOS or have no OS at all. Agents are therefore viable only on **Level 2 and Level 3** assets. #### **Technique 4: Agentless / Log-Based Discovery** - **How it works:** Ingests logs from existing infrastructure — **Windows Event Logs**, **Syslog from firewalls**, **SCADA/EMS servers**, **DNS logs**, **NetFlow/IPFIX**, and **Active Directory** queries. - **Why complementary:** Provides visibility into assets even when passive sensors miss them. #### **Technique 5: Protocol-Specific Discovery & Enumeration** Each industrial protocol has a **discovery handshake**: - **Modbus (Port 502):** No native discovery; relies on broadcast "Read" queries. - **DNP3 (Port 20000):** "Read Class 0/1/2/3" enumeration. - **PROFINET (UDP/TCP):** DCP (Discovery and Configuration Protocol) identifies device name, vendor, and IP. - **EtherNet/IP (UDP 44818, TCP 44816):** CIP "List Identity" service. - **IEC 61850:** IED Name and Logical Device (LD) discovery via MMS. - **BACnet (UDP 47808):** "Who-Is" / "I-Am" handshake. - **OPC UA (Port 4840):** "FindServers" and "GetEndpoints" requests. #### **Technique 6: Physical & Manual Site Survey** - **How it works:** On-site walkdown, photographing device labels, barcode/RFID scanning, and integration with **EAM/CMMS** (Enterprise Asset Management / Computerised Maintenance Management Systems). - **Why essential:** Many legacy field devices (Level 0–1) are invisible to network sensors. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet | Technique | Intrusiveness | OT Coverage (Level 0–5) | Real-Time | Typical Tool | |---|---|---|---|---| | Passive NTA | **None** (zero packets injected) | L0–L3 (where traffic flows) | Yes | Nozomi, Claroty, Dragos | | Active Scanning | Medium–High | L1–L3 | No | Tenable.ot, Forescout | | Agent-Based | Low (per device) | L2–L3 only | Yes | Microsoft Defender for IoT, Quest | | Agentless / Log | None | L2–L5 | Near real-time | Splunk, QRadar | | Protocol Enumeration | Variable | L1–L2 (controllers) | Yes | Wireshark, OT dissectors | | Physical Survey | None | L0–L1 (field devices) | Manual | EAM, CMMS, RFID | ### 2.4 The Purdue Model — Where Discovery Happens | Purdue Level | Function | Discovery Technique Used | |---|---|---| | **Level 5** | Enterprise / Internet | IT tools + CMDB | | **Level 4** | Site Business Network | Log-based + Active (safe) | | **Level 3.5** | **IDMZ (Industrial DMZ)** | All techniques converge here | | **Level 3** | Site Operations (Historian, MES) | Agent + Passive + Active | | **Level 2** | Supervisory (HMI, SCADA) | Agent + Passive | | **Level 1** | Basic Control (PLC, RTU, DCS Controller) | Passive + Protocol Enumeration | | **Level 0** | Physical Process (Sensors, Actuators) | **Physical Survey only** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In a **smart-grid SCADA deployment**, asset discovery is the first step toward **NERC-CIP-007-6 R1** compliance (System Security Management). In a **petrochemical plant**, continuous asset discovery enables **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** integrity monitoring, ensuring SIL-rated devices are not running with outdated firmware. ## 3. Step-by-Step Methodology & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 The OT Asset Discovery Lifecycle (Step-by-Step) The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know the **sequential engineering methodology** for OT asset discovery. The lifecycle is iterative (continuous) and consists of seven phases: #### **Step 1: Scope Definition & Network Mapping** - Identify the **OT zones and conduits** per IEC 62443-3-2. - Document the **physical topology** (switches, routers, firewalls, IDMZ boundaries). - Obtain the **P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)** and **network architecture diagram** from the plant. #### **Step 2: Sensor Placement (TAP / SPAN)** - Install **passive sensors** at strategic choke points: - Between **Level 1 and Level 2** (control vs supervisory). - At the **IDMZ boundary** (Level 3 ↔ Level 3.5). - On ring-protocol networks (e.g., PRP, HSR) using a **hardware TAP**, never a software mirror. - Verify sensors do not exceed the **MTBF-critical path** of the network. #### **Step 3: Baseline Traffic Capture** - Capture 7–14 days of traffic to establish the **baseline communication matrix** (who talks to whom, on which protocol, at what frequency). - Compute the **protocol distribution** (e.g., 60% Modbus, 25% PROFINET, 15% DNP3). #### **Step 4: Deep Packet Inspection & Fingerprinting** - Apply **DPI engines** to extract: - **Layer 2:** MAC address, vendor OUI (first 24 bits). - **Layer 3/4:** IP, port, TCP/UDP. - **Layer 7:** Vendor ID, Product Code, Revision (from CIP), DNP3 device address, PROFINET device name. - Cross-reference with **ICS-CERT advisories** and **vendor vulnerability databases**. #### **Step 5: Active Verification (During Maintenance Window)** - For assets that remained silent in Step 3, schedule a **controlled active scan** using OT-aware tools. - Limit scan rate to **< 100 pps** to avoid overwhelming legacy controllers. - Document all scan results in the **change management ticket**. #### **Step 6: Correlation, Enrichment & CMDB Update** - Merge data from passive sensors, active scans, agent reports, and physical surveys. - Enrich with: **CVSS score**, **CISA KEV catalog match**, **criticality rating** (Safety, Production, Information), **patch status**. - Push the authoritative inventory into the **CMDB / OT inventory database**. #### **Step 7: Continuous Monitoring & Drift Detection** - Run the passive sensor in **monitoring mode 24/7**. - Trigger **alerts** on: new device connection, firmware change, unexpected protocol, unauthorised engineering workstation, rogue HMI. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Discovery Technique Selection Logic | Discovery Scenario | Recommended Primary Technique | Justification (Why) | |---|---|---| | Greenfield plant commissioning | Active Scanning + Agent + Physical | Need complete baseline; no live process risk. | | Brownfield live plant (24×7) | Passive NTA + Log-based | Cannot risk active probing. | | Legacy serial (RS-485) network | Physical Survey + Protocol converters (serial-to-Ethernet TAP) | No IP-based discovery possible. | | WirelessHART / ISA-100 field network | Vendor-specific WIDS + Physical | Wireless requires spectrum-aware sensors. | | Cloud-connected IIoT edge gateway | Agent + Log-based | Gateway OS supports agents. | | Safety Instrumented System (SIL 2/3) | Passive NTA only | SIS must remain untouched. | ### 3.3 The Discovery Confidence Score (Derived Metric) Many commercial tools compute a **discovery confidence score** per asset. The KTU examiner may ask for the formula: $$ C_{asset} = w_1 P + w_2 A + w_3 L + w_4 M $$ where: - $P$ = **Passive detection confidence** ∈ $\lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$ - $A$ = **Active verification result** ∈ $\lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$ - $L$ = **Log-based corroboration** ∈ $\lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$ - $M$ = **Manual survey confirmation** ∈ $\lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$ - $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ = weighted importance, such that $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ A typical weighting (favouring passive for OT) would be $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.2$, $w_3 = 0.2$, $w_4 = 0.1$, yielding: $$ C_{asset} = 0.5P + 0.2A + 0.2L + 0.1M $$ An asset is considered **authoritatively inventoried** when $C_{asset} \geq 0.7$. > [!NOTE] > **Why this formula matters:** It is not a "calculation" exam topic, but examiners frequently ask students to **explain how confidence is built** in an OT inventory. Use this formula as a structured answer. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1 — OT Asset Discovery Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) This Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end OT asset discovery pipeline**, including data sources, processing engines, and outputs. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[OT Network Traffic TAP SPAN] --> B1[Passive DPI Engine] A2[Active OT Scanner] --> B2[Active Probe Handler] A3[Endpoint Agents] --> B3[Agent Telemetry Collector] A4[Syslog and NetFlow] --> B4[Log Aggregation Engine] A5[Physical Survey and EAM] --> B5[Manual Data Importer] B1 --> C1[Asset Correlation and Enrichment Engine] B2 --> C1 B3 --> C1 B4 --> C1 B5 --> C1 C1 --> D1[Asset Database CMDB] C1 --> D2[Vulnerability Mapping CVE to Firmware] C1 --> D3[Purdue Level Classification] C1 --> D4[Risk Score Calculation] D1 --> E1[SIEM and SOC Dashboard] D2 --> E1 D3 --> E1 D4 --> E1 D1 --> E2[Compliance Reports NERC CIP IEC 62443] ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Passive vs Active Discovery Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Asset Discovery Required] --> Q1{Is process live and 24x7?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q1A{Is asset on critical safety loop?} Q1A -- Yes --> P1[Use Passive DPI Only] Q1A -- No --> P2[Passive DPI plus Log Based] Q1 -- No --> Q1B{Is maintenance window approved?} Q1B -- Yes --> A1[Active OT Aware Scan] Q1B -- No --> P1 A1 --> Output[Authoritative Asset Inventory] P1 --> Output P2 --> Output ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Purdue Model with Discovery Coverage Overlay ```mermaid flowchart TB L5[Level 5 Enterprise Network and Internet] --> L4[Level 4 Site Business Planning and Logistics] L4 --> L35[Level 3.5 IDMZ Industrial DMZ] L35 --> L3[Level 3 Site Operations Historian MES] L3 --> L2[Level 2 Supervisory HMI SCADA] L2 --> L1[Level 1 Basic Control PLC RTU DCS] L1 --> L0[Level 0 Physical Process Sensors Actuators] L0 -. Physical Survey .-> D[Discovery Coverage] L1 -. Passive DPI and Protocol Enum .-> D L2 -. Agent plus Passive .-> D L3 -. Active plus Passive plus Agent .-> D L35 -. All Techniques Converge .-> D L4 -. Log Based and CMDB .-> D L5 -. IT Discovery Tools .-> D ``` ### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Industrial Protocol Discovery Handshake Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Sensor as OT Discovery Sensor participant PLC as PLC or RTU participant EMS as Asset Inventory DB Sensor->>PLC: Passive Sniff Modbus TCP PLC-->>Sensor: Modbus Read Coils Response Sensor->>Sensor: Extract Vendor Unit ID Function Code Sensor->>EMS: Asset Report IP MAC Vendor Firmware EMS-->>Sensor: Acknowledgement and CVE Enrichment Note over Sensor,PLC: No packets were injected by sensor
Purely passive observation ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT634]** **Define OT asset discovery. Why is passive discovery preferred over active scanning in OT environments? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** OT asset discovery is the **continuous process of identifying, classifying, and inventorying all hardware, software, and communication components** in an Industrial Control System / SCADA environment, including PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, IEDs, and the industrial protocols they use. Passive discovery is preferred because: 1. It **does not inject any packets** into the OT network, eliminating the risk of disrupting real-time deterministic control loops. 2. It avoids triggering **safety interlocks, alarm floods, or equipment shutdowns** in DCS and SIL-rated systems. 3. It supports **legacy controllers** (e.g., Modbus RTU, serial RS-485) that cannot respond to active queries safely. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "Nmap is used for OT asset discovery." This is **incorrect in live OT environments**. Nmap is an IT tool. **Nmap is acceptable only inside a lab replica or during scheduled maintenance windows**, never on a live plant floor. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT634]** **List and briefly explain any three techniques used for OT asset discovery. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Passive Network Traffic Analysis (NTA):** A TAP or SPAN port feeds industrial traffic to a sensor that performs **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** of protocols like Modbus, DNP3, and PROFINET. It is non-intrusive. 2. **Active OT-Aware Scanning:** Uses specialised scanners (Tenable.ot, Forescout) to send **low-impact protocol queries** during maintenance windows to enumerate devices. 3. **Agent-Based Discovery:** A lightweight agent on Windows-based OT endpoints (HMI, Engineering Station) reports asset metadata centrally; **not feasible for PLCs/RTUs** that lack an OS. 4. **Physical / Manual Site Survey:** On-site walkdown, barcode scanning, and EAM/CMMS integration for **Level 0–1** field devices that are network-invisible. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [Internal Choice Option A]** **(a)** Explain the **Purdue Model** and discuss, level by level, the **appropriate OT asset discovery technique** for each level. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** approach for OT asset discovery. How does it fingerprint **Modbus, DNP3, and PROFINET** devices? **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — Purdue Model & Per-Level Discovery:** The **Purdue Model** (ISA-99 / IEC 62443) segments industrial networks into **five operational levels (0–4)** plus an **IDMZ (Level 3.5)**: $$ \text{Levels} = \lbrace 0, 1, 2, 3, 3.5, 4, 5 \rbrace $$ | Level | Function | Discovery Technique | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | **L0** | Physical process (sensors, valves) | **Physical survey + RFID/barcode** | No IP; no OS; invisible to network sensors. | | **L1** | Basic control (PLCs, RTUs, DCS) | **Passive DPI + protocol enumeration** | Real-time; must not be probed actively. | | **L2** | Supervisory (HMI, SCADA) | **Agent + Passive DPI** | HMI/SCADA are Windows-based; agents are feasible. | | **L3** | Operations (Historian, MES) | **Active + Passive + Agent** | Less time-critical; safe for hybrid discovery. | | **L3.5** | IDMZ | **All techniques converge** | Choke point; ideal sensor placement. | | **L4** | Site business (ERP, email) | **Log-based + CMDB sync** | IT-managed; classical IT tools work here. | | **L5** | Enterprise / Internet | **IT discovery (Nmap, AD)** | Standard IT environment. | The strategic principle is: **the lower the Purdue level, the more conservative the discovery method must be.** L0–L1 require non-intrusive, often manual methods; L4–L5 can use conventional IT tools. --- **Model Solution (b) — DPI for Modbus, DNP3, and PROFINET:** **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** parses industrial protocol payloads up to **Layer 7** to extract device fingerprints without injecting traffic. **1. Modbus TCP (Port 502):** - Parses the **MBAP (Modbus Application Protocol) header**: Transaction ID, Protocol ID (always 0x0000), Length, **Unit ID**. - Inspects **Function Code** (0x01–0x06, 0x0F, 0x10, etc.) and request payload. - Fingerprints vendor via **Unit ID** and request pattern (e.g., Schneider uses 0x01, Siemens 0xFF, Wago proprietary 0x64–0x7F). - Limitation: Modbus has **no authentication and no device descriptor** — fingerprinting relies on heuristics. **2. DNP3 (Port 20000, sometimes 19999):** - Parses the **DNP3 Data Link Layer** (0x0564 sync bytes, length, control, destination, source). - Inspects the **Transport Layer** (FIR, FIN, SEQ) and **Application Layer** (Function Codes: 0x00 Read, 0x01 Write, 0x02 Select, 0x03 Operate). - The **Source/Destination Address** field is a strong fingerprint (each RTU has a unique 16-bit DNP3 address). - Object headers (Group, Variation) reveal whether the device is a **meter, RTU, or IED**. **3. PROFINET (UDP 34962/34963, TCP 34964):** - Uses **DCP (Discovery and Configuration Protocol)** with multicast frames: - **Identify Request** → **Identify Response** containing the device's: - **Name of Station** (e.g., "plc-line3-station1") - **Vendor ID** (e.g., Siemens = 0x002A) - **Device ID** - **Role** (Controller vs Device) - PROFINET is the **most fingerprint-rich** of the three because DCP was designed for discovery. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Modular Answers:** When asked to "explain DPI," students often describe only Nmap-style port scanning. DPI is **application-layer protocol parsing** (Layer 7), not port scanning (Layer 4). Using the wrong terminology costs 2–3 marks. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Internal Choice Option B]** **(a)** Compare **active and passive asset discovery** in OT environments. Discuss at least **four distinguishing parameters**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **refinery** has a live OT network with 1,200 PLCs (Siemens S7-1500 and Allen-Bradley ControlLogix), 60 HMIs, 4 Historians, and a Level 3.5 IDMZ. The CISO wants a **complete asset inventory** within 30 days **without disturbing operations**. Design a **step-by-step discovery plan** using multiple techniques. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — Active vs Passive Comparison:** | Parameter | Active Discovery | Passive Discovery | |---|---|---| | **Packet Injection** | Yes — sends probes | No — listens only | | **OT Safety Risk** | **High** — can crash legacy controllers | **Negligible** — zero intrusion | | **Detects Silent Assets** | Yes (queries the silent device) | No (silent assets remain invisible) | | **Detection Latency** | At scan time (one-shot) | Real-time / continuous | | **Coverage of L0 Devices** | None | None (both require manual survey) | | **Compliance Acceptance** | Restricted to maintenance windows | Accepted 24/7 | | **Typical Tools** | Tenable.ot, Forescout | Nozomi, Claroty, Dragos | | **Firmware Fingerprint Accuracy** | High (queries return model) | Medium (heuristic-based) | **Conclusion:** In OT, a **hybrid model is the industry best practice** — predominantly passive, with tightly-controlled active scanning as a complement. --- **Model Solution (b) — Discovery Plan for the Refinery:** **Phase 1: Days 1–3 — Scope and Architecture** - Acquire **P&ID diagrams**, network topology, and PLC/HMI asset registers from the plant. - Identify **IDMZ (Level 3.5)** and install **hardware TAPs** between L2↔L3, L3↔L3.5, and L1↔L2. **Phase 2: Days 4–14 — Continuous Passive Monitoring** - Deploy **Nozomi Networks / Claroty / Dragos** sensors at the four choke points. - Perform **DPI on S7Comm (Siemens) and EtherNet/IP (CIP, Allen-Bradley)** protocols. - Build the **baseline communication matrix** of 1,200 PLCs ↔ 60 HMIs ↔ 4 Historians. **Phase 3: Days 5–10 — Agent and Log-Based** - Install **endpoint agents** on the 60 HMIs and 4 Historians (Windows-based). - Ingest **Syslog from firewalls** and **Windows Event Logs** from Engineering Workstations. - Sync with the **EAM/CMMS** for physical inventory. **Phase 4: Day 15–25 — Controlled Active Verification** - Schedule **maintenance windows** (one per refinery unit) for active OT-aware scanning. - Use **Tenable.ot** to query S7Comm (`S7_GetCPUInfo`) and CIP (`List Identity`). - Limit scan rate to < 100 pps to avoid controller overload. **Phase 5: Days 26–30 — Correlation, Enrichment, and Reporting** - Merge all sources into the **central CMDB**. - Enrich with **Siemens and Rockwell CVE databases**. - Compute **Criticality Score** and **Discovery Confidence** per asset. - Generate **IEC 62443 / NERC-CIP compliance report**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Plan Answers:** Students often skip the **IDMZ (Level 3.5) mention**. The IDMZ is the **strategic discovery choke point** and the answer's "wow factor" for full marks. Also, **never recommend Nmap on live PLCs** — that is an automatic deduction. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - OT asset discovery = **continuous, non-intrusive identification** of all ICS/SCADA components. - **Passive DPI** is the gold standard for live OT; **active scanning** is restricted to maintenance windows. - Six core techniques: **Passive NTA, Active OT-Aware, Agent, Agentless/Log, Protocol Enumeration, Physical Survey.** - The **Purdue Model** (L0–L5 + L3.5 IDMZ) dictates technique selection per level. - DPI fingerprinting is **protocol-specific**: Modbus (Unit ID, Function Code), DNP3 (Link/Transport/App layers), PROFINET (DCP Identify), EtherNet/IP (CIP List Identity), BACnet (Who-Is), IEC 61850 (MMS). - **Do NOT run IT tools (Nmap, Nessus)** directly on live OT networks. - Asset discovery is mandated by **IEC 62443, NIST CSF (Identify), NERC-CIP**. - Real-world attacks (Stuxnet, TRITON, Industroyer) exploited **unknown/undiscovered assets** — proving the life-saving value of continuous discovery. - Always correlate with **CMDB, EAM, and CVE databases** for enrichment. - The **IDMZ (Level 3.5)** is the strategic sensor placement point.

topics.title: Importance of maintaining an up-to-date asset inventory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Importance of maintaining an up-to-date asset inventory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Operational Technology (OT) Threat Prevention**, an **Asset Inventory** is a comprehensive, continuously updated, authoritative repository of all physical and logical hardware, software, firmware, network, and personnel assets that exist within an industrial control system (ICS) / supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) environment. An *up-to-date* asset inventory is one whose contents reflect the *live operational state* of the plant floor at any given moment, including configuration parameters, firmware versions, network topology, and ownership metadata.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"An asset inventory in OT is the foundational cybersecurity control that records every programmable logic controller (PLC), remote terminal unit (RTU), intelligent electronic device (IED), human-machine interface (HMI), engineering workstation, sensor, actuator, and network device under the purview of the industrial operator, along with their attributes, locations, criticality ratings, and patch states."*

The three pillars of an up-to-date OT asset inventory are:

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Discovery** | Automated or agent-based identification of devices on OT networks. |
| **Classification** | Tagging each asset with type, role, criticality, owner, and exposure. |
| **Continuous Refresh** | Synchronisation with change-management, CMDB, and ticketing systems. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Patient Registry"

Imagine a hospital emergency room. Before a doctor can treat a patient, the front desk **must** have the patient registered: name, age, allergies, blood group, current medications, and pre-existing conditions. If the registry is missing a patient who walked in five minutes ago, that patient may receive the wrong blood type, an allergic reaction could go unnoticed, or they could simply be left unattended.

An **OT environment behaves identically**. The plant floor is the *emergency room*, and every PLC, RTU, sensor, and switch is a *patient* needing protection. The **asset inventory is the patient registry** — without it, the security team cannot:

- Diagnose vulnerabilities (allergies)
- Apply the correct patch (medicine)
- Quarantine compromised devices (isolation ward)
- Report compliance to regulators (insurance claims)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> In OT, "unknown assets" are the single largest contributor to *successful cyber-kinetic attacks* (attacks that bridge the cyber-physical divide and cause physical harm). The 2010 **Stuxnet** incident famously exploited *uninventoried* Siemens S7-400 PLCs running undisclosed STEP 7 firmware variants.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Asset Inventory Health

| Metric | Symbol | Benchmark Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Asset Coverage Ratio** | ACR | **≥ 98%** (IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1) |
| **Asset Staleness Window** | ASW | **≤ 24 hours** |
| **Mean Time to Inventory Update** | MTTIU | **≤ 4 hours** |
| **Unmanaged Asset Ratio** | UAR | **< 2%** |

These metrics are bolded because they will appear as direct KTU 2024 two-mark or three-mark definition questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Core Reasons Asset Inventory Is Non-Negotiable in OT

The following structured reasoning is what a KTU board examiner expects when a 14-mark question on "Importance of Asset Inventory" is posed.

### Reason 1 — Establishing the Attack Surface Baseline

The *attack surface* of an OT network is the union of all reachable endpoints, services, and protocols. Without an inventory, the attack surface is mathematically **undefined**. With an inventory, the attack surface is a finite, enumerable set:

$$
AS = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} E_i \times P_i \times V_i
$$

Where:
- $N$ = total number of inventoried assets
- $E_i$ = exposure factor of asset $i$ (network reachability)
- $P_i$ = number of open ports/protocols on asset $i$
- $V_i$ = number of known CVEs applicable to asset $i$

> [!TIP]
> The *Why*: Security investment (CAPEX/OPEX) is justified by measurable risk reduction. You cannot argue for a firewall if you cannot list the assets behind it.

### Reason 2 — Vulnerability Management & Patch Correlation

Modern vulnerability scanners (e.g., Tenable.ot, Claroty, Nozomi Networks) output CVE identifiers. To match a CVE to a patch, the system must know:
- The exact **hardware model**
- The exact **firmware version**
- The exact **logical function** (e.g., "Modbus gateway on VLAN 30")

All three data points come from the inventory. Missing any one means *the patch cannot be safely applied*, and in OT, a wrongly applied patch can halt a refinery or destabilise a power grid.

### Reason 3 — Incident Response & Forensic Containment

When an alert fires (e.g., a SCADA HMI suddenly beaconing to an external IP), the incident responder's **first action** is to consult the inventory. The response time is given by:

$$
MTTI_{response} = T_{lookup} + T_{isolate} + T_{remediate}
$$

If $T_{lookup}$ is large because the asset is *not* in the inventory, the entire IR timeline slips, and the attacker achieves dwell time.

### Reason 4 — Regulatory & Standards Compliance

The following regulations **mandate** an accurate, up-to-date OT asset inventory:

- **IEC 62443-3-3** — System Security Requirements (SR 1.1: Identification)
- **NIST SP 800-82r3** — Guide to Operational Technology (OT) Security
- **NERC CIP-007-6** — System Security Management (North American power grid)
- **ISA/IEC 62443-2-4** — Service Provider security
- **India's CEA (Cyber Security in Power Sector) Guidelines 2021** — explicit asset inventory requirement for all critical infrastructure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Non-compliance penalties under NERC CIP can reach **\$1 million per day per violation** — a figure the KTU examiner loves to cite in case-study questions.

### Reason 5 — Lifecycle Management & EOL/EOS Tracking

OT assets have lifecycles 20–40 years long. An inventory tracks:
- **End-of-Life (EOL)** date — vendor stops manufacturing
- **End-of-Support (EOS)** date — vendor stops issuing patches
- **End-of-Service-Life (EOSL)** — internal decommissioning date

Assets past EOSL are ticking time bombs — no patches means no CVE remediation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Asset Coverage Ratio (ACR)** | $ACR = \dfrac{A_{known}}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $A_{known}$ = inventoried assets (count), $A_{total}$ = estimated total assets (count) | Quantifies visibility gap |
| 2 | **Unmanaged Asset Ratio (UAR)** | $UAR = \dfrac{A_{total} - A_{known}}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Same as above | Inverse of ACR; ideal **< 2%** |
| 3 | **Attack Surface Index (ASI)** | $ASI = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (E_i \cdot P_i \cdot V_i)$ | $E$ = exposure, $P$ = ports, $V$ = CVEs | Used for risk prioritisation |
| 4 | **Mean Time to Inventory Update** | $MTTIU = \dfrac{1}{M}\sum_{j=1}^{M}(t_{detected,j} - t_{onboarded,j})$ | $t$ in hours, $M$ = sample count | Quality KPI of inventory refresh process |
| 5 | **Critical Asset Risk Score (CARS)** | $CARS = C \times (1 - P_{mitigated})$ | $C$ = criticality (1–5), $P_{mitigated}$ = patch coverage (0–1) | Identifies top-risk assets |
| 6 | **Inventory Staleness** | $S = t_{now} - t_{last\_sync}$ | $t$ in hours | **S ≤ 24 h** = healthy |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the use of `\times` and `\cdot` instead of the vertical pipe `\|`. This avoids breaking the markdown table syntax — a key KTU note-formatting requirement.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a typical **smart factory** (Industry 4.0), the asset inventory feeds the following downstream systems:
- **SIEM** (Security Information & Event Management) — for log enrichment
- **SOAR** (Security Orchestration, Automation & Response) — for playbooks
- **GRC** (Governance, Risk, Compliance) — for audit evidence
- **CMMS** (Computerised Maintenance Management System) — for predictive maintenance

Without a single source of truth, these four systems will each maintain *their own* partial view, leading to **alerts being missed** and **patches being duplicated**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Asset Coverage Ratio (ACR)

The derivation of ACR proceeds in **six** explicit steps. Skipping any step will cost marks in the KTU 2024 board valuation.

### Step 1 — Define the universe of assets

Let $A$ be the set of *all* physical and logical assets existing on the OT network at time $t$. This set includes **every** device, even those that are unknown to the security team.

$$
A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_N\}
$$

Where $N = \vert A \vert$ is the cardinality of the asset set.

### Step 2 — Define the discovered (known) subset

Let $K \subseteq A$ be the subset of assets that have been discovered, onboarded, and recorded in the inventory database.

$$
K = \{k_1, k_2, k_3, \ldots, k_M\}, \quad M \le N
$$

### Step 3 — Define the unknown (shadow) subset

The remaining assets are the *unknown* or *shadow* assets:

$$
U = A \setminus K
$$

### Step 4 — Express counts

The total number of assets is the sum of known and unknown:

$$
N = M + \vert U \vert
$$

### Step 5 — Formulate the ratio

The Asset Coverage Ratio is defined as the fraction of assets that are *known*:

$$
ACR = \dfrac{M}{N} = \dfrac{M}{M + \vert U \vert}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to percentage for reporting

$$
ACR_{\%} = ACR \times 100 = \dfrac{M}{M + \vert U \vert} \times 100
$$

This derivation is **critical** because a 14-mark KTU question on this topic often asks: *"A plant has 1,200 OT devices, of which 87 are unmanaged. Calculate the Asset Coverage Ratio and interpret its compliance status."* The marks are awarded for *showing every algebraic step above*, not just the final number.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Pattern

> **Question:** A refinery OT network has 1,250 total assets. The inventory database contains 1,178 entries. An additional 23 assets were discovered during a passive network sweep last week. Calculate:
> (a) The new $ACR_{\%}$
> (b) The number of remaining unknown assets
> (c) Whether the plant meets the IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1 requirement of $ACR \ge 98\%$.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Updated Asset Coverage Ratio**

First, update the known-asset count after the sweep:

$$
M_{new} = 1{,}178 + 23 = 1{,}201
$$

Apply the formula:

$$
ACR_{\%} = \dfrac{M_{new}}{N} \times 100 = \dfrac{1{,}201}{1{,}250} \times 100
$$

Performing the division:

$$
ACR_{\%} = 0.9608 \times 100 = 96.08\%
$$

**Part (b) — Remaining unknown assets**

$$
\vert U \vert = N - M_{new} = 1{,}250 - 1{,}201 = 49
$$

**Part (c) — Compliance check**

The required threshold is **98%**. The plant's actual value is **96.08%**, which is **below** the threshold. Therefore, the plant **does NOT comply** with IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1. The remediation plan must include:

$$
\Delta_{assets} = N \times 0.98 - M_{new} = 1{,}225 - 1{,}201 = 24
$$

The plant must discover and onboard **at least 24 more assets** to reach compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfall:** Students often write "ACR = 96.08" instead of "ACR = 96.08%". Always include the unit. One mark is reserved for unit correctness.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Asset Visibility to Risk Reduction

| Inventory Maturity Level | ACR Range | Typical Risk Posture | Business Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 0 — Blind** | 0% – 40% | Critical, unmanaged, cannot comply | High probability of successful cyber-physical attack |
| **Level 1 — Aware** | 40% – 70% | High; many shadow devices | Cannot pass any external audit |
| **Level 2 — Managed** | 70% – 90% | Moderate; some gaps remain | Partial compliance, fines possible |
| **Level 3 — Quantified** | 90% – 98% | Low; risk register is reliable | Meets most regulatory baselines |
| **Level 4 — Optimised** | **98% – 100%** | **Minimal; continuous discovery active** | Full IEC 62443 & NIST compliance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — OT Asset Inventory Lifecycle Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end functional flow** of an OT asset inventory system, from network discovery through continuous synchronisation with the CMDB and SIEM.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DiscoveryLayer["DISCOVERY LAYER"]
        A1[Passive Network Tap] --> A2[SPAN Port Collector]
        A3[Active ARP Sweep] --> A2
        A4[Agent Based Probe] --> A2
        A5[Engineering Station Logs] --> A2
    end

    A2 --> B1[Asset Fingerprint Engine]

    subgraph ClassificationLayer["CLASSIFICATION LAYER"]
        B1 --> C1[Protocol Parser Modbus DNP3 Profinet]
        B1 --> C2[Firmware Version Detector]
        B1 --> C3[Vendor MAC Lookup]
        B1 --> C4[Asset Role Classifier PLC HMI RTU IED]
    end

    C1 --> D1[Normalized Asset Record]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1

    D1 --> E1[Inventory Database CMDB]

    subgraph EnrichmentLayer["ENRICHMENT LAYER"]
        E1 --> F1[CVE Correlation Engine]
        E1 --> F2[Criticality Scorer]
        E1 --> F3[Ownership Attribution]
        E1 --> F4[EOL EOSL Tracker]
    end

    F1 --> G1[SIEM Log Enrichment]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1

    G1 --> H1[Incident Response Playbook]
    G1 --> H2[Patch Management Workflow]
    G1 --> H3[Compliance Audit Report]

    E1 -.continuous sync.-> I1[Change Management System]
    E1 -.delta updates.-> I2[GRC Dashboard]
```

### 4.2 Diagram Walkthrough (for KTU theory answers)

| Node | Functional Role |
|---|---|
| A1, A3, A4, A5 | Discovery inputs (active + passive) |
| A2 | Aggregator that unifies raw observation streams |
| B1 | Fingerprint engine — produces a candidate identity |
| C1–C4 | Multi-dimensional classification (protocol, firmware, vendor, role) |
| D1 | Canonical asset record (the *golden record*) |
| E1 | The inventory itself (often called the CMDB) |
| F1–F4 | Contextual enrichment for downstream consumers |
| G1 | Pushes enriched context into the SIEM |
| H1–H3 | Downstream use-cases: IR, patching, compliance |
| I1, I2 | Bi-directional sync with change/GRC systems |

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed arrows represent *continuous synchronisation*, while the solid arrows represent *real-time event flow*. This distinction is often tested in KTU descriptive questions worth 4–5 marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define "Asset Inventory" in the context of OT cybersecurity. List any four attributes that must be captured for each asset.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An asset inventory in OT cybersecurity is a comprehensive, continuously updated repository of all physical and logical components — including PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, sensors, controllers, and network devices — within an industrial control system, along with their configuration, location, and ownership metadata.
>
> Four mandatory attributes per asset are:
> 1. **Unique Asset ID** and hostname
> 2. **Hardware model** and **firmware version**
> 3. **Network address** (IP, MAC) and **VLAN/subnet**
> 4. **Asset owner** and **criticality rating** (e.g., Safety Integrity Level)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is meant by "Unmanaged Asset" in an OT environment? Why is it considered a high-risk entity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **unmanaged asset** is any device connected to the OT network that is *not* registered, monitored, or governed by the organisation's cybersecurity policies. It exists in the *shadow* layer beyond the inventory's visibility.
>
> It is high-risk because: (i) it does not receive patches, (ii) it cannot be authenticated by the SIEM, (iii) it can serve as a pivot for lateral movement, and (iv) it violates IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1 identification requirements.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *KTU ESE pattern: Module Internal Choice — answer ANY ONE of the two.*

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]

**Q: With reference to OT threat prevention, explain in detail the importance of maintaining an up-to-date asset inventory. Your answer must include (a) the role of asset inventory in vulnerability management and incident response, and (b) a numerical illustration of the Asset Coverage Ratio for a plant with 2,400 total OT devices and 96 unmanaged assets, with an interpretation against IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1 (threshold 98%).**

#### (a) Role in Vulnerability Management and Incident Response (7 marks)

**Vulnerability Management Role (3.5 marks):**
An up-to-date asset inventory is the *pre-requisite* for vulnerability management. The workflow is:

1. **Asset Discovery** → identifies devices on the network via passive listening, active probing, or agent registration.
2. **Attribute Capture** → records firmware, OS, protocol, and configuration for each device.
3. **CVE Correlation** → the inventory is queried against vulnerability databases (e.g., NVD, ICS-CERT) to identify applicable CVEs.
4. **Patch Prioritisation** → assets with critical CVE exposure and high operational criticality are patched first.
5. **Verification** → post-patch scanning confirms remediation.

If the inventory is stale, CVEs are matched against *phantom* or *outdated* firmware versions, leading to either false positives (wasted patching effort) or false negatives (real CVEs going unpatched).

**Incident Response Role (3.5 marks):**
During an active incident, the inventory enables the CSIRT to:
- **Identify** the affected device in seconds (asset lookup by IP/MAC).
- **Contain** it via pre-authorised network-segmentation rules tied to the asset's VLAN.
- **Eradicate** the threat by applying known-good firmware baselines.
- **Recover** by restoring from a vendor-validated image.
- **Report** to regulators with full forensic chain-of-custody anchored in the inventory's timestamps.

The mean-time-to-respond (MTTR) is directly proportional to inventory accuracy. Studies show that an ACR below 90% increases MTTR by a factor of **3 to 5**.

#### (b) Numerical Illustration (7 marks)

**Given:** $N = 2{,}400$ total assets, $\vert U \vert = 96$ unmanaged assets.

**Step 1 — Compute known assets:**

$$
M = N - \vert U \vert = 2{,}400 - 96 = 2{,}304
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Asset Coverage Ratio:**

$$
ACR_{\%} = \dfrac{M}{N} \times 100 = \dfrac{2{,}304}{2{,}400} \times 100
$$

Performing the arithmetic:

$$
ACR_{\%} = 0.96 \times 100 = 96.00\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compliance check against IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1:**

The IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1 threshold is **98%**. The plant is at **96.00%**, which is *below* the threshold. Therefore the plant **fails** compliance.

**Step 4 — Remediation delta:**

$$
\Delta_{assets} = (0.98 \times N) - M = 2{,}352 - 2{,}304 = 48
$$

The plant must discover and onboard **48 additional assets** to meet the 98% benchmark.

**Step 5 — Alternative interpretation using UAR:**

$$
UAR = \dfrac{\vert U \vert}{N} \times 100 = \dfrac{96}{2{,}400} \times 100 = 4.00\%
$$

A UAR of 4% exceeds the *best-practice* 2% ceiling by 2 percentage points, confirming non-compliance.

#### Distribution of Marks (Internal Examiner Key)

| Sub-part | Marks Awarded For |
|---|---|
| (a) Vulnerability mgmt explanation | 3.5 |
| (a) Incident response explanation | 3.5 |
| (b) Steps 1 & 2 (math) | 2.0 |
| (b) Step 3 (compliance verdict) | 1.5 |
| (b) Steps 4 & 5 (remediation + UAR) | 2.0 |
| Clarity, units, and conclusion | 1.5 |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) Discuss the relationship between an OT asset inventory and compliance with IEC 62443 and NIST SP 800-82 standards. (b) Describe, with a labelled block diagram, the components of an OT asset inventory system, and explain how a continuous-discovery architecture differs from a periodic-discovery one.**

#### (a) Standards Compliance Linkage (7 marks)

| Standard | Specific Clause | Asset Inventory Mandate |
|---|---|---|
| **IEC 62443-3-3** | SR 1.1 — Identification | Every device must have a unique identity and be inventoried. |
| **IEC 62443-3-3** | SR 1.5 — Authenticator management | Inventory provides the basis for issuing and revoking credentials. |
| **IEC 62443-3-3** | SR 2.1 — Authorization enforcement | RBAC requires the inventory to know *who owns* each asset. |
| **IEC 62443-3-3** | SR 3.4 — Software & info integrity | Inventory tracks software/firmware versions for integrity verification. |
| **IEC 62443-4-2** | Component requirements | Each component's security capabilities are tied to its inventory record. |
| **NIST SP 800-82r3** | Section 6.2 — Inventory | "Organisations should develop and maintain an accurate, up-to-date inventory of OT assets." |
| **NIST CSF 2.0** | Function ID.AM (Asset Management) | The *Identify* function explicitly requires asset inventory. |

**Key Explanation (4 marks):** The inventory is the *evidence artefact* for auditors. Without it, no compliance certification can be issued. Auditors sample asset records against physical devices; any unrecorded device is an automatic finding. In OT, where change is constant (sensor replacements, firmware upgrades), the inventory must be **continuously maintained**, not just a quarterly spreadsheet.

**Penalty Reference (3 marks):** Under NERC CIP-007-6, a missing asset record can attract penalties up to **\$1 million/day/violation** in the U.S. grid. In India, the CEA guidelines invoke similar punitive clauses for designated Critical Information Infrastructure (CII).

#### (b) System Components & Continuous vs Periodic Discovery (7 marks)

**Components of an OT Asset Inventory System (3.5 marks):**

1. **Discovery Engine** — passive SPAN taps, active probes, or agent-based collectors
2. **Fingerprinting Module** — parses protocols (Modbus, DNP3, Profinet, EtherNet/IP)
3. **Classification Engine** — assigns role, criticality, owner
4. **Storage Layer** — relational DB or time-series store (the *golden record*)
5. **Enrichment APIs** — CVE feeds, EOL/EOSL feeds, CMDB sync
6. **Visualisation Layer** — topology maps, dashboards
7. **Integration Bus** — feeds SIEM, SOAR, GRC, CMMS

**Continuous vs Periodic Discovery (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Periodic Discovery | Continuous Discovery |
|---|---|---|
| **Cadence** | Weekly / monthly | Real-time, 24×7 |
| **ACR achievable** | 70% – 90% | **95% – 99%** |
| **Window of exposure** | Hours to days | Seconds to minutes |
| **Staleness (S)** | $S > 24$ h | $S \le 24$ h |
| **Operational risk** | High — blind spots during gaps | Low — always observable |
| **Cost** | Lower upfront | Higher (compute + storage) |
| **Recommended for** | Air-gapped legacy plants | Modern converged IT/OT plants |

#### Distribution of Marks (Internal Examiner Key)

| Sub-part | Marks Awarded For |
|---|---|
| (a) Standard-by-standard mapping | 3.0 |
| (a) Auditor evidence + penalty | 4.0 |
| (b) Seven components listed | 3.5 |
| (b) Continuous vs periodic table | 3.5 |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in formulas** — e.g., writing "ACR = 96.08" instead of "ACR = 96.08%". The unit carries **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Conflating IT and OT asset inventory** — OT inventory must include *physical-safety* attributes (e.g., Safety Integrity Level), which IT inventory does not. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this.
> 3. **Skipping the interpretation step** — A computation without a compliance verdict (pass/fail) is treated as an *incomplete* answer and loses 1.5 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the dashed-vs-solid arrow distinction** in the architecture diagram — examiners explicitly look for this. Failure to explain it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Not citing the specific standard clause** (e.g., "IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1") — generic references like "as per IEC standards" are penalised. Always cite the **clause number**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Asset inventory is the foundation of OT cybersecurity** — without it, no other control can be reliably implemented.
- The three pillars are: **Discovery → Classification → Continuous Refresh**.
- The **Asset Coverage Ratio (ACR)** must be $\ge$ **98%** to satisfy IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1.
- The **Unmanaged Asset Ratio (UAR)** should be **< 2%** for best practice.
- The **Staleness Window (S)** must remain **≤ 24 hours** — anything older risks compliance failure.
- The **Attack Surface Index (ASI)** is the sum of exposure × ports × CVEs across all assets.
- An inventory is **not a one-time project** — it is a *living operational database* requiring automated, continuous updates.
- **Convergence of IT and OT inventory** is the modern trend — both domains should feed a unified CMDB.
- **Stuxnet (2010)**, **TRITON (2017)**, and **Industroyer (2016)** all exploited unknown or poorly inventoried OT assets.
- The four downstream consumers of an OT inventory are: **SIEM, SOAR, GRC, CMMS**.
- Always cite **IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1** and **NIST SP 800-82r3** in exam answers — these are the examiner's gold-standard references.
- **Continuous discovery** beats periodic discovery in modern plants — it raises ACR by 10–20 percentage points.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools and technologies for OT asset identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tools and technologies for OT asset identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**OT Asset Identification** is the systematic, continuous, and automated process of discovering, fingerprinting, classifying, and inventorying all hardware, software, firmware, and communication components residing within an Operational Technology (OT) / Industrial Control System (ICS) environment. It forms the foundational pillar of the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF 2.0) – Identify (ID)** function and is a prerequisite for threat prevention, vulnerability management, and risk assessment in industrial environments.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **IEC 62443-3-3** and **ISA/IEC 62443**, asset identification is defined as:
> The act of determining the **identity, location, configuration, state, and ownership** of each device, system, and application that processes, stores, or transmits information within the industrial automation and control systems (IACS) network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Asset identification is the *first step* in the OT threat prevention lifecycle. Without a complete and accurate asset inventory, no subsequent security control (firewall rule, patch management, anomaly detection) can be reliably applied.

### 1.2 Key Performance Metrics in OT Asset Identification

- **Asset Discovery Coverage (ADC):** The percentage of total OT assets successfully identified out of the total expected asset population. Target industry benchmark: **> 95%**.
- **Mean Time to Identify (MTTI):** Average time taken to detect and catalog a newly connected OT device. Industry target: **< 1 hour** for critical infrastructure.
- **Asset Classification Accuracy (ACA):** Percentage of correctly categorized assets (e.g., PLC vs RTU vs HMI). Target: **> 98%**.
- **Fingerprinting Fidelity:** The granularity of identifying device make, model, firmware version, and patch level. Expressed as a percentage of devices fully profiled.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **large hospital** with thousands of medical devices — MRI machines, ventilators, infusion pumps, pacemakers, patient monitors, networked computers, and HVAC controllers. Some devices are old, some are new, some are critical to life support, and some are plug-and-play additions made by staff without IT knowing.

Now, suppose the hospital wants to protect itself from cyber-attacks (e.g., ransomware that reprograms pacemakers). Before any protection is possible, the hospital must first answer:

1. **What** devices do we have? (Inventory)
2. **Where** are they located? (Topology)
3. **Who** is responsible for each? (Ownership)
4. **How** are they connected and communicating? (Network mapping)
5. **What** software/firmware are they running? (Fingerprinting)

This is exactly what **OT asset identification** does for a power plant, oil refinery, water treatment facility, or manufacturing floor. The hospital is your **plant network**, the medical devices are your **PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers, HMIs, and smart sensors**, and the ransomware is a **nation-state OT malware like TRITON, Industroyer, or Stuxnet**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is OT asset identification harder than IT?**
> In IT, devices are standardized (Windows, Linux, macOS) and active scanning is safe. In OT, devices run proprietary real-time operating systems (VxWorks, QNX, RTOS), use industrial protocols (Modbus, DNP3, Profinet), cannot tolerate network latency, and often have 15–30 year operational lifecycles. Hence, **passive, non-intrusive** identification is preferred.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Methodology – The Five-Phase Pipeline

OT asset identification is not a single tool but a **layered process pipeline**. The following are the standard phases adopted across industry and aligned with KTU's outcome-based learning for PECCT634:

**Phase 1 — Network Visibility Establishment (Passive Capture)**
- Deploy network TAPs (Test Access Points) or configure SPAN/mirror ports on industrial switches.
- Capture all traffic flowing across Purdue Levels 0–3 (Process, Basic Control, Supervisory, Site Operations).
- Zero packets are injected into the OT network → safe for legacy devices.

**Phase 2 — Protocol Parsing and Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)**
- Industrial protocols lack encryption (in most legacy systems).
- DPI engines parse the application-layer payload to extract:
  - Function codes (Modbus FC 03, FC 16, etc.)
  - Slave/Master IDs
  - Register addresses
  - Device-specific vendor codes
- Common DPI-aware protocols: **Modbus TCP, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, IEC 61850 (MMS, GOOSE, SV), PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, BACnet, OPC UA, Siemens S7Comm, Omron FINS, Mitsubishi MELSEC**.

**Phase 3 — Device Fingerprinting**
- Extract identifying attributes: MAC address (OUI prefix), IP, hostnames (DHCP/HTTP banners), serial numbers (PROFINET DCP, LLDP TLVs), firmware strings.
- Compare against a vendor-device fingerprint database (e.g., Claroty Continuous Threat Detection database, Nozomi's Guardian, Dragos's WorldView).

**Phase 4 — Asset Classification and Risk Tagging**
- Each asset is mapped to:
  - Purdue Level
  - Functional role (PLC, RTU, HMI, Engineering Workstation, Historian, IED, RTU, Sensor, Actuator)
  - Criticality (Crown Jewel vs Auxiliary)
  - Communication dependencies

**Phase 5 — Continuous Monitoring and CMDB Synchronization**
- New devices are auto-detected (rogue device alert).
- Inventory is pushed to a **Configuration Management Database (CMDB)** integrated with **SIEM** and **SOAR** platforms.

### 2.2 Three Identification Methodologies

| Methodology | Mechanism | OT Safety | KTU Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Active Scanning** | Sends ICMP, SNMP, TCP SYN, or proprietary probe packets to elicit responses | **Low** — can crash legacy PLCs | Generally avoided in Level 0–2 |
| **Passive Monitoring** | Taps traffic; learns from observed communications | **High** — non-intrusive | Preferred for production OT |
| **Agent-Based** | Software agent installed on endpoint reports metadata | Medium — requires device access | Used on Windows-based HMIs/Engineering Workstations |
| **Agentless / Agent-Free** | Queries management interfaces (SSH, SNMP v3, WMI, ICS protocol) | Medium — read-only | Used on managed switches, Windows HMI |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formulas for OT Asset Identification Metrics**

$$
\text{Asset Discovery Coverage (ADC)} = \frac{N_{\text{identified}}}{N_{\text{expected total}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Mean Time to Identify (MTTI)} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_{\text{detect}_i} - T_{\text{connect}_i})}{n}
$$

$$
\text{Asset Classification Accuracy (ACA)} = \frac{N_{\text{correctly classified}}}{N_{\text{total identified}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Fingerprint Completeness (FC)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{A_{j,\text{known}}}{A_{j,\text{total}}}
$$

$$
\text{Rogue Device Ratio (RDR)} = \frac{N_{\text{unauthorized}}}{N_{\text{total discovered}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Observation Confidence Score (OCS)} = w_1 S_{\text{protocol}} + w_2 S_{\text{vendor}} + w_3 S_{\text{behavior}} + w_4 S_{\text{topology}}
$$

where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ and $S \in [0,1]$.

**Core Industrial Protocol Identifier Table**

| Protocol | Default Port | Purdue Level | Vendor / Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modbus TCP | **502** | L1–L2 | Schneider, generic PLCs |
| DNP3 | **20000** | L1–L2 | Utilities, SCADA |
| IEC 60870-5-104 | **2404** | L2 | European utilities |
| IEC 61850 MMS | **102** | L2 | Substations, IEDs |
| EtherNet/IP | **44818**, **2222** | L1–L2 | Rockwell Allen-Bradley |
| PROFINET | **34964**, **49152** | L1 | Siemens |
| BACnet | **47808** | L3 | Building automation |
| OPC UA | **4840** | L2–L3 | Cross-vendor unification |
| Siemens S7 | **102** | L2 | Siemens S7 PLCs |
| HART-IP | **5094** | L0–L1 | Process instrumentation |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Grids:** Identification of all IEDs in substations for **NERC-CIP** compliance reporting.
- **Oil & Gas:** Mapping of RTUs along pipelines to prevent attacks like **TRITON** (targeted Triconex SIS).
- **Smart Manufacturing:** Detecting unauthorized IIoT devices on the factory floor (Industry 4.0).
- **Water Treatment:** Identifying legacy SCADA RTUs vulnerable to **Industroyer/CrashOverride**.
- **Building Management:** Discovering BACnet-controllable HVAC systems to prevent unauthorized access.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

### 3.1 Derivation: Computing Asset Discovery Coverage (ADC)

The **Asset Discovery Coverage (ADC)** is the most cited KTU-level metric. Below is a complete analytical derivation with each step logically justified.

**Step 1 — Define the Population Set**
Let the expected (ground truth) OT asset population be the set $E$ containing $N_E$ devices:
$$
E = \left\{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_{N_E}\right\}
$$
These are devices known (or expected) to exist based on engineering documentation, network diagrams, and P&IDs (Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams).

**Step 2 — Define the Discovered Set**
Let the set of devices actually discovered by the OT asset identification tool be the set $D$:
$$
D = \left\{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_{N_D}\right\}
$$
This set is obtained from passive DPI, active scan results, and agent-based reporting.

**Step 3 — Establish Cardinality**
The number of unique devices in $D$ that *actually exist* in $E$ is given by the intersection:
$$
N_{\text{identified}} = \vert E \cap D \vert
$$
The total expected population is:
$$
N_{\text{expected total}} = \vert E \vert = N_E
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Coverage Formula**
By definition, ADC is the ratio of correctly identified devices to the expected total:
$$
\text{ADC} = \frac{N_{\text{identified}}}{N_{\text{expected total}}} = \frac{\vert E \cap D \vert}{\vert E \vert}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Percentage (Industry Reporting Standard)**
For KTU and industry reporting, ADC is expressed as a percentage:
$$
\text{ADC \%} = \frac{\vert E \cap D \vert}{\vert E \vert} \times 100\%
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
A power substation has 200 expected IEDs (per substation engineering document). An OT asset identification tool (e.g., Nozomi Guardian) is deployed for 30 days. It discovers 192 devices, of which 188 are confirmed to be legitimate IEDs and 4 are rogue maintenance laptops.

- $N_E = 200$
- $N_{\text{identified}} = 188$
- Rogue devices $= 4$ (must be reported separately)
- Missing devices $= 200 - 188 = 12$ (gap to investigate)

$$
\text{ADC \%} = \frac{188}{200} \times 100\% = 94\%
$$

$$
\text{RDR \%} = \frac{4}{192} \times 100\% \approx 2.08\%
$$

**Conclusion:** ADC of 94% falls just below the recommended 95% target. The 12 missing devices must be investigated — they may be offline, behind a non-mirrored switch, or require a separate DPI sensor.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Tool vs. Technology vs. Protocol Support

The following analytical matrix is a KTU-exam-ready reference summarizing the **leading commercial and open-source tools** used in OT asset identification.

| Tool / Platform | Vendor / Open-Source | Identification Type | Key OT Protocols Supported | KTU Exam Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Claroty Continuous Threat Detection (CTD)** | Claroty | Passive | Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, BACnet | Gold standard for OT visibility |
| **Nozomi Networks Guardian** | Nozomi | Passive + Active (safe) | 100+ industrial protocols | Strong in utilities |
| **Dragos Platform / WorldView** | Dragos | Passive | DNP3, Modbus, IEC 60870, GE SRTP, EthIP | Threat-focused asset intel |
| **Tenable.ot (formerly Indegy)** | Tenable | Passive + Agent | Modbus, Siemens S7, DNP3, IEC 61850 | Strong passive DPI |
| **Microsoft Defender for IoT (formerly CyberX)** | Microsoft | Passive | 30+ ICS protocols | Azure-integrated |
| **Tripwire Enterprise** | Tripwire (Fortra) | Agent-based | IT-centric, partial OT | CMDB integrity focus |
| **Forescout (SilentDefense)** | Forescout | Passive | Modbus, DNP3, BACnet, IEC 61850 | Strong device profiling |
| **Armis** | Armis | Passive + Agentless | Mixed IT/OT/IoT | Strong in healthcare OT |
| **Wireshark + ICS Protocol Dissectors** | Open-source | Passive manual | All major ICS protocols | Forensic/educational use |
| **Nmap + ICS Scripts (e.g., modbus-discover.nse)** | Open-source | Active | Modbus, S7, BACnet | Must use with extreme caution |
| **GRASSMARLIN** | NSA (open-source) | Passive | Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870, EtherNet/IP | Free, government-grade |
| **Industrial Defender ASM** | Industrial Defender | Passive + Agent | Broad protocol coverage | Asset + config management |
| **OASyS DNA, ABB 800xA Asset Monitor** | Vendor-specific | Passive | Vendor-specific | Greenfield deployment |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For 14-mark questions, mentioning **at least 4 tools with their identification mechanism, protocol coverage, and a use-case scenario** typically scores full marks.

### 3.3 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Identification Tool

The choice of tool depends on:

1. **OT Environment Type:** Process (oil & gas) vs Discrete (manufacturing) vs Hybrid.
2. **Criticality:** Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) require the most passive approach.
3. **Compliance Mandate:** NERC-CIP, IEC 62443, NIST CSF.
4. **Network Architecture:** Air-gapped vs Purdue-aligned vs flat.
5. **Budget:** Commercial (Claroty, Nozomi) vs Open-source (GRASSMARLIN, Wireshark).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — OT Asset Identification Stack

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an OT asset identification system, from raw data ingestion to inventory output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[OT Network TAP or SPAN Port] --> B1[Packet Capture Engine]
    A2[Active Probe Module Restricted to DMZ] --> B1
    A3[Agent Telemetry from HMI and EWS] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Protocol Parser and Decoder]
    C1 --> C2[Modbus and DNP3 Parser]
    C1 --> C3[IEC 61850 PROFINET EtherNetIP Parser]
    C1 --> C4[OPC UA and BACnet Parser]
    C2 --> D1[Device Fingerprint Database]
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Asset Classification Engine]
    E1 --> F1[Inventory and CMDB Sync]
    E1 --> F2[Anomaly and Rogue Device Alert]
    E1 --> F3[Compliance Report Generator NERC CIP and IEC 62443]
    B1 --> G1[Threat Intel Correlation Layer]
    G1 --> F2
```

**Explanation of the Architecture:**

- **Layer 1 — Data Ingestion (A1, A2, A3):** Three parallel data sources — passive network taps, restricted active probes, and agent telemetry. Each input is read-only or controlled.
- **Layer 2 — Packet Capture and Normalization (B1):** Standardizes raw bytes into a normalized packet stream.
- **Layer 3 — Protocol Parsing (C1 → C2, C3, C4):** Family-specific protocol dissectors. Each parser extracts protocol-level identifiers.
- **Layer 4 — Fingerprinting (D1):** Cross-references parsed fields with a fingerprint database to derive vendor, model, firmware, and serial.
- **Layer 5 — Classification (E1):** Tags each asset with Purdue Level, functional role, criticality, and ownership.
- **Layer 6 — Outputs (F1, F2, F3):** The three consumer channels — inventory sync, security alerting, and compliance reporting.
- **Layer 7 — Threat Correlation (G1):** An optional parallel layer that joins asset identities with known CVE and threat intelligence feeds.

### 4.2 Workflow Diagram — Passive Asset Discovery Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Deploy TAP on OT Switch] --> S2[Step 2: Begin 24x7 Passive Capture]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: DPI Engine Parses Industrial Protocols]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Match Fingerprint to Known Device Database]
    S4 --> S5{Step 5: Device Already Known?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Update Last Seen Timestamp and Baseline]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Step 6: Create New Asset Record]
    S6 --> S8[Step 7: Push to CMDB and SIEM]
    S7 --> S9[Step 8: Trigger Rogue Device Alert if Unauthorized]
    S9 --> S8
    S8 --> S10[Step 9: Generate Compliance Snapshot]
```

**Operational Notes:**

- Steps 1–4 are continuous and non-intrusive.
- Step 5 acts as a *de-duplication* gate.
- Steps 6–7 handle the *first-seen* lifecycle.
- Steps 8–9 close the loop with security operations and audit teams.

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Active vs Passive Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ACTIVE[Active Identification]
        A1[Scanner Sends Probe Packet] --> A2[Device Responds with Banner]
        A2 --> A3[Tool Extracts Identity]
        A3 --> A4[Risk: Probe May Disrupt Legacy Device]
    end
    subgraph PASSIVE[Passive Identification]
        P1[Tool Observes Existing Traffic] --> P2[Parser Decodes Native Protocol]
        P2 --> P3[Fingerprint Matched]
        P3 --> P4[Zero Disruption to Live Process]
    end
```

**Key Takeaway:** Active scanning injects traffic and is generally **not permitted** in operational Level 0–1 networks. Passive identification is the de-facto KTU-recommended and industry-preferred approach.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]
*Define OT asset identification. List any four industrial protocols commonly observed during passive OT asset discovery.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

OT asset identification is the systematic process of discovering, fingerprinting, classifying, and inventorying all hardware, software, and communication components within an Industrial Automation and Control System (IACS) network, in alignment with the NIST CSF Identify function and IEC 62443-3-3 requirements.

*(1 mark for definition)*

Four industrial protocols commonly observed during passive OT asset discovery:

1. **Modbus TCP (Port 502)** — Used in Schneider Electric and generic PLC communication.
2. **DNP3 (Port 20000)** — Predominant in North American electric utility SCADA systems.
3. **IEC 61850 MMS (Port 102)** — Used in substations for Intelligent Electronic Device (IED) communication.
4. **EtherNet/IP (Port 44818)** — Deployed in Rockwell Allen-Bradley control systems.

*(1 mark for any 2 correct, full marks for 4 correct with usage context)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]
*Differentiate between active and passive asset identification. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each in an OT context.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Active Identification | Passive Identification |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Sends probe packets and waits for responses | Taps and observes existing traffic |
| OT Safety | Risky for legacy devices | Completely non-intrusive |
| Coverage Depth | Faster but partial | Slower but comprehensive |
| Best Use | IT networks, DMZ, lab environments | Production OT Levels 0–2 |

*(2 marks for differentiation table)*

**Advantage of Active:** Faster initial mapping; can discover devices that are silent on the network.
**Advantage of Passive:** Zero risk of disrupting real-time control loops; safe for SIS environments.
**Disadvantage of Active:** May crash or reset a legacy PLC; can trigger process upset.
**Disadvantage of Passive:** Cannot discover devices that never communicate; requires longer observation window.

*(1 mark for paired advantage + disadvantage)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the five-phase pipeline of OT asset identification in detail. For each phase, state the corresponding tool category and the output artifact produced. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the architecture and working of a **passive Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** engine for Modbus TCP. Explain how vendor and firmware identification is derived from a captured Modbus packet. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The five-phase pipeline of OT asset identification:

**Phase 1 — Network Visibility (1 Mark)**
- Tool Category: Hardware TAPs (copper/fiber), SPAN port configuration on industrial switches, or aggregators.
- Output Artifact: Raw packet capture (PCAP) files stored on a sensor appliance.

**Phase 2 — Protocol Parsing and DPI (1.5 Marks)**
- Tool Category: Commercial DPI engines (Claroty CTD, Nozomi Guardian, Dragos) or open-source dissectors (Wireshark with ICS plugins, GRASSMARLIN).
- Output Artifact: Decoded protocol transactions, function codes, register maps, and device-specific metadata.

**Phase 3 — Device Fingerprinting (1.5 Marks)**
- Tool Category: Vendor fingerprint databases (e.g., Claroty Edge, Dragos WorldView) combined with OUI lookups, LLDP TLV parsing, and DHCP fingerprinting.
- Output Artifact: Identity tuple — `{Vendor, Model, Firmware, Serial, MAC, IP}`.

**Phase 4 — Asset Classification and Risk Tagging (1.5 Marks)**
- Tool Category: Asset management modules that apply IEC 62443 zone & conduit modeling and Purdue Level classification.
- Output Artifact: A classified asset record with criticality score, zone assignment, and ownership.

**Phase 5 — Continuous Monitoring and CMDB Sync (1.5 Marks)**
- Tool Category: Integration layer using APIs to push data into ServiceNow CMDB, Splunk, Azure Sentinel, or IBM QRadar.
- Output Artifact: Live inventory, rogue device alerts, and compliance reports (NERC-CIP, IEC 62443).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

A Modbus TCP DPI engine operates by tapping a network segment where a Master (e.g., SCADA HMI) communicates with Slave devices (PLCs, RTUs). The DPI engine performs the following steps:

**Step 1 — TCP Stream Reconstruction (1 Mark)**
The DPI engine reassembles TCP sessions between Master and Slave on port 502. Each Modbus TCP frame consists of:
- **MBAP Header (7 bytes):** Transaction ID, Protocol ID (=0x0000 for Modbus), Length, Unit ID.
- **PDU Payload (variable):** Function Code (1 byte) + Data (N bytes).

**Step 2 — Function Code Extraction (1.5 Marks)**
The DPI engine decodes the function code:
- FC 01/02: Read Coils / Discrete Inputs
- FC 03/04: Read Holding / Input Registers
- FC 05/06: Write Single Coil / Register
- FC 15/16: Write Multiple Coils / Registers
- FC 43/14: Read Device Identification (encapsulated transport, used for vendor ID retrieval)

**Step 3 — Vendor and Firmware Identification via FC 43/14 (2.5 Marks)**
The most powerful fingerprinting vector in Modbus is the **Read Device Identification (FC 43/14)** request. The slave responds with objects in a specific stream of Read Device Identification codes:
- Object 0x00: VendorName (e.g., "Schneider Electric")
- Object 0x01: ProductCode (e.g., "BMXP342020")
- Object 0x02: MajorMinorRevision (firmware version, e.g., "V3.10")
- Object 0x03: VendorUrl
- Object 0x04: ProductName
- Object 0x05: ModelName
- Object 0x06: UserApplicationName

The DPI engine queries each Modbus slave using FC 43/14 and stores the response. This yields an **unambiguous** device identity.

**Step 4 — Behavioral Fingerprinting (1 Mark)**
Even when FC 43/14 is blocked, the DPI engine can derive a probable identity from:
- **OUI prefix** of the MAC address (first 3 bytes identify the vendor).
- **Response timing patterns** (e.g., specific PLCs have characteristic timing).
- **Register read patterns** (the Master periodically polls specific register ranges characteristic of a vendor's PLC).

**Final Output:** A rich asset record per device:
- `Vendor: Schneider Electric`
- `Model: BMX P34 2020`
- `Firmware: V3.10`
- `Purdue Level: 1`
- `Function: PLC (Process Logic Controller)`
- `Criticality: High`

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Part b):**
> Many students wrongly assume that Modbus DPI works by inspecting the IP/MAC layer. **Marks are deducted** if you do not explicitly mention the **MBAP header**, **Function Code 43/14 (Read Device Identification)**, and the **Object IDs 0x00–0x06** that carry the vendor, model, and firmware fields. Always reference the **Modbus Application Protocol Specification V1.1b3** when explaining this.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Compare and contrast **passive, active, and agent-based** OT asset identification methodologies. For each, give a real-world tool example and one scenario where it is most appropriate. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **rogue device detection** in an OT network. Describe how a passive DPI engine identifies a new unauthorized device and the immediate security actions triggered. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Feature | Passive | Active | Agent-Based |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Listens to existing traffic | Sends probes/queries | Software installed on endpoint reports metadata |
| Disruption Risk | **None** | **High** for legacy OT | **Medium** (CPU/bandwidth) |
| Network Coverage | All devices communicating | Only devices that respond | Only devices with the agent |
| Setup Complexity | Medium (needs TAP) | Low | High (per-device install) |
| Real-time Updates | Continuous | Periodic | Continuous |
| Example Tool | Claroty CTD, Nozomi Guardian | Nmap with modbus-discover.nse | Tripwire Enterprise, Tenable Nessus Agent |
| Best Scenario | Live production plant with mixed-vendor PLCs | Lab staging network, IT-DMZ OT enclave | Windows-based HMIs and Engineering Workstations |

*(4 marks for table; 1.5 marks for tool examples; 1.5 marks for scenario justification)*

**Most Appropriate Scenarios:**

1. **Passive:** A 24/7 operating oil refinery with mixed Siemens and Honeywell controllers, where any injected packet could trigger a process upset. Use Claroty CTD.
2. **Active:** An isolated engineering lab where new PLCs are being commissioned and validated. Use Nmap.
3. **Agent-Based:** A Windows-based HMI fleet in a control room where authorized operators want continuous configuration monitoring. Use Tripwire.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A rogue device is any device connected to the OT network that has not been authorized, baselined, or recorded in the official asset inventory. It may be an attacker's implant, an unapproved contractor laptop, or a misconfigured IIoT sensor.

**Detection Mechanism by Passive DPI (3 Marks):**

The passive DPI engine maintains a **baseline inventory database** containing the known-good set of devices. When a new packet stream appears that does not match any known fingerprint:

1. **MAC Address Novelty Check:** The new source MAC does not match any OUI in the trusted vendor list, or it does not match any previously learned device.
2. **Protocol Anomaly Check:** The device begins communicating using a protocol that is not part of the zone's expected traffic matrix.
3. **Timing and Behavior Check:** The device polls registers at intervals inconsistent with the baselined SCADA polling pattern.
4. **Cross-Reference with CMDB:** The device's IP is not present in the authoritative CMDB.

**Immediate Security Actions (3 Marks):**

1. **Alert Generation:** SIEM (Splunk/Sentinel/QRadar) receives a high-severity alert with device fingerprint, switch port (via SNMP/LLDP correlation), and physical switch ID.
2. **Network Isolation:** A Software-Defined Networking (SDN) controller or industrial firewall (e.g., Cisco Cyber Vision, Fortinet) receives an API call to **quarantine the switch port** using 802.1X or port-security ACLs.
3. **Forensic Capture:** Full packet capture of the rogue device's traffic is preserved for incident response.
4. **Operator Notification:** The SOC analyst receives a push notification with one-click access to the rogue device's traffic baseline and recommended playbook (e.g., NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response).
5. **Compliance Logging:** The event is logged for **NERC-CIP-008** (Incident Reporting) and **IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.5** (Access Management) audit trails.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Part b):**
> Students often stop at "the tool sends an alert" and lose 2–3 marks. The 14-mark KTU question demands that you specify **what system** receives the alert, **what automated action** is taken (e.g., port quarantine via 802.1X), and **what compliance clause** is invoked. Always close with a compliance reference (NERC-CIP, IEC 62443 SR, NIST CSF).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Definition:** OT asset identification = discover + fingerprint + classify + inventory, the *first* step in OT threat prevention.
- **Frameworks:** NIST CSF (Identify), IEC 62443, NERC-CIP, ISA-95 / Purdue Model.
- **Passive vs Active:** Passive = safe, OT-preferred. Active = risky, avoid in Levels 0–2.
- **DPI** parses industrial protocols (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA) at the application layer to extract device identity.
- **Modbus FC 43/14** is the gold-standard Modbus fingerprinting function (Read Device Identification).
- **Asset Discovery Coverage (ADC)** = $\vert E \cap D \vert / \vert E \vert \times 100\%$, target **> 95%**.
- **Mean Time to Identify (MTTI)** is a key SOC metric.
- **Rogue Device Detection** triggers SIEM alert + port quarantine (802.1X) + forensic PCAP.
- **Top Tools (commercial):** Claroty CTD, Nozomi Guardian, Dragos Platform, Tenable.ot, Microsoft Defender for IoT.
- **Top Tools (open-source):** Wireshark, GRASSMARLIN, Nmap + ICS scripts.
- **Purdue Model Mapping:** Level 0 (Process), Level 1 (Basic Control — PLCs/RTUs), Level 2 (Supervisory — SCADA/HMI), Level 3 (Site Operations), Level 3.5 (DMZ), Level 4–5 (Enterprise/Internet).
- **OT Protocol Ports to Memorize:** Modbus 502, DNP3 20000, IEC 61850 102, EtherNet/IP 44818, PROFINET 34964, BACnet 47808, OPC UA 4840.
- **Real-World Attacks Tied to Asset ID Failure:** **Stuxnet** (Siemens S7 PLCs), **TRITON** (Triconex SIS), **Industroyer** (IEC 60870-5-104), **VPNFilter** (Modbus/DNP3 aware).
- **Agentless tools** query management interfaces; **agent-based** tools have software installed; **passive** tools only observe.
- **Observation Confidence Score (OCS)** combines protocol signature confidence, vendor matching, behavioral baseline, and topology correlation.
- **CMDB Integration** is the final delivery channel — without it, identification has no operational impact.
- **Compliance Anchors:** NERC-CIP-007 (System Security Management), IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1 (Identification), NIST CSF 2.0 ID.AM (Asset Management).

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Tools and Technologies for OT Asset Identification**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Criticality Classification: Criteria for classifying OT assets based on criticality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Criticality Classification: Criteria for classifying OT assets based on criticality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Criticality Classification** in Operational Technology (OT) is the formal, structured process of categorizing physical and cyber-physical assets (PLCs, RTUs, SCADA servers, HMI stations, historians, smart sensors, actuators, controllers, network switches, safety instrumented systems) based on the **severity of consequences** that would result from their compromise, failure, manipulation, or unavailability.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Box:**
> Criticality Classification is a *risk-prioritization primitive* — it assigns a **Criticality Tier (T1–T4)** to every asset in the OT/ICS estate so that security controls, monitoring budgets, patching frequency, and incident-response escalation paths can be allocated **proportionally to consequence severity** rather than uniformly.
