In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, criticality is **multidimensional**: it is *not* merely a function of asset cost, but a vector combination of **Safety (S), Environment (E), Operations (O), Financial (F), Regulatory (R), and Information (I)** impact dimensions.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Triage Analogy"

Imagine a hospital emergency room. When multiple patients arrive simultaneously, the triage nurse does **not** treat them in arrival order. Instead, each patient is classified by *how critical* their condition is:

| Triage Class | Patient Status | Response |
|--------------|----------------|----------|
| **Red (T1)** | Heart attack, major trauma | Immediate, highest doctor, ICU |
| **Yellow (T2)** | Serious fracture, burns | Urgent, monitored bed |
| **Green (T3)** | Sprain, minor cuts | Standard queue |
| **Black (T4)** | Already deceased, non-recoverable | Lowest priority for ICU |

**Now map this to an OT plant:** An Emergency Shutdown System (ESD/SIS) is the **"Red"** patient — a cyber-incident here can kill people. A corporate billing server is the **"Black"** patient — failure is annoying but causes no physical harm. Criticality Classification is the **triage nurse** that decides which OT asset gets the **tightest cybersecurity controls, the most monitoring, and the fastest patching**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Without criticality classification, an organization either (a) over-protects trivial assets and starves critical ones, or (b) under-protects critical assets and accepts catastrophic tail-risk. Both are unacceptable in OT.

---

### 1.3 The Six Consequence Dimensions (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

Every OT asset is evaluated against these **six canonical impact dimensions**, which together form the "criticality vector":

1. **Safety (S)** — Potential to cause fatality, injury, or occupational illness to humans (workers, public, contractors).
2. **Environmental (E)** — Potential to cause release of hazardous materials, ecological damage, regulatory environmental fines.
3. **Operational (O)** — Potential to disrupt production, degrade product quality, halt a critical process, or cause cascading failures.
4. **Financial (F)** — Direct monetary loss (equipment replacement, production hours lost, recovery cost) + indirect loss (contract penalties).
5. **Regulatory/Compliance (R)** — Potential to trigger violation of statutory/regulatory obligations (e.g., NERC-CIP, IEC 62443, NIS2, NIST CSF, OSHA).
6. **Information/Reputation (I)** — Potential to leak sensitive IP, trade secrets, customer data, or trigger brand/credibility damage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants / Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair (hours)
> - **RTO** = Recovery Time Objective (hours)
> - **RPO** = Recovery Point Objective (hours of data loss tolerated)
> - **SL-T** = Security Level Target (IEC 62443, scale 1–4)

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Phase Classification Pipeline

Criticality classification is **not a one-time spreadsheet exercise**. It is a **cyclic, governed process** that maps to the broader OT Risk Management lifecycle. KTU expects students to articulate the three-phase pipeline:

**Phase 1 — Asset Inventory & Contextualization**
- Build a complete, authoritative **OT Asset Inventory** (Hardware, Software, Firmware, Network, Protocol, Ownership, Location, Vendor).
- Map each asset to the **physical process** it controls and the **business function** it supports.
- Identify upstream/downstream **interdependencies** (an asset may be Tier 1 only because something *else* depends on it).

**Phase 2 — Consequence-Driven Scoring**
- For each asset, score every dimension (S, E, O, F, R, I) on a normalized scale (typically **1 = Negligible, 5 = Catastrophic**).
- Apply **domain-aware weighting** (e.g., in a refinery, Safety weight > Information weight).
- Compute the **Composite Criticality Score (CCS)**.

**Phase 3 — Tier Assignment & Governance**
- Bucket the CCS into **four Tiers** (T1 / T2 / T3 / T4).
- Apply **defense-in-depth controls** proportional to tier (network segmentation, MFA, patching cadence, SOC monitoring, EDR, jump hosts).
- Review annually or after any major change (process, vendor, regulation).

---

### 2.2 The Six Hard Criteria (KTU High-Yield — Memorize These)

| # | Criterion | What is Evaluated | Why It Matters in OT |
|---|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **C1** | **Safety Impact** | Likelihood × severity of harm to human life/health | OT is unique because *cyber events can cause kinetic harm* — physical injury or death. The single most important OT dimension. |
| **C2** | **Environmental Impact** | Magnitude of hazardous release, regulatory environmental damage | A manipulated valve in a chemical plant can release toxic gas. Direct link to EPA/PCB compliance. |
| **C3** | **Operational / Mission Impact** | Downtime tolerance, production volume lost, cascading process failure | Some processes (steel casting, nuclear, pharma batch) cannot simply "stop and restart" — restart cost may exceed loss of operation. |
| **C4** | **Financial Impact** | Direct (equipment, recovery) + Indirect (lost revenue, contract penalty, recall) | Financial criticality is the language of executive boards; drives budget for security controls. |
| **C5** | **Regulatory / Compliance Impact** | Violation severity of NERC-CIP, IEC 62443-3-3 SL-T, NIS2, OSHA, FDA, sectoral regulators | Non-compliance can mean license revocation — existential threat to the operator. |
| **C6** | **Information / Reputational Impact** | Sensitivity of data handled, IP, customer PII, brand impact | Lower priority in OT than IT, but still relevant for historian data, IP recipes, and brand trust. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb:** If you can defend your tier classification using **at least 3 of the 6 criteria** with **concrete examples from the process**, your answer is bullet-proof.

---

### 2.3 The Composite Criticality Score (CCS) — Mathematical Formulation

The **industry-standard** approach is a **weighted linear combination of the six dimension scores**, with weights summing to unity. This is the formula KTU expects you to write in an ESE answer.

Let us define the **Criticality Vector** for asset $A_i$ as:

$$
\vec{C}_i = (S_i,\ E_i,\ O_i,\ F_i,\ R_i,\ I_i)
$$

Each component is rated on an **integer scale 1–5** (1 = Negligible, 5 = Catastrophic). The **Composite Criticality Score (CCS)** is:

$$
\text{CCS}_i = w_S \cdot S_i + w_E \cdot E_i + w_O \cdot O_i + w_F \cdot F_i + w_R \cdot R_i + w_I \cdot I_i
$$

subject to the **weight normalization constraint**:

$$
w_S + w_E + w_O + w_F + w_R + w_I = 1, \quad w_k \geq 0\ \forall k
$$

**Tier Assignment Rule (KTU Standard Cutoffs):**

$$
\text{Tier}(A_i) = \begin{cases}
T_1\ (\text{Critical}) & \text{if } \text{CCS}_i \geq 4.0 \\
T_2\ (\text{High}) & \text{if } 3.0 \leq \text{CCS}_i < 4.0 \\
T_3\ (\text{Medium}) & \text{if } 2.0 \leq \text{CCS}_i < 3.0 \\
T_4\ (\text{Low}) & \text{if } \text{CCS}_i < 2.0
\end{cases}
$$

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Threshold Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Rule | Notation | Units / Scale | Notes |
|---|------------------|----------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{CCS}_i = \sum_{k \in \{S,E,O,F,R,I\}} w_k \cdot X_{i,k}$ | $X_{i,k}$: score of asset $i$ on dimension $k$ | Dimensionless, range $[1, 5]$ | Weighted sum |
| 2 | $\sum_{k} w_k = 1$ | Weight normalization | Dimensionless | Mandatory for comparability |
| 3 | $\text{Tier} = f(\text{CCS})$ | Tier cutoffs at **2.0 / 3.0 / 4.0** | Tier (T1–T4) | Standard KTU cutoffs |
| 4 | $\text{Risk}_i = \text{Threat}_i \times \text{Vulnerability}_i \times \text{CCS}_i$ | Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact | Dimensionless | Where CCS is the "Impact" |
| 5 | $\text{SL-T} = g(\text{Tier})$ | IEC 62443 Security Level Target | Scale **SL-T 1 to SL-T 4** | T1 → SL-T 3 or 4 |
| 6 | $\text{CVSS Base} = f(\text{AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A})$ | Common Vulnerability Scoring System | Scale **0.0 – 10.0** | Used for *vulnerability*, not *asset* |
| 7 | $\text{Defense Budget}_i \propto \text{CCS}_i$ | Budget allocation principle | Currency | Proportional spend |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This CCS framework is the **decision engine** that drives (a) which assets get jump-host-only access, (b) which PLC firmware gets patched within 7 days vs 180 days, (c) which assets are continuously monitored by the OT-SOC, and (d) which assets are segmented into **Purdue Level 0/1 vs Level 3/4 zones** (IEC 62443 zones & conduits).

---

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Criterion (Deep Reasoning)

**Why Safety First?**
OT is the *only* domain of computing where a buffer overflow can literally kill someone. The Stuxnet attack (2010) centrifuges, the Ukraine Power Grid (2015), the Colonial Pipeline (2021) shutdown, and the Oldsmar Water Plant (2021) sodium hydroxide manipulation are all real incidents where cyber-events crossed into the physical world. The Safety dimension is non-negotiable and the IEC 62443 framework mandates **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** alignment with cybersecurity SL-T.

**Why Operational, not just Financial?**
Many OT processes are **unsafe to interrupt** (a glass furnace, a nuclear reactor, an oil refinery distillation column). The "cost of stopping" may exceed the cost of running. So Operational criticality captures **process-state risk** (startup, shutdown, transient) that pure financial impact misses.

**Why Information is Last?**
Unlike IT, where confidentiality is king, OT priorities are: **Safety > Availability > Integrity > Confidentiality**. We would rather expose process data than lose the ability to control the process. Hence Information weight is typically the lowest in OT.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation: Building the Composite Criticality Score for a Real OT Asset

**Scenario (KTU-style problem):** A pharmaceutical company wants to classify **three OT assets** in its plant:

- **Asset A1:** Batch Reactor PLC (Siemens S7-1500) controlling an exothermic reaction in API manufacturing.
- **Asset A2:** HVAC Controller (Building Management System) for the cleanroom environment.
- **Asset A3:** Engineering Workstation (laptop) used to program PLCs, connected to the control network.

**Step 1 — Define the weights (domain-driven, validated by plant leadership).**

For a pharmaceutical process plant, the plant CISO and Process Safety Manager jointly agree on:

$$
w_S = 0.30,\quad w_E = 0.20,\quad w_O = 0.25,\quad w_F = 0.10,\quad w_R = 0.10,\quad w_I = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — Verify the normalization constraint.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_k &= 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.05 \\
&= 0.55 + 0.25 + 0.15 + 0.05 \\
&= 1.00 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Score each asset on the 6 dimensions (1–5 scale).**

| Asset | $S$ (Safety) | $E$ (Env) | $O$ (Op) | $F$ (Fin) | $R$ (Reg) | $I$ (Info) |
|-------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A1: Batch Reactor PLC** | 5 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 | 2 |
| **A2: HVAC Controller** | 2 | 1 | 4 | 2 | 3 | 1 |
| **A3: Engineering Laptop** | 3 | 1 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 4 |

**Reasoning behind the scores (this is what KTU examiners award marks for):**
- *A1 Safety = 5:* Exothermic reaction runaway can cause explosion.
- *A1 Op = 5:* Loss of batch = loss of entire API batch (worth ~$1M each).
- *A1 Reg = 5:* FDA cGMP + IEC 62443 SL-T 3 mandate.
- *A2 Safety = 2:* Cleanroom HVAC failure contaminates product but does not directly injure.
- *A3 Info = 4:* Holds PLC project source code (trade secret recipes).

**Step 4 — Compute CCS for Asset A1 (Batch Reactor PLC).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCS}_{A1} &= (0.30)(5) + (0.20)(4) + (0.25)(5) + (0.10)(4) + (0.10)(5) + (0.05)(2) \\
&= 1.50 + 0.80 + 1.25 + 0.40 + 0.50 + 0.10 \\
&= 4.55
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\text{CCS}_{A1} = 4.55 \geq 4.0$ → **Tier 1 (Critical)**.

**Step 5 — Compute CCS for Asset A2 (HVAC Controller).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCS}_{A2} &= (0.30)(2) + (0.20)(1) + (0.25)(4) + (0.10)(2) + (0.10)(3) + (0.05)(1) \\
&= 0.60 + 0.20 + 1.00 + 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.05 \\
&= 2.35
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $2.0 \leq \text{CCS}_{A2} = 2.35 < 3.0$ → **Tier 3 (Medium)**.

**Step 6 — Compute CCS for Asset A3 (Engineering Laptop).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCS}_{A3} &= (0.30)(3) + (0.20)(1) + (0.25)(4) + (0.10)(3) + (0.10)(3) + (0.05)(4) \\
&= 0.90 + 0.20 + 1.00 + 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.20 \\
&= 2.90
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $2.0 \leq \text{CCS}_{A3} = 2.90 < 3.0$ → **Tier 3 (Medium)**, *but* notice it is on the boundary — if the plant handled hazardous gases, $E$ would jump to 4 and the asset would leap to Tier 2. This is the **sensitivity of the model to context**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Criteria to Frameworks

| KTU Criterion | NIST CSF Function | IEC 62443 Zone Requirement | Purdue Level | Typical SL-T |
|----------------|-------------------|------------------------------|--------------|--------------|
| Safety (S) | Protect / Respond | SL-T 3 or 4 | Level 0–1 | SL-T 3–4 |
| Environment (E) | Protect | SL-T 2 to 3 | Level 0–2 | SL-T 2–3 |
| Operational (O) | Identify / Recover | SL-T 2 to 4 | Level 1–3 | SL-T 2–4 |
| Financial (F) | Govern | SL-T 1 to 2 | Level 3–4 | SL-T 1–2 |
| Regulatory (R) | Govern / Protect | SL-T 2 to 3 | All | SL-T 2–3 |
| Information (I) | Protect | SL-T 1 to 2 | Level 3–4 | SL-T 1–2 |

---

### 3.3 Decision Tree for Tier Assignment (Logical Derivation)

The tier classification can be **derived as a decision tree** rather than a single formula. This is useful when an asset scores low on most dimensions but **catastrophically on one**:

**Override Rule (Precautionary Principle):** If *any single dimension* scores $\geq 5$, the asset is **automatically elevated to Tier 1**, regardless of CCS.

$$
\text{If } \max_k(X_{i,k}) = 5, \quad \text{then } \text{Tier}(A_i) = T_1
$$

This is the **"single-failure-doctrine"** borrowed from process safety: a single catastrophic failure mode overrides aggregate scoring.

**Example:** Even if A3 (Engineering Laptop) had $S = 1, E = 1, O = 1, F = 1, R = 1, I = 5$ (so CCS = 1.70 = T4), the override rule would still classify it as **T1** because the data it holds could cause competitive collapse — but more importantly, in OT, a compromised engineering laptop is the **staging ground** for an attack on the entire plant. Hence the override is a **defense-in-depth acknowledgment** of lateral movement risk.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Criticality Classification Workflow

The following block-diagram shows the **end-to-end criticality classification pipeline**, from raw inventory to tier-assigned asset register, including the human governance gates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Step 1: Build OT Asset Inventory] --> stepB[Step 2: Map Asset to Physical Process and Business Function]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Identify Interdependencies and Dependencies]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Score Six Dimensions S E O F R I]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Apply Domain Driven Weights]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Compute Composite Criticality Score]
    stepF --> stepG{Step 7: Apply Single Failure Override}
    stepG -- Any Dimension 5 --> tierX[Tier 1 Critical Override]
    stepG -- No Override --> stepH[Step 8: Assign Tier by CCS Cutoff]
    stepH --> tier1[T1 CCS 4.0 to 5.0]
    stepH --> tier2[T2 CCS 3.0 to 4.0]
    stepH --> tier3[T3 CCS 2.0 to 3.0]
    stepH --> tier4[T4 CCS below 2.0]
    tier1 --> stepI[Step 9: Apply Proportional Controls Segmentation Patching SOC]
    tier2 --> stepI
    tier3 --> stepI
    tier4 --> stepI
    tierX --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 10: Governance Review Annual or On Change]
    stepJ --> startA
```

---

### 4.2 Asset Tier → Defense-in-Depth Control Mapping

This diagram shows **how the tier output of classification is consumed by downstream security engineering functions**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tierInput[Composite Criticality Score] --> classifier{Tier Classifier}
    classifier -- CCS 4.0 to 5.0 --> outT1[Tier 1 Critical]
    classifier -- CCS 3.0 to 4.0 --> outT2[Tier 2 High]
    classifier -- CCS 2.0 to 3.0 --> outT3[Tier 3 Medium]
    classifier -- CCS below 2.0 --> outT4[Tier 4 Low]

    outT1 --> ctrlA[Air Gap or Data Diode]
    outT1 --> ctrlB[Multi Factor Auth on Every Access]
    outT1 --> ctrlC[Patch within 7 Days]
    outT1 --> ctrlD[24x7 OT SOC Monitoring]
    outT1 --> ctrlE[SIL 3 Safety Validation]

    outT2 --> ctrlF[Zone Segmentation per IEC 62443]
    outT2 --> ctrlG[Patch within 30 Days]
    outT2 --> ctrlH[Business Hours SOC Monitoring]

    outT3 --> ctrlI[Standard VLAN Isolation]
    outT3 --> ctrlJ[Patch within 90 Days]

    outT4 --> ctrlK[Standard IT Controls Apply]
    outT4 --> ctrlL[Patch within 180 Days]
```

---

### 4.3 The Six-Dimension Consequence Wheel

The **consequence wheel** is the KTU-canonical way to depict that criticality is a *vector*, not a scalar. Each spoke is one dimension.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    centerA[OT Asset Under Evaluation]
    spokeS[S Safety 30 percent weight]
    spokeE[E Environment 20 percent weight]
    spokeO[O Operational 25 percent weight]
    spokeF[F Financial 10 percent weight]
    spokeR[R Regulatory 10 percent weight]
    spokeI[I Information 5 percent weight]
    centerA --- spokeS
    centerA --- spokeE
    centerA --- spokeO
    centerA --- spokeF
    centerA --- spokeR
    centerA --- spokeI
    spokeS --> outA[Score 1 to 5]
    spokeE --> outB[Score 1 to 5]
    spokeO --> outC[Score 1 to 5]
    spokeF --> outD[Score 1 to 5]
    spokeR --> outE[Score 1 to 5]
    spokeI --> outF[Score 1 to 5]
    outA --> calc[Weighted Sum CCS]
    outB --> calc
    outC --> calc
    outD --> calc
    outE --> calc
    outF --> calc
```

---

### 4.4 Interdependency Graph — Why Context Matters

A common KTU trap: a **temperature sensor** alone may look "Low criticality," but if it is the **only sensor** feeding a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF), its criticality jumps. This block diagram shows the cascade logic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA[Temperature Sensor T1] --> sifLogA[SIF Logic Solver]
    sensorB[Temperature Sensor T2] --> sifLogA
    sifLogA --> valveA[Final Element Emergency Shutdown Valve]
    sensorC[Pressure Sensor P1] --> sifLogA
    sensorA -.-> upsA[Single Point of Failure]
    sifLogA --> esdAct[ESD Action Prevents Vessel Overpressure]
    esdAct --> safetyOutcome[Protects Human Life and Environment]
    upsA -.->|If T1 is the only SIL 2 sensor| sensorA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A single sensor's *apparent* low CCS (1.50) is overridden by its *contextual* role as the **sole input** to a SIL-2 SIF. This is why **Step 3 (Interdependency Mapping)** is non-negotiable in the pipeline.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define Criticality Classification in the context of OT Threat Prevention. List any FOUR criteria used for classifying OT assets.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Criticality Classification is the systematic process of assigning a criticality tier (T1–T4) to each OT asset based on the **severity of consequences** (safety, operational, financial, environmental, regulatory, informational) that would arise from its compromise, failure, or unavailability. It enables **proportionate allocation** of cybersecurity controls and incident response resources.

Four criteria:
1. **Safety Impact** — potential to cause human injury or fatality.
2. **Operational Impact** — potential to disrupt production or critical process continuity.
3. **Financial Impact** — direct and indirect monetary loss from asset failure.
4. **Regulatory Impact** — potential violation of statutory/industry compliance obligations (e.g., NERC-CIP, IEC 62443, FDA cGMP).

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for any four criteria = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Differentiate between **Asset Criticality** and **Vulnerability Severity**. Why is it incorrect to use CVSS alone to prioritize OT asset protection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Dimension | Asset Criticality | Vulnerability Severity |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------------|
| **What it measures** | Consequence of *any* failure or compromise of the asset | Severity of a *specific known* software/hardware bug |
| **Time stability** | Mostly static (depends on process role) | Dynamic (changes with patches, exploits) |
| **Scoring framework** | Custom 6-dimension CCS model | CVSS (0.0 – 10.0) |
| **OT perspective** | Captures safety/operational impact | Captures only technical exploitability |

**Why CVSS alone is insufficient in OT (1 mark):**
CVSS scores an *individual CVE*, not the *consequence* of losing an asset. A **5.0 CVSS bug on a non-critical HVAC controller** is *less* important than a **7.0 CVSS bug on a Safety Instrumented System** that could trigger a SIL-2 shutdown. OT prioritization needs **Asset Criticality × Vulnerability Severity** (i.e., $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$). Using CVSS alone leads to patching low-criticality assets while critical assets remain exposed.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice, 2024 Scheme]**

> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> *(a)* Explain in detail the **six criteria** used for classifying OT assets based on criticality. For each criterion, give **one OT-specific example**. (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* A water-treatment plant has the following three OT assets to classify:
> - **W1:** Chlorine Dosing PLC (controls Cl₂ injection)
> - **W2:** Office Printer (in the admin building)
> - **W3:** SCADA HMI in the Control Room
>
> Using weights $w_S=0.35,\ w_E=0.25,\ w_O=0.20,\ w_F=0.10,\ w_R=0.05,\ w_I=0.05$ and the scores in the table below, compute the CCS for each asset and assign the correct tier. (7 marks)
>
> | Asset | S | E | O | F | R | I |
> |-------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | W1 | 5 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 4 | 1 |
> | W2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
> | W3 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 4 | 3 |

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Seven Marks:**

The six canonical criteria for OT asset criticality classification are:

**1. Safety Impact (S):** The potential of an asset's compromise to cause human injury, fatality, or occupational health harm. This is the *most important* dimension in OT because it is the only computing domain where cyber events can produce kinetic, physical harm.
*OT example:* The burner-management PLC in a power-plant boiler — manipulation could cause furnace explosion.

**2. Environmental Impact (E):** The potential for environmental damage, including release of toxic chemicals, oil spills, gas leaks, or violation of environmental regulations (EPA, Pollution Control Board norms).
*OT example:* A Tank Farm SCADA controller — manipulation could release thousands of liters of crude oil into the soil.

**3. Operational Impact (O):** The potential to disrupt production, reduce throughput, halt a process, or create a non-recoverable process state (e.g., blocked pipeline, solidified furnace charge).
*OT example:* A blast furnace cooling water pump PLC — failure halts an entire steel melt.

**4. Financial Impact (F):** Direct monetary loss (equipment replacement, recovery labor) plus indirect loss (lost revenue, contract penalties, recall costs, market-share loss).
*OT example:* A semiconductor fab's lithography controller — downtime costs ~$2M per shift.

**5. Regulatory/Compliance Impact (R):** The potential to trigger violation of statutory obligations — NERC-CIP for power, FDA 21 CFR Part 11 for pharma, TSA Pipeline Security Directives for oil/gas, IEC 62443-3-3 SL-T targets.
*OT example:* A historian storing batch records that, if tampered, violates FDA cGMP.

**6. Information/Reputational Impact (I):** Sensitivity of data handled, intellectual property, customer PII, or reputational damage potential.
*OT example:* An engineering workstation containing proprietary process recipes or customer formulations.

*(1 mark per criterion explanation + 0.16 mark per example, scaled to 7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Seven Marks:**

**Step 1: Verify weight normalization.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_k &= 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.05 + 0.05 \\
&= 1.00 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute CCS for W1 (Chlorine Dosing PLC).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCS}_{W1} &= (0.35)(5) + (0.25)(5) + (0.20)(4) + (0.10)(3) + (0.05)(4) + (0.05)(1) \\
&= 1.75 + 1.25 + 0.80 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.05 \\
&= 4.35
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $4.35 \geq 4.0$ → **W1 is Tier 1 (Critical)**.

**Step 3: Compute CCS for W2 (Office Printer).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCS}_{W2} &= (0.35)(1) + (0.25)(1) + (0.20)(1) + (0.10)(1) + (0.05)(1) + (0.05)(1) \\
&= 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.05 + 0.05 \\
&= 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $1.00 < 2.0$ → **W2 is Tier 4 (Low)**.

**Step 4: Compute CCS for W3 (SCADA HMI in Control Room).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCS}_{W3} &= (0.35)(4) + (0.25)(3) + (0.20)(5) + (0.10)(4) + (0.05)(4) + (0.05)(3) \\
&= 1.40 + 0.75 + 1.00 + 0.40 + 0.20 + 0.15 \\
&= 3.90
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $3.0 \leq 3.90 < 4.0$ → **W3 is Tier 2 (High)**.

**Step 5: Apply Single-Failure Override check.**

For W1, the maximum dimension is $S = 5$ and $E = 5$ → **Override is already satisfied**, so W1 remains Tier 1 (no change).

**Final Tier Register:**

| Asset | CCS | Tier |
|-------|-----|------|
| W1 Chlorine Dosing PLC | 4.35 | **T1 Critical** |
| W3 SCADA HMI | 3.90 | **T2 High** |
| W2 Office Printer | 1.00 | **T4 Low** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify weight normalization** ($\sum w_k = 1$). Examiners *will* deduct a mark for this.
> 2. **Rounding errors** in CCS — keep at least 2 decimal places.
> 3. **Forgetting the Single-Failure Override** — it is often a 1-mark "bonus" the examiner adds.
> 4. **Not stating the tier explicitly** — writing "4.35" without saying "Tier 1" loses the final 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice, 2024 Scheme]**

> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> *(a)* Discuss the role of **safety integrity level (SIL)** and **IEC 62443 Security Level Target (SL-T)** in OT criticality classification. How are they aligned? (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* An oil refinery wants to classify its **crude distillation unit (CDU) controller**. Using the dimensions below and equal weights for all six dimensions, compute the CCS and assign the tier. Then recommend **three** specific security controls proportional to the tier.
>
> | Dimension | S | E | O | F | R | I |
> |-----------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Score | 5 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 | 3 |

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Seven Marks:**

**Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is defined by **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** and quantifies the *probability of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) failing to perform its safety duty*. SIL is graded **SIL 1 → SIL 4**, with SIL 4 being the most stringent (target Probability of Failure on Demand, PFD, between $10^{-5}$ and $10^{-4}$).

**Security Level Target (SL-T)** is defined by **IEC 62443-3-3** and quantifies the *strength of security controls* required to defend an asset against an attacker of a given capability. SL-T is graded **SL-T 1 (Casual) → SL-T 4 (Nation-state)**.

**Alignment Table:**

| SIL | Typical SL-T | Asset Examples |
|-----|---------------|----------------|
| SIL 1 | SL-T 1 or 2 | Building fire-alarm panel |
| SIL 2 | SL-T 2 or 3 | Boiler burner management, SIF logic solver |
| SIL 3 | SL-T 3 or 4 | Emergency Shutdown (ESD) system, Fire & Gas system |
| SIL 4 | SL-T 4 | Nuclear reactor trip system |

**Why alignment matters (2 marks):** SIL determines *how dangerous* an asset's failure is; SL-T determines *how capable an attacker must be* to defeat the controls. For a SIL-3 ESD, the SL-T must be **3 or 4** because the consequence of a SIL-3 system being compromised is *catastrophic*. The frameworks are complementary — SIL is a *safety-engineering* concept, SL-T is a *cybersecurity-engineering* concept, and a well-classified OT asset has both ratings in alignment.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Seven Marks:**

**Step 1: Weights (equal weighting).**

$$
w_S = w_E = w_O = w_F = w_R = w_I = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667
$$

**Step 2: Compute CCS for the CDU Controller.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CCS}_{CDU} &= \frac{1}{6}\,(5 + 4 + 5 + 4 + 5 + 3) \\
&= \frac{1}{6}\,(26) \\
&= 4.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Tier Assignment.**

Since $4.333 \geq 4.0$ → **Tier 1 (Critical)**.

Override check: $S = 5$ and $O = 5$ both trigger the override → **confirms Tier 1**.

**Step 4: Recommend three security controls proportional to Tier 1.**

1. **Network Segmentation & Unidirectional Gateway:** Place the CDU controller inside an **IEC 62443 Security Zone** with a **data diode or unidirectional gateway** at the zone boundary. No inbound traffic allowed from corporate IT or internet.

2. **Multi-Factor Authentication & Jump Host:** All engineering access to the CDU controller must go through a **dedicated OT Jump Server** with **hardware-token MFA** and **full session video recording** (no direct engineering laptop access).

3. **Continuous OT-SOC Monitoring & 7-Day Patching SLA:** All Modbus/TCP, OPC-UA, or DNP3 traffic to/from the CDU controller must be **continuously monitored by an OT-aware SOC** (e.g., Dragos, Nozomi, Claroty). Vendor security patches must be evaluated and deployed within **7 days** for known-exploited CVEs and **30 days** for others. Additionally, the controller must be part of a **Red-Team / Purple-Team annual OT-specific penetration test**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes on This Question:**
> 1. **Recommending IT controls (e.g., antivirus, host firewall)** as Tier 1 OT controls — these often *break* OT operations. Examiners deduct marks for generic IT answers.
> 2. **Ignoring the override** — when S = 5 and O = 5, the override rule *must* be mentioned.
> 3. **Forgetting to use IEC 62443 vocabulary** (zones, conduits, SL-T) — KTU 2024 scheme mandates this.
> 4. **Not tying the controls to the *consequences*** of Tier 1 (e.g., "because the CDU processes flammable hydrocarbons at 350 °C, unidirectional data flow prevents an attacker from sending a malicious setpoint").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Criticality Classification** is a *vector* (6 dimensions), not a scalar.
- [x] The **six dimensions** are: **S, E, O, F, R, I** — remember as **"SEOFRI"** or **"Safety → Environment → Operations → Financial → Regulatory → Information"**.
- [x] Scoring is on a **1–5 scale** (1 = Negligible, 5 = Catastrophic).
- [x] Weights are **domain-specific** and must satisfy $\sum w_k = 1$.
- [x] The **Composite Criticality Score (CCS)** is a weighted linear sum: $\text{CCS}_i = \sum w_k X_{i,k}$.
- [x] **Tier cutoffs (KTU standard):** T1 $\geq 4.0$, T2 $\geq 3.0$, T3 $\geq 2.0$, T4 $<$ 2.0.
- [x] **Single-Failure Override Rule:** If any single dimension = 5, the asset is automatically **Tier 1**.
- [x] **CVSS scores vulnerabilities, not assets.** Criticality $\neq$ CVSS. Use **$\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{CCS}$**.
- [x] **SIL and SL-T must be aligned:** SIL 1 → SL-T 1/2, SIL 2 → SL-T 2/3, SIL 3 → SL-T 3/4, SIL 4 → SL-T 4.
- [x] **Interdependency context** can elevate an asset's tier even if its standalone CCS is low.
- [x] The classification pipeline is **cyclic** — review annually or on any major process/regulatory change.
- [x] **OT priority order** (memorize!): **Safety > Availability > Integrity > Confidentiality** (opposite of IT!).
- [x] Standard KTU answer structure: **Definition → Six Criteria with Examples → CCS Computation → Tier Assignment → Override Check → Proportional Controls**.
- [x] Always **state the tier explicitly** at the end of any computation answer.
- [x] Always **verify weight normalization** at the start of any computation.
- [x] Common frameworks referenced in KTU: **NIST CSF, NIST 800-82, IEC 62443, ISA-99, Purdue Model, NERC-CIP, ISO 27001**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Tip:** KTU 2024 scheme is **outcome-based (NEP 2020)**. The Course Outcomes (COs) for PECCT634 Module 2 are mapped to Bloom's Levels **L2 (Understand) → L4 (Analyze)**. To score full marks: (1) give a precise definition (L1/L2), (2) explain *why* each criterion matters with an OT example (L3), (3) *compute* CCS and assign tier (L4). Skip any of these three layers and you will lose 3–4 marks even if your final answer is "correct."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact analysis of OT asset failure on overall operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Impact analysis of OT asset failure on overall operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Operational Technology (OT) Asset Failure Impact Analysis** is a systematic, multi-disciplinary engineering process that identifies, quantifies, and evaluates the consequences arising from the malfunction, degradation, or complete failure of physical hardware, control systems, and associated software components within an industrial process. It maps the dependency relationships between critical assets (PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers, SCADA servers, HMI stations, smart sensors, and actuators) and the organization's operational, safety, environmental, financial, and reputational objectives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** Impact analysis is NOT a list of components. It is a *consequence-mapping exercise* that traces a single asset failure through its **first-order, second-order, and N-th order effects** on the **Safety–Production–Quality–Environment–Cost (SPQEC)** pentagon.

Formally, for any OT asset $A_i$, the impact is expressed as:

$$ I(A_i) = f(\text{Safety Loss}, \text{Production Loss}, \text{Quality Loss}, \text{Environmental Loss}, \text{Financial Loss}) $$

The function $f$ is usually a **weighted linear sum** of normalized sub-impacts, as will be derived in Section 3.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Analogy

Imagine an industrial plant is a **living human body**:

| Industrial OT Component | Human Body Equivalent |
|---|---|
| PLC / DCS Controller | Brain |
| Sensors (Pressure, Temperature, Flow) | Eyes, Ears, Skin |
| Actuators (Valves, Motors, Pumps) | Hands, Legs, Muscles |
| SCADA / HMI | Consciousness (Awareness) |
| Power Supply / UPS | Heart (Circulation) |
| Network (Industrial Ethernet) | Nervous System |
| Safety Instrumented System (SIS) | Reflexes / Immune System |

Now, an **OT asset failure** is equivalent to a medical condition. A **sensor failure** is "blindness" — the brain keeps issuing commands but with no real feedback, leading to dangerous actions. A **PLC failure** is a "stroke" — the entire control logic stops. A **network switch failure** is "nerve damage" — communication is severed. Just as a doctor performs an **impact analysis** (what happens to the patient if the liver fails?), an OT security engineer performs **impact analysis** to predict how the plant reacts when any single component fails.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common answer that only lists "production loss" scores **2/10**. Examiners expect the candidate to mention the **cascading, multi-dimensional nature** of the impact (Safety → Shutdown → Production → Financial → Reputation).

### 1.3 Why "Overall Operations" is the Operative Phrase

The phrase *overall operations* explicitly signals that the analysis must extend **beyond the failed asset itself**. Modern OT environments are deeply interconnected via Purdue Model Levels 0–3 (and sometimes Level 4/5), so a single field device failure can:

1. Trigger a **safety shutdown (SIF activation)** at Level 0–1.
2. Cause a **control loop instability** at Level 2.
3. Create a **SCADA alarm storm** at Level 3.
4. Mislead **MES planning** at Level 4.
5. Result in **erroneous ERP orders** at Level 5.

Hence, impact analysis must follow the **dependency graph**, not the asset list.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Dimensional Impact Model (SPQEC Pentagon)

Every OT asset failure is evaluated against **five orthogonal dimensions**. The acronym **SPQEC** is the KTU-blessed mnemonic.

1. **S — Safety Impact** (Human life & injury potential)
2. **P — Production Impact** (Throughput, downtime, OEE loss)
3. **Q — Quality Impact** (Product specification deviation, scrap rate)
4. **E — Environmental Impact** (Spills, emissions, regulatory fines)
5. **C — Cost / Financial Impact** (Direct loss + recovery + opportunity cost)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme CO2):** Safety ALWAYS dominates. An asset with **Catastrophic Safety** impact is *always* prioritized regardless of low financial cost. The Safety dimension is a **veto function**, not just a weighted variable.

### 2.2 Theoretical Frameworks for OT Impact Analysis

#### 2.2.1 IEC 62443-3-2 — Zones & Conduits

The standard mandates grouping assets into **Security Zones (SZ)** and **Conduits (C)**. Impact analysis is performed:

- **Within a zone** (intra-zone failure propagation).
- **Across conduits** (inter-zone failure propagation).
- **To critical assets** (SL-1 to SL-4: Security Level Target).

#### 2.2.2 NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3 — OT Impact Categories

NIST defines impact as: **Low, Moderate, High** on three axes:

- **Injury / Loss of Life**
- **Operational Mission**
- **Financial / Regulatory**

#### 2.2.3 FMECA (Failure Mode, Effects, and Criticality Analysis)

The classical reliability-engineering framework adapted for OT:

$$ RPN = S \times O \times D $$

where:
- $RPN$ = Risk Priority Number
- $S$ = Severity (1–10)
- $O$ = Occurrence likelihood (1–10)
- $D$ = Detectability / Difficulty to detect (1–10)

Higher $RPN$ implies higher priority for mitigation.

#### 2.2.4 Safety Integrity Level (SIL) — IEC 61508 / 61511

For safety-instrumented functions, the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** determines SIL:

| SIL | PFD Range | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$ | $10$ to $100$ |
| 2 | $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ | $100$ to $1000$ |
| 3 | $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$ | $1000$ to $10000$ |
| 4 | $10^{-5} \leq PFD < 10^{-4}$ | $10000$ to $100000$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **higher the SIL, the lower the tolerable failure probability**, and the more rigorous the impact analysis must be.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $I(A_i)$ | Total impact score of asset $A_i$ | Dimensionless (0–100) |
| $I = \sum w_k \cdot I_k$ | Weighted linear impact aggregation | $w_k \in [0,1]$, $\sum w_k = 1$ |
| $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number (FMECA) | Integer 1–1000 |
| $PFD$ | Probability of Failure on Demand | Probability (0–1) |
| $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | Dimensionless |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair | Hours |
| $MTBF$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Hours |
| $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Operational availability | 0–1 (or %) |
| $C_{\text{loss}} = D \times R \times H$ | Production loss cost | Currency |
| $D$ = downtime (hours), $R$ = rate (units/hr), $H$ = hourly margin (currency/unit) |  |  |
| $SIL = -\log_{10}(PFD)$ | Safety Integrity Level mapping | Integer 1–4 |
| $E[D] = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Expected failure interval | Hours, $\lambda$ = failure rate (1/hr) |
| $C_{\text{Risk}} = P_{\text{fail}} \times L_{\text{impact}}$ | Annualized Loss Expectancy | Currency/year |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, the symbol $R$ denotes **production rate**, NOT the resistance in electrical engineering. KTU examiners often use multi-domain notation — always read the question stem to disambiguate.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Impact analysis is the **entry point** for:

- **Business Continuity Planning (BCP)** and **Disaster Recovery (DR)** in plants.
- **Insurance underwriting** for industrial cyber insurance.
- **Regulatory compliance** (NERC-CIP, NIS2 Directive, ISA-99/IEC 62443).
- **CAPEX justification** — buying a redundant PLC is justified only if its failure impact is quantified.
- **Incident Response Prioritization** — which alarms to escalate first.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Weighted Linear Impact Score

**Problem Setup:** Given an OT asset $A_i$ with five sub-impact scores $I_S, I_P, I_Q, I_E, I_C \in [0, 10]$ and corresponding weights $w_S, w_P, w_Q, w_E, w_C \in [0, 1]$ such that $\sum w_k = 1$, derive the total impact $I(A_i)$.

**Step 1 — Define the Impact Vector.**

$$ \vec{I_i} = [I_S, I_P, I_Q, I_E, I_C]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{5} $$

This is a column vector capturing the five normalized sub-impacts.

**Step 2 — Define the Weight Vector.**

$$ \vec{W} = [w_S, w_P, w_Q, w_E, w_C] \in \mathbb{R}^{5} $$

The constraint $\sum w_k = 1$ ensures that the impact is normalized between 0 and 10.

**Step 3 — Form the Linear Aggregation.**

The total impact is the inner product of the two vectors:

$$ I(A_i) = \vec{W} \cdot \vec{I_i} $$

**Step 4 — Expand the Dot Product.**

$$ I(A_i) = w_S \cdot I_S + w_P \cdot I_P + w_Q \cdot I_Q + w_E \cdot I_E + w_C \cdot I_C $$

**Step 5 — Apply the Normalization Constraint.**

Since $0 \leq I_k \leq 10$ and $0 \leq w_k \leq 1$, the maximum possible impact is when all $I_k = 10$:

$$ I_{\max} = 10 \cdot \sum_{k} w_k = 10 \cdot 1 = 10 $$

Similarly, the minimum is $0$. Hence the impact score is naturally bounded in $[0, 10]$.

**Step 6 — Convert to a Risk Class.**

| Range of $I(A_i)$ | Risk Class | KTU Action |
|---|---|---|
| $0 \leq I < 2$ | Negligible (Green) | Routine maintenance |
| $2 \leq I < 5$ | Minor (Yellow) | Schedule mitigations |
| $5 \leq I < 8$ | Major (Orange) | Mitigate within 30 days |
| $8 \leq I \leq 10$ | Catastrophic (Red) | Immediate action, redundant design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 valuation, if a candidate uses the formula but **forgets the constraint $\sum w_k = 1$**, 1 mark is deducted for normalization error.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Boiler Feedwater Pump PLC

**Scenario:** A **Boiler Feedwater Pump PLC** in a thermal power plant fails.

**Given Sub-Impact Scores (out of 10):**

- $I_S = 9$ (Boiler dry-fire can cause explosion → multiple fatalities)
- $I_P = 8$ (Trip of 210 MW unit → 8 hours of lost generation)
- $I_Q = 3$ (Quality not immediately affected; tertiary)
- $I_E = 6$ (Possible chemical release into cooling water)
- $I_C = 7$ (₹2.1 crore revenue + restart cost)

**Given Weights (from plant safety policy):**

$$ w_S = 0.40, \quad w_P = 0.20, \quad w_Q = 0.10, \quad w_E = 0.20, \quad w_C = 0.10 $$

**Verification of constraint:**

$$ 0.40 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark $$

**Step-by-Step Calculation:**

$$ I = (0.40)(9) + (0.20)(8) + (0.10)(3) + (0.20)(6) + (0.10)(7) $$

$$ I = 3.60 + 1.60 + 0.30 + 1.20 + 0.70 $$

$$ I = 7.40 $$

**Step-by-Step Arithmetic Breakdown:**

- $0.40 \times 9 = 3.60$
- $0.20 \times 8 = 1.60$
- $0.10 \times 3 = 0.30$
- $0.20 \times 6 = 1.20$
- $0.10 \times 7 = 0.70$

**Sum:** $3.60 + 1.60 = 5.20$; $5.20 + 0.30 = 5.50$; $5.50 + 1.20 = 6.70$; $6.70 + 0.70 = 7.40$.

**Conclusion:** $I(A_{\text{PLC}}) = 7.40$ falls in the **Major (Orange)** band. Mitigation must be implemented within 30 days, with a redundant hot-standby PLC.

### 3.3 Derivation of the RPN (FMECA) Score

**Given:** A flow transmitter FT-101 has:

- Severity $S = 8$ (Loss of feedwater regulation → tube rupture possible)
- Occurrence $O = 4$ (Failure rate $\lambda = 0.0002$/hr, $O = 5\lambda \times 10^4 \approx 4$)
- Detectability $D = 7$ (Failure silent; only inferred from cascade)

**Step 1:** Apply the formula:

$$ RPN = S \times O \times D $$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$ RPN = 8 \times 4 \times 7 $$

**Step 3:** Multiply:

$$ RPN = 32 \times 7 = 224 $$

**Step 4:** Compare against threshold. KTU benchmarks suggest $RPN \geq 150$ requires immediate corrective action. Hence, **FT-101 must be added to the watch list** and a redundant transmitter installed.

### 3.4 Production Loss Cost Derivation

**Given:** Pump trip in a refinery with $R = 120 \text{ barrels/hr}$, $H = \text{₹}3500/\text{barrel margin}$, $D = 6$ hours.

**Step 1 — Production Loss Volume:**

$$ V_{\text{loss}} = D \times R = 6 \times 120 = 720 \text{ barrels} $$

**Step 2 — Direct Revenue Loss:**

$$ C_{\text{loss}} = V_{\text{loss}} \times H = 720 \times 3500 $$

**Step 3 — Multiplication:**

$$ 720 \times 3500 = 720 \times (3000 + 500) = 2{,}160{,}000 + 360{,}000 = 2{,}520{,}000 $$

**Step 4 — Final Answer:**

$$ C_{\text{loss}} = \text{₹}25{,}20{,}000 \quad (\text{₹} 25.2 \text{ lakh}) $$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Impact Propagation Across Purdue Levels

| Purdue Level | Asset | Failure Example | First-Order Impact | Second-Order Impact | Third-Order Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| L0 | Pressure Transmitter | Sensor stuck at 4 mA | Controller sees zero pressure | Valve opens fully | Pump cavitation → rupture |
| L1 | PLC (Siemens S7-1500) | CPU halt | Loop freezes in last state | SIS triggers shutdown | Plant trips |
| L2 | SCADA Server | HMI freeze | Operator blindness | Manual intervention delayed | Production loss ₹₹ |
| L3 | Historian | Time-skewed data | Wrong trend plots | Bad root-cause analysis | Repeated failures |
| L4 | MES | Order mis-route | Wrong product mix | Customer shipment error | Penalty + reputation loss |
| L5 | ERP | PO duplication | Excess raw material | Inventory cost | Working capital lock |

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners LOVE this table. Drawing it in the exam (even hand-drawn) instantly elevates a 7-mark answer to 10+ marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 OT Asset Failure Cascade Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Asset Failure Trigger Event] --> B1[First Order Local Effect]
    B1 --> C1[Control Loop Instability]
    B1 --> C2[Sensor Reading Anomaly]
    B1 --> C3[Actuator Sticking]
    C1 --> D1[SIS Trip Decision]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> E1{SIL Threshold Exceeded?}
    E1 -- YES --> F1[Safety Shutdown]
    E1 -- NO --> F2[Process Continues with Degraded Performance]
    F1 --> G1[Plant Wide Production Loss]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[SCADA Alarm Storm]
    H1 --> I1[Operator Confusion]
    I1 --> J1[Misdiagnosis]
    J1 --> K1[Wrong Manual Action]
    K1 --> L1[Secondary Incident]
    L1 --> M1[Catastrophic Loss Event]
    M1 --> N1[Final Impact: Safety, Production, Quality, Environment, Cost]

    style A1 fill:#ff9999
    style M1 fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
    style N1 fill:#003366,color:#ffffff
    style E1 fill:#ffcc00
```

### 4.2 SPQEC Five-Dimensional Impact Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AF[OT Asset Failure] --> SPQEC{SPQEC Impact Pentagon}
    SPQEC --> S1[Safety Impact]
    SPQEC --> P1[Production Impact]
    SPQEC --> Q1[Quality Impact]
    SPQEC --> E1D[Environmental Impact]
    SPQEC --> C1D[Cost Financial Impact]
    S1 --> S2[Injury, Fatality, Asset Destruction]
    P1 --> P2[Downtime, Throughput Loss, OEE Drop]
    Q1 --> Q2[Spec Deviation, Scrap, Rework]
    E1D --> E2[Emissions, Spills, Regulatory Fine]
    C1D --> C2[Direct Loss, Recovery, Opportunity Cost]

    style AF fill:#ff6666,color:#ffffff
    style SPQEC fill:#3366cc,color:#ffffff
    style S2 fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#ff9900
    style Q2 fill:#ffcc00
    style E2 fill:#009933,color:#ffffff
    style C2 fill:#660066,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 FMECA Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Step 1: Identify OT Asset] --> B2[Step 2: List All Failure Modes]
    B2 --> C2[Step 3: Determine Local Effect]
    C2 --> D2[Step 4: Determine System Effect]
    D2 --> E2[Step 5: Assign Severity S]
    E2 --> F2[Step 6: Estimate Occurrence O]
    F2 --> G2[Step 7: Estimate Detectability D]
    G2 --> H2[Step 8: Compute RPN]
    H2 --> I2{RPN Threshold Check}
    I2 -- RPN GE 150 --> J2[High Priority: Immediate Action]
    I2 -- 100 LE RPN LT 150 --> K2[Medium Priority: Plan Mitigation]
    I2 -- RPN LT 100 --> L2[Low Priority: Monitor]
    J2 --> M2[Redesign, Redundancy, Alarm]
    K2 --> N2[Scheduled Maintenance]
    L2 --> O2[Routine Inspection]

    style A2 fill:#3366cc,color:#ffffff
    style H2 fill:#ff9900
    style I2 fill:#ffcc00
    style J2 fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
    style K2 fill:#ff9900
    style L2 fill:#009933,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 OT Impact Analysis Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1 Asset Inventory] --> P2[Phase 2 Dependency Mapping]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Failure Mode Enumeration]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Impact Scoring SPQEC]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5 Risk Computation RPN]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6 Prioritization and Treatment]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 7 Documentation and Review]
    P7 --> P1

    style P1 fill:#003366,color:#ffffff
    style P4 fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
    style P5 fill:#ff9900
    style P6 fill:#009933,color:#ffffff
    style P7 fill:#660066,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The last diagram illustrates the **cyclical nature** of impact analysis — it is not a one-time activity. After every configuration change, the analysis loop must be re-executed. KTU examiners often test this with questions like *"Why is impact analysis a continuous process?"* — the answer lies in this iterative loop.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term "OT Asset Failure Impact Analysis" and list its **five primary dimensions** as per the SPQEC framework.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Impact analysis of OT asset failure is the systematic process of evaluating the consequences of malfunction, degradation, or failure of physical and cyber-physical assets in an industrial control system. The five dimensions are:
1. **S** — Safety (human life and injury)
2. **P** — Production (downtime and throughput)
3. **Q** — Quality (specification deviation)
4. **E** — Environmental (emissions and spills)
5. **C** — Cost (direct, recovery, and opportunity)

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the five dimensions — 0.4 marks each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **first-order** and **second-order impacts** of an OT asset failure. Give one industrial example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **first-order impact** is the *immediate, local* consequence at the failed component or its direct loop. A **second-order impact** is the *propagated consequence* on adjacent systems, higher Purdue levels, or downstream operations.

**Example:** Failure of a temperature sensor (first-order: incorrect reading) leads to a steam valve opening fully (second-order), which causes a boiler tube overheating (third-order), resulting in a plant shutdown (fourth-order).

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark distinction, 1 mark example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**
**(a)** Explain the **FMECA methodology** in detail as applied to OT assets. Derive the Risk Priority Number (RPN) and discuss its limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a **chemical reactor cooling system** with the following failure scenario:
- A cooling water pump trips, leading to a temperature rise from 80 °C to 145 °C in 12 minutes.
- The Safety Instrumented System (SIS) has a PFD of $5 \times 10^{-3}$.
- Production rate is 4.5 tonnes/hour, margin is ₹18,000/tonne.
- Downtime estimate is 9 hours.

Compute the **annualized loss expectancy** if the failure occurs **twice a year**, and determine the **SIL rating** of the SIS. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — FMECA Definition (1 Mark):**
Failure Mode, Effects, and Criticality Analysis (FMECA) is a bottom-up inductive analytical method that identifies potential failure modes of an asset, evaluates their effects on system mission, and ranks them by criticality.

**Step 2 — FMECA Steps (3 Marks):**
1. Define the system boundary and list all OT assets.
2. For each asset, enumerate all plausible failure modes.
3. Determine the **local effect** (immediate) and **system effect** (propagated).
4. Assign **Severity (S)** on a 1–10 scale.
5. Assign **Occurrence (O)** on a 1–10 scale (from failure rate data).
6. Assign **Detectability (D)** on a 1–10 scale (10 = impossible to detect).
7. Compute **$RPN = S \times O \times D$**.
8. Prioritize by RPN, with $RPN \geq 150$ requiring immediate action.

**Step 3 — RPN Derivation (1 Mark):**
The RPN is a multiplicative risk metric combining severity, likelihood, and detection. Derived from military reliability engineering (MIL-STD-1629), it compresses three dimensions into a single scalar in the range 1–1000.

**Step 4 — Limitations (2 Marks):**
- Subjective scoring of S, O, D.
- RPN thresholds (e.g., 150) are arbitrary.
- Masks high-S, low-O failures.
- No consideration of failure mode interactions.
- Does not account for cost-benefit of mitigation.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Production Loss per Event (2 Marks):**

$$ C_{\text{loss}} = D \times R \times H = 9 \times 4.5 \times 18{,}000 $$

$$ = 40.5 \times 18{,}000 = 7{,}29{,}000 $$

$$ C_{\text{loss}} = \text{₹}7{,}29{,}000 \text{ per event} $$

**Step 2 — Annualized Loss Expectancy (3 Marks):**
Given the failure occurs twice per year, $n = 2$:

$$ \text{ALE} = n \times C_{\text{loss}} = 2 \times 7{,}29{,}000 $$

$$ \text{ALE} = \text{₹}14{,}58{,}000 \text{ per year} $$

**Step 3 — SIL Determination (2 Marks):**
Given $PFD = 5 \times 10^{-3}$:

$$ SIL = -\log_{10}(PFD) = -\log_{10}(5 \times 10^{-3}) $$

$$ = -[\log_{10}(5) + \log_{10}(10^{-3})] = -[0.6990 - 3] $$

$$ = 2.3010 $$

The integer part is $2$, so the SIL is **SIL 2** (with PFD range $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Computing $C_{\text{loss}} = D \times R \times H$ but forgetting to multiply by occurrence frequency → Lose 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Rounding $SIL = 2.3010$ to **SIL 3** — incorrect. SIL is determined by which **decade** the PFD falls in, not by rounding. Here $PFD = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ is in the $10^{-3}$ decade → **SIL 2**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not stating units in the final answer → Lose 0.5 mark for unit omission.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative Path]**
**(a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the **cascading impact of an OT asset failure across Purdue Model Levels 0 to 5**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A water treatment plant's **SCADA server** crashes. The plant has:
- Production rate: 8000 litres/hour
- Margin: ₹6/litre
- Recovery time: 4 hours
- $I_S = 7$, $I_P = 9$, $I_Q = 6$, $I_E = 5$, $I_C = 8$
- $w_S = 0.35$, $w_P = 0.25$, $w_Q = 0.10$, $w_E = 0.15$, $w_C = 0.15$

Calculate the **total impact score** $I$ and identify the **risk class** with the recommended action. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Purdue Model Recall (1 Mark):**
The Purdue Model segments enterprise networks into Levels 0 (Physical Process) to 5 (Enterprise), providing a layered view of OT-IT convergence.

**Step 2 — Cascade Description (5 Marks, 1 mark per level):**
- **Level 0 (Process Sensors & Actuators):** A pressure transmitter drifts → provides a false low reading to the controller.
- **Level 1 (PLCs / DCS Controllers):** PLC misinterprets data → opens a vent valve excessively → process pressure drops.
- **Level 2 (SCADA / HMI):** SCADA displays conflicting alarms → operator receives a flood of notifications.
- **Level 3 (Operations / Historians):** Historian logs wrong trends → control engineers cannot perform root-cause analysis post-incident.
- **Level 4 (MES / Site Manufacturing):** MES, using bad historian data, mis-schedules batch release → wrong shipment to customer.
- **Level 5 (ERP / Business):** ERP duplicates raw material purchase order based on MES mis-communication → inventory overstock and working capital lock.

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 Mark):**
This demonstrates that an asset failure at L0 propagates through control, supervisory, planning, and enterprise layers — justifying the "**overall operations**" scope of impact analysis.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Verify Weight Constraint (1 Mark):**

$$ \sum w_k = 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.15 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark $$

**Step 2 — Direct Production Loss (2 Marks):**

$$ C_{\text{loss}} = D \times R \times H = 4 \times 8000 \times 6 = \text{₹}1{,}92{,}000 $$

(This is supplementary context; the main deliverable is the $I$ score.)

**Step 3 — Compute Weighted Impact (4 Marks):**

$$ I = (0.35)(7) + (0.25)(9) + (0.10)(6) + (0.15)(5) + (0.15)(8) $$

**Step 4 — Multiply Out Each Term:**
- $0.35 \times 7 = 2.45$
- $0.25 \times 9 = 2.25$
- $0.10 \times 6 = 0.60$
- $0.15 \times 5 = 0.75$
- $0.15 \times 8 = 1.20$

**Step 5 — Sum:**

$$ 2.45 + 2.25 = 4.70; \quad 4.70 + 0.60 = 5.30; \quad 5.30 + 0.75 = 6.05; \quad 6.05 + 1.20 = 7.25 $$

$$ I = 7.25 $$

**Step 6 — Risk Classification (Mark embedded in above):**
$5 \leq I = 7.25 < 8$ → falls in the **Major (Orange)** band.

**Recommended Action:** Mitigate within 30 days. Implement a redundant SCADA server (hot standby) and a fault-tolerant historian cluster.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Adding the weights and forgetting to verify the sum equals 1 — Lose 0.5 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Stating "Major" without specifying the numeric range — Lose 0.5 mark. Always write the range, e.g., "$5 \leq 7.25 < 8$".
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting the **direct production loss** (₹1,92,000) — examiners sometimes allocate a sub-mark for recognizing financial impact numerically.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **SPQEC** is the **5-dimension impact mnemonic** (Safety, Production, Quality, Environment, Cost) — KTU 2024 syllabus core.
- **Safety dominates:** Safety is a **veto function**, not just a weighted variable.
- **RPN formula** = $S \times O \times D$; threshold for action is typically **$\geq 150$**.
- **Total impact** = $\sum w_k \cdot I_k$ with constraint $\sum w_k = 1$.
- **SIL** = $-\log_{10}(PFD)$; ranges are 1–4 based on **decade**, not rounding.
- **Production loss cost** = $D \times R \times H$ (downtime × rate × hourly margin).
- **Purdue Model cascade:** L0 failure → L1 instability → L2 alarm storm → L3 historian error → L4 MES mis-route → L5 ERP fault.
- **FMECA is bottom-up inductive**, while **FTA (Fault Tree Analysis) is top-down deductive** — examiners love this contrast.
- **Impact analysis is iterative**, not a one-time activity — re-run after every configuration or asset change.
- **Redundancy justification** uses ALE = $n \times C_{\text{loss}}$ compared against CAPEX.
- **Available standards:** IEC 62443-3-2 (zones & conduits), NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3, ISA-99, IEC 61508/61511.
- **Risk classes:** Green (0–2), Yellow (2–5), Orange (5–8), Red (8–10).
- **Always state units** in numerical answers — examiners deduct 0.5 marks otherwise.
- **Draw the cascade diagram** whenever asked about "overall operations" — instant 2-mark bonus.
- **Mention at least one standard** (IEC 62443, NIST, or ISA) in every long answer to demonstrate CO2 alignment.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the Exam Hall:** **"S-P-Q-E-C, S-owns-the-veto, RPN-triple-product, SIL-is-decade-not-round"** — write this on the first page of your answer booklet the moment you receive it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prioritizing security efforts based on asset criticality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prioritizing security efforts based on asset criticality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 Module 2: Prioritizing Security Efforts Based on Asset Criticality

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (ISA/IEC 62443 Aligned):**
> **Asset Criticality** is a qualitative or quantitative measure assigned to an Operational Technology (OT) asset that reflects the **degree of harm** that would result from the asset's **confidentiality, integrity, or availability (CIA triad)** being compromised. It is the primary input variable for **prioritizing security efforts** in an OT environment, ensuring that finite cybersecurity resources (budget, personnel, controls) are allocated to assets whose failure would cause the greatest **safety, environmental, financial, operational, or reputational impact**.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, this process is formally known as **Criticality Analysis** and is a sub-component of the broader **OT Risk Assessment Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-82r3 / IEC 62443-3-2)**.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Triage Model

Imagine a disaster strikes a city and 500 injured people arrive at a hospital. The doctors do **not** treat them on a "first-come, first-served" basis. Instead, they perform **triage** — they rapidly classify patients by how critical their condition is:

| Triage Color | Patient State | Doctor Action |
|---|---|---|
| 🔴 **RED** | Life-threatening (heart attack) | Immediate ICU, senior doctor |
| 🟡 **YELLOW** | Serious but stable (broken leg) | Treated within hours |
| 🟢 **GREEN** | Minor injury (bruise) | Basic first aid |

**Asset Criticality works exactly the same way in an OT plant.** A Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) running a nuclear reactor's cooling system is your **RED** patient. A printer in the admin office is your **GREEN** patient. You cannot secure everything equally — so you secure the *critical* things first, harder, and faster.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECCT634):**
> Asset criticality is the **filter** that converts an unmanageable list of *thousands* of OT devices into a *ranked, prioritized shortlist* of devices requiring the highest protection. Without criticality analysis, security spending is essentially random.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars Driving Criticality in OT

Unlike Information Technology (IT) — where criticality is mostly about *data confidentiality* — in **OT**, the order of importance is famously **inverted**:

$$ \text{IT Priority Order: } C \rightarrow I \rightarrow A \quad \text{(Confidentiality first)} $$

$$ \text{OT Priority Order: } A \rightarrow I \rightarrow C \quad \text{(Availability first)} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Why "A" first in OT?** Because if a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) goes offline for even 60 seconds in a refinery, it can cause a **catastrophic explosion, environmental disaster, or loss of life**. Confidentiality of a temperature reading is irrelevant if the *plant itself* is destroyed.

The four primary impact categories that determine an OT asset's criticality are:

1. **🛡️ Safety Impact (S)** — Can compromise cause injury or death? (Highest weight in OT)
2. **🌍 Environmental Impact (E)** — Can compromise cause spills, emissions, or ecological damage?
3. **💰 Financial Impact (F)** — Production loss, equipment replacement, regulatory fines.
4. **📉 Operational/Reputational Impact (O)** — Service disruption, customer trust, brand damage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Criticality-Driven Prioritization Pipeline (CDPP)

Prioritizing security efforts based on asset criticality follows a **five-stage pipeline** recognized by both **IEC 62443-3-2** and **NIST SP 800-82r3**:

### Stage 1: Asset Identification & Inventory
- Enumerate *every* hardware and software asset in the OT environment.
- For each asset, capture: **Name, Type, Location, Function, Network Zone, Owner**.
- This is a **Zone & Conduit** (IEC 62443-3-2) prerequisite.

### Stage 2: Functional & Mission Dependency Mapping
- Trace which business processes depend on which assets.
- Example: *Steam Cracker Unit → Distillation Column PLC → SIS → Production Schedule*.
- An asset inherits criticality from the **most critical function** it supports.

### Stage 3: Impact Dimension Scoring
- For each asset, score each impact dimension (S, E, F, O) on a standard scale.

| Score | Label | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **5** | Catastrophic | Death, multi-million $ loss, plant shutdown > 1 month |
| **4** | Major | Severe injury, regulatory shutdown, major $ loss |
| **3** | Moderate | Lost-time injury, partial shutdown, significant $ loss |
| **2** | Minor | First-aid injury, brief disruption, moderate $ loss |
| **1** | Negligible | No injury, no disruption, minimal $ loss |
| **0** | None | No impact at all |

### Stage 4: Criticality Aggregation (The Math)

The **Composite Criticality Score (CCS)** of an asset is computed as the **weighted sum** of the four impact dimensions:

$$ \text{CCS} = w_S \cdot S + w_E \cdot E + w_F \cdot F + w_O \cdot O $$

Where the **weights satisfy the normalization condition**:

$$ w_S + w_E + w_F + w_O = 1.0 \quad \text{and} \quad w_S \geq w_E \geq w_F \geq w_O $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why must the weights be ordered this way?**
> In OT, *Safety* is the supreme concern. The weighting hierarchy $w_S \ge w_E \ge w_F \ge w_O$ encodes the **ethical and legal precedence** of human life over environment, over money, over reputation. A typical industrial weighting is:
>
> $$ w_S = 0.40, \quad w_E = 0.30, \quad w_F = 0.20, \quad w_O = 0.10 $$

### Stage 5: Tiered Prioritization & Control Allocation

| CCS Range | Tier | Label | Security Effort Allocation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **4.0 – 5.0** | **T1** | Mission-Critical | Maximum controls, 24/7 SOC monitoring, SL-3 / SL-4 |
| **3.0 – 3.9** | **T2** | Business-Critical | Strong controls, periodic monitoring, SL-2 |
| **2.0 – 2.9** | **T3** | Operational | Baseline controls, SL-1 / SL-2 |
| **1.0 – 1.9** | **T4** | Administrative | Standard IT-grade controls |
| **0.0 – 0.9** | **T5** | Non-Essential | Minimal controls |

> Here **SL** refers to the **Security Level** concept from IEC 62443-3-3, where SL-1 is casual/coincidental adversary and SL-4 is nation-state adversary.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the master reference for solving any numerical problem on asset criticality prioritization in the KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Composite Criticality Score | $\text{CCS} = w_S S + w_E E + w_F F + w_O O$ | Dimensionless, $[0, 5]$ | Rank-assign every OT asset |
| 2 | Normalization Constraint | $w_S + w_E + w_F + w_O = 1$ | Sum = 1.0 | Validates weight selection |
| 3 | Weight Precedence | $w_S \geq w_E \geq w_F \geq w_O$ | Descending order | Enforces safety-first ethics |
| 4 | Risk Score (full model) | $R = T \times V \times I$ where $I \equiv \text{CCS}$ | $[0, 125]$ normalized | Determines *where* to spend $ |
| 5 | Prioritization Index | $PI_i = \frac{\text{CCS}_i \cdot \text{Exposure}_i}{\text{Cost of Control}_i}$ | Score per $1000 | Ranks candidate controls |
| 6 | Tier Threshold | $T_k = (k-1) \cdot 1.0$ for $k=1..5$ | Tier boundaries | Auto-bucket assets |
| 7 | % of Effort Rule (Pareto) | $E_{\text{T1}} \approx 0.50, E_{\text{T2}} \approx 0.30$ | Fraction of budget | Resource allocation heuristic |
| 8 | Criticality Inheritance | $\text{CCS}_{\text{child}} \geq \text{CCS}_{\text{parent}}$ | Monotonic | For dependent assets |
| 9 | Mean CCS of a Zone | $\overline{\text{CCS}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{CCS}_i$ | $[0, 5]$ | Compares zones |
| 10 | Max CCS in Zone | $\text{CCS}_{\max} = \max_i \text{CCS}_i$ | $[0, 5]$ | Drives zone protection level |

## 2.3 Why This System Matters in Engineering Practice

In real engineering deployments, a mid-size refinery has **~15,000–60,000 OT I/O points** and ~500 networked controllers. Without criticality-based prioritization:

- **Budget** is wasted securing a coffee machine in the control room while the SIS is left exposed.
- **Compliance** with NERC-CIP, IEC 62443, NIST CSF, and ISA 99 cannot be demonstrated.
- **Incident Response** cannot triage alarms effectively during a live attack (cf. **Triton/TRISIS attack on SIS, 2017**).
- **Insurance premiums** (cyber-liability) become unaffordable because the insurer cannot model residual risk.

Thus, criticality prioritization is not merely an academic exercise — it is the **single most important risk-management decision** an OT cybersecurity engineer makes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Criticality Analysis of a Water Treatment Plant

A water-treatment SCADA system contains five assets. The chief security officer has set the weights to:

$$ w_S = 0.40, \quad w_E = 0.35, \quad w_F = 0.15, \quad w_O = 0.10 $$

We are told to compute the Composite Criticality Score (CCS) for each asset, then assign it a Tier, then propose the priority of security efforts.

### Asset Inventory and Raw Impact Scores

| Asset ID | Asset Description | $S$ | $E$ | $F$ | $O$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A1** | Chlorine Dosing PLC (Safety-critical) | 5 | 5 | 4 | 5 |
| **A2** | SCADA Historian Server | 3 | 2 | 4 | 3 |
| **A3** | Engineering Workstation | 4 | 3 | 3 | 2 |
| **A4** | Office Printer (DMZ) | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| **A5** | Pump Motor VFD Controller | 3 | 2 | 5 | 4 |

### Step 3.1.1 — Verification of Weight Constraints

$$ w_S + w_E + w_F + w_O = 0.40 + 0.35 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00 \checkmark $$

$$ w_S (0.40) \geq w_E (0.35) \geq w_F (0.15) \geq w_O (0.10) \checkmark $$

Both constraints satisfied. The weighting is valid.

### Step 3.1.2 — Calculation of CCS for Asset A1 (Chlorine PLC)

$$ \text{CCS}_{A1} = (0.40)(5) + (0.35)(5) + (0.15)(4) + (0.10)(5) $$

$$ \text{CCS}_{A1} = 2.00 + 1.75 + 0.60 + 0.50 $$

$$ \text{CCS}_{A1} = 4.85 $$

### Step 3.1.3 — Calculation of CCS for Asset A2 (Historian)

$$ \text{CCS}_{A2} = (0.40)(3) + (0.35)(2) + (0.15)(4) + (0.10)(3) $$

$$ \text{CCS}_{A2} = 1.20 + 0.70 + 0.60 + 0.30 = 2.80 $$

### Step 3.1.4 — Calculation of CCS for Asset A3 (Engineering Workstation)

$$ \text{CCS}_{A3} = (0.40)(4) + (0.35)(3) + (0.15)(3) + (0.10)(2) $$

$$ \text{CCS}_{A3} = 1.60 + 1.05 + 0.45 + 0.20 = 3.30 $$

### Step 3.1.5 — Calculation of CCS for Asset A4 (Office Printer)

$$ \text{CCS}_{A4} = (0.40)(1) + (0.35)(1) + (0.15)(1) + (0.10)(1) $$

$$ \text{CCS}_{A4} = 0.40 + 0.35 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00 $$

### Step 3.1.6 — Calculation of CCS for Asset A5 (Pump VFD)

$$ \text{CCS}_{A5} = (0.40)(3) + (0.35)(2) + (0.15)(5) + (0.10)(4) $$

$$ \text{CCS}_{A5} = 1.20 + 0.70 + 0.75 + 0.40 = 3.05 $$

### Step 3.1.7 — Tabulated Results & Tier Assignment

| Asset | CCS | Tier (Threshold) | Priority Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| A1 (Chlorine PLC) | 4.85 | **T1 — Mission-Critical** | 🥇 **1** |
| A3 (Eng. Workstation) | 3.30 | **T2 — Business-Critical** | 🥈 **2** |
| A5 (Pump VFD) | 3.05 | **T2 — Business-Critical** | 🥉 **3** |
| A2 (Historian) | 2.80 | **T3 — Operational** | 4 |
| A4 (Office Printer) | 1.00 | **T4 — Administrative** | 5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observations a KTU Examiner will test:**
> 1. **A1 dominates** because of the $w_S$ weight on its maximum safety score. Even though A5 has a higher *financial* score (F = 5), A1's *safety* dominance pushes it to #1. This is the **safety-first principle** of OT.
> 2. **A3 beats A2** despite A2 having a higher financial score, because A3 has direct safety/E access (engineers can reprogram PLCs).
> 3. **A4 is correctly deprioritized** — securing a printer first is a sign of an immature OT security program.

### Step 3.1.8 — Derivation of the Prioritization Index (PI)

To allocate a finite budget (say, ₹10,00,000) across candidate controls, the security officer must rank the *controls*, not just the assets. The **Prioritization Index** for control $j$ is:

$$ PI_j = \frac{\text{CCS}_{\text{target asset}} \times \text{Exposure}_j}{\text{Cost}_j} $$

Where $\text{Exposure}_j$ is the fraction of attack surface closed by control $j$ (range $[0,1]$) and $\text{Cost}_j$ is in ₹10,000 units.

Suppose for A1 (CCS = 4.85) we have three controls:

| Control $j$ | Exposure | Cost (₹10k) | PI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Network segmentation (VLAN) | 0.8 | 5 | $(4.85 \times 0.8) / 5 = 0.776$ |
| Multi-factor auth on PLC | 0.5 | 2 | $(4.85 \times 0.5) / 2 = 1.213$ |
| 24/7 SOC monitoring | 0.9 | 15 | $(4.85 \times 0.9) / 15 = 0.291$ |

**Order of implementation:** MFA → Segmentation → SOC Monitoring.

This shows the *non-obvious* power of the prioritization index: the cheapest, most effective control wins first — the security officer does not chase "fancy" controls.

### Step 3.1.9 — Mean and Max CCS of the Plant (Zone Summary)

$$ \overline{\text{CCS}} = \frac{4.85 + 2.80 + 3.30 + 1.00 + 3.05}{5} = \frac{15.00}{5} = 3.00 $$

$$ \text{CCS}_{\max} = 4.85 \quad (\text{Asset A1}) $$

The mean (3.00) tells the auditor that the plant's *average* criticality is "Business-Critical", but the maximum (4.85) tells the auditor that the *worst-case* asset is "Mission-Critical" — both metrics are needed for **Defense-in-Depth** planning.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Impact Types to Controls

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is the *single most important* study tool for KTU 2024 PECCT634. It directly maps criticality dimensions to the *family* of controls you would prioritize.

| Impact Dim. | High Score $\rightarrow$ Asset Type | Primary Control Family | KTU-Standard Control IDs (IEC 62443-3-3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Safety (S) | SIS, ESD, Burner Mgmt, Chlorine | **Physical isolation + Zone segregation** | SR 1.1, SR 2.1, SR 3.1, SR 5.1 |
| Environmental (E) | Chemical dosing, Flare stack, Boiler | **Network segmentation + Alarm mgmt** | SR 1.2, SR 3.4, SR 6.2 |
| Financial (F) | Production line PLC, Batch reactor | **Backup + Change control** | SR 7.1, SR 7.2, SR 7.6 |
| Operational (O) | Historian, Reporting server | **Logging + Patch management** | SR 2.8, SR 2.10, SR 7.4 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Five-Stage Criticality Prioritization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Stage 1 Asset Inventory Zone and Conduit Mapping"] --> A2["Stage 2 Dependency Mapping Mission and Function Tracing"]
    A2 --> A3["Stage 3 Impact Scoring S E F O Dimensions"]
    A3 --> A4["Stage 4 Weighted Aggregation CCS equals wS times S plus wE times E plus wF times F plus wO times O"]
    A4 --> A5["Stage 5 Tier Assignment T1 through T5 Bucketing and Control Allocation"]
    A5 --> A6["Prioritized Security Effort Budget People Controls Are Deployed Here"]

    style A1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#7b1fa2,color:#000
    style A4 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c,color:#000
    style A5 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000
    style A6 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Purdue Model with Criticality Overlays

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["Level 5 Enterprise Network Internet Email ERP Low Criticality T4"]
    L4["Level 4 Site Business Planning MES Reporting Moderate T3"]
    L35["Level 3.5 DMZ Patch Server Anti Virus Replication Broker Medium T3"]
    L3["Level 3 Site Operations SCADA HMI Historian High T2"]
    L2["Level 2 Area Supervisory Control Engineering Station High T2"]
    L1["Level 1 Basic Control PLC DCS Controllers Mission Critical T1"]
    L0["Level 0 Physical Process Sensors Actuators SIS Mission Critical T1"]

    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L35
    L35 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L0

    style L0 fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L1 fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L2 fill:#f57c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L3 fill:#fbc02d,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L35 fill:#fdd835,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L4 fill:#9e9d24,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L5 fill:#558b2f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Reading guide:** The deeper the level (closer to L0/L1), the higher the criticality and the higher the priority of security effort. This is the inverse of "where the budget usually goes" in IT-heavy organizations.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Criticality-Driven Control Allocation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is CCS greater than or equal to 4.0"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> T1["T1 Mission Critical Apply SL3 SL4 Controls"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is CCS between 3.0 and 3.99"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> T2["T2 Business Critical Apply SL2 SL3 Controls"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Is CCS between 2.0 and 2.99"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> T3["T3 Operational Apply SL1 SL2 Controls"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Is CCS between 1.0 and 1.99"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> T4["T4 Administrative Apply Baseline IT Controls"]
    Q4 -- No --> T5["T5 Non Essential Minimal Controls"]

    style T1 fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T2 fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T3 fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style T4 fill:#9e9d24,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T5 fill:#558b2f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Asset Criticality Inheritance Across Dependency Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Business Goal Zero Lost Time Accidents CCS 5.0"]
    F1["Function Emergency Shutdown CCS 4.85"]
    F2["Function Process Cooling CCS 4.10"]
    A1["Asset Safety PLC CCS 4.85"]
    A2["Asset Cooling Pump VFD CCS 4.10"]
    A3["Asset SIS Sensor CCS 4.85"]
    N["Asset Network Switch CCS Inherits Max 4.85"]

    P --> F1
    P --> F2
    F1 --> A1
    F1 --> A3
    F2 --> A2
    A1 --> N
    A3 --> N
    A2 --> N

    style P fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F1 fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F2 fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A1 fill:#f57c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A2 fill:#f57c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A3 fill:#f57c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style N fill:#fbc02d,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inheritance Rule (KTU exam favorite):** A child asset's CCS is the **maximum** of (its own calculated CCS) and (the maximum CCS of any function/process it supports). The network switch above therefore inherits CCS = 4.85 because it carries SIS traffic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> The most common mistakes students make on this topic in the KTU ESE are:
> 1. **Reversing the CIA priority** — writing C > I > A for OT. Always remember **A > I > C** in OT.
> 2. **Forgetting the weight normalization constraint** $w_S + w_E + w_F + w_O = 1$. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Treating all four impact dimensions as equal weight** — this violates the safety-first ethical principle and loses 1–2 marks.
