Continuous, not linear. - **Zone-Conduit Model:** IEC 62443's core architecture — group assets into zones, secure the conduits between them. - **Five-Layer Purdue Model:** L0 (Field) → L1 (Control) → L2 (Supervisory/OT) → L3.5 (IDMZ) → L4/5 (IT Enterprise). - **Key Formulas (must memorize):** * $R = L \times I$ (inherent risk) * $R_{residual} = R_{inherent} \times (1 - E_{control})$ * $CC = (C_{impl}/C_{req}) \times 100\%$ * $PCR = (P_{applied}/P_{due}) \times 100\%$ * $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) \times 100\%$ (target **≥ 99.9%**) - **Frameworks — know the differences cold:** IEC 62443 (OT-specific), NIST CSF (voluntary, broad), NERC CIP (mandatory, N. American grid), ISO 27001 (generic ISMS, 27019 for energy). - **Audit Finding SLAs:** Critical = 24h, Major = 30d, Minor = 90d. - **72-Hour Rule:** NIS2 and GDPR both require incident notification within **72 hours**. - **Evidence Retention:** Typically **3 years** minimum; 7 years for financial/regulated industries. - **ALARP Principle:** As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the threshold for acceptable residual risk. - **Common Pitfalls to Avoid in Exams:** 1. Confusing IEC 62443-3-3 (system) with -4-2 (component). 2. Forgetting to state the framework clause number in comparison questions. 3. Skipping formula substitution steps in numerical answers. 4. Writing compliance as a "one-time project" — it is a lifecycle. 5. Ignoring the **Safety triad** in OT compliance questions. > **End of Module 2 Notes — Compliance Considerations in OT-IT Integration** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | PECCT634 | OT Threat Prevention*

topics.title: Standards and best practices (e.g., NIST, IEC 62443)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standards and best practices (e.g., NIST, IEC 62443)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OT Security Standards** are formally documented, consensus-driven frameworks, guidelines, and technical specifications that prescribe the policies, procedures, and controls required to protect Operational Technology (OT) and Industrial Automation and Control Systems (IACS) from cyber threats. They provide a common language and structured methodology for identifying, mitigating, and managing cyber risk across the entire industrial lifecycle (design → deploy → operate → decommission).

In the context of KTU's PECCT634 syllabus, the two most authoritative bodies are:

1. **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology, U.S. Department of Commerce)** — produces the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)**, **NIST SP 800-82** (Guide to ICS Security), and the **NIST SP 800-53** control catalog.
2. **IEC 62443** (also published as **ISA/IEC 62443**) — the *umbrella* international standard for **Industrial Automation and Control Systems (IACS)** security, jointly maintained by the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) and the International Society of Automation (ISA).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Best Practices" accompany Standards:** Standards are *prescriptive* ("you *shall* do X"), while best practices are *descriptive* (proven methods that work in the field). Together, they bridge regulatory compliance and operational reality.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Safety Code"

Imagine an **Operating Theatre (OT) in a hospital**:
- A **Standard** is like a **National Building Code** — it dictates minimum wiring, fire exits, sterilization protocols, and emergency procedures.
- A **Best Practice** is the *senior surgeon's checklist* refined over decades — like "scrub hands for 40 seconds, not 20."
- An **Audit** is when the government inspector walks through with a clipboard.

In **OT cybersecurity**, the "hospital" is a power plant, refinery, or water treatment facility. The "patients" are physical processes (turbines, valves, PLCs). The "surgeons" are control engineers. Standards like **IEC 62443** ensure that every PLC, HMI, and engineering workstation follows a common safety & security protocol — much like every hospital follows WHO hygiene standards.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Vocabulary You MUST know for KTU Exam:**
>
> | Term | Definition |
> |------|------------|
> | **IACS** | Industrial Automation and Control Systems — the full stack of sensors, PLCs, SCADA, DCS used in OT |
> | **SL (Security Level)** | IEC 62443's metric for *how strong* a security control must be (SL 1 → SL 4) |
> | **Zone** | A grouping of physical and logical assets sharing common security requirements |
> | **Conduit** | The communication path *between* zones (the "pipes" through which data flows) |
> | **CSMS** | Cyber Security Management System (IEC 62443-2-1) |
> | **CSF** | Cybersecurity Framework (NIST) |
> | **SP** | Special Publication (NIST document series) |
> | **Purdue Model** | Reference architecture (Levels 0–5) for industrial networks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NIST Ecosystem — A Three-Pillar Architecture

NIST's contribution to OT security is organized into **three complementary documents**:

### Pillar 1: NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) — The "What"
The **NIST CSF** is built on **5 Core Functions** that form the acronym **IDENTIFY–PROTECT–DETECT–RESPOND–RECOVER**:

1. **IDENTIFY** — Asset inventory, business environment, governance, risk assessment.
2. **PROTECT** — Access control, awareness training, data security, protective technology.
3. **DETECT** — Anomalies & events, continuous security monitoring, detection processes.
4. **RESPOND** — Response planning, communications, analysis, mitigation, improvements.
5. **RECOVER** — Recovery planning, improvements, communications.

### Pillar 2: NIST SP 800-82 — The "How" (OT-Specific)
This is the **definitive U.S. guide for ICS/OT security**. It:
- Adapts IT controls for OT environments.
- Explicitly maps the **Purdue Reference Model (Levels 0–5)**.
- Provides overlays for sectors (energy, water, manufacturing, transportation).
- Recommends a **defense-in-depth** architecture with **defense-in-breadth** governance.

### Pillar 3: NIST SP 800-53 — The "Catalog"
A massive catalog of **1000+ security and privacy controls** organized into **20 control families** (e.g., AC = Access Control, AU = Audit and Accountability, SC = System and Communications Protection).

## 2.2 The IEC 62443 Series — The Global Industrial Standard

IEC 62443 is structured in **four horizontal layers** (General, Policies and Procedures, System, Component) and **numbered documents**:

| Document | Scope | Audience |
|----------|-------|----------|
| **IEC 62443-1-1** | Concepts and models | All |
| **IEC 62443-2-1** | CSMS (Cyber Security Management System) | Asset owners / operators |
| **IEC 62443-2-4** | Service provider security | Integrators / vendors |
| **IEC 62443-3-1** | System security technologies & metrics | Engineers |
| **IEC 62443-3-2** | Security risk assessment & system design | Engineers |
| **IEC 62443-3-3** | **System Security Requirements & Security Levels (SLs)** | **High-yield for exams** |
| **IEC 62443-4-1** | Secure product development lifecycle | Vendors |
| **IEC 62443-4-2** | Component security requirements | Vendors |

## 2.3 Security Levels (SL) — The Heart of IEC 62443

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Security Levels (SL 1 – SL 4) — MEMORIZE THIS:**
>
> $$\text{SL} = f(\text{Threat Sophistication}, \text{Motivation}, \text{Resources}, \text{Skill})$$

| Security Level | Description | Threat Actor |
|---------------|-------------|--------------|
| **SL 1** | Casual / coincidental | Script kiddies, accidental misuse |
| **SL 2** | Intentional with simple means / low resources | Insider with basic skills |
| **SL 3** | Intentional with sophisticated means / moderate resources | Hacktivists, cybercriminals |
| **SL 4** | Intentional with sophisticated means / extensive resources | Nation-state APTs |

**Foundational Requirements (FR)** in IEC 62443-3-3:
- **FR 1** — Identification & authentication control
- **FR 2** — Use control
- **FR 3** — System integrity
- **FR 4** — Data confidentiality
- **FR 5** — Restricted data flow
- **FR 6** — Timely response to events
- **FR 7** — Resource availability

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` for absolute value (NOT `|`) to keep tables valid.**

| Concept | Equation / Definition | Variables / Notes |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Target Security Level (SL-T)** | $SL_T = \max(SL_{1}, SL_{2}, SL_{3}, SL_{4})$ per zone | $SL_{i}$ = achieved level on requirement *i* |
| **Risk Score (NIST 800-30)** | $R = L \times I$ | $L$ = Likelihood, $I$ = Impact |
| **Residual Risk** | $R_{residual} = R_{inherent} - R_{controls}$ | After applying controls |
| **Defense-in-Depth Layers** | $N_{layers} = 7$ (typical NIST) | Network, Host, App, Data, Perimeter, Physical, Policies |
| **Zone-Conduit Ratio** | $\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{\text{Assets inside zones}}{\text{Total assets}} \times 100\%$ | Goal: 100% |
| **Maturity Tiers (NIST CSF)** | Tier 1 (Partial) → Tier 4 (Adaptive) | Organizational risk governance |
| **System under Consideration (SuC)** | Set of components fulfilling a function | Subject of SL assignment |
| **Foundational Requirements** | Count = 7 (FR1 to FR7) | IEC 62443-3-3 core |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power grids** → NERC CIP (derived from IEC 62443) for grid reliability.
- **Oil & Gas** → IEC 62443 is the de-facto baseline for refineries & pipelines.
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)** → IEC 62443 zones align with **RAMI 4.0** layers.
- **Water utilities** → AWWA J100 / NIST CSF 2.0 (2024 release) baseline.
- **Compliance** → IEC 62443 certification gives legal/regulatory cover post-incident (e.g., NIS2 Directive in EU).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Determining a Zone's Target Security Level (SL-T)

The process of assigning an SL to a zone follows **IEC 62443-3-2**'s risk-based methodology.

### Step 1 — Identify the System under Consideration (SuC)
A SuC is a discrete operational function (e.g., "Boiler Temperature Control Loop").

$$\text{SuC} = \{\text{Sensors}, \text{Controllers}, \text{Actuators}, \text{HMI}\}$$

### Step 2 — Identify Worst-Case Threat Scenario
Ask: *"What is the most capable adversary that could plausibly target this zone?"*

$$\text{Adversary} = \{ \text{Skill}, \text{Resources}, \text{Motivation}, \text{Opportunity} \}$$

### Step 3 — Map Adversary to Security Level
The mapping rule is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If Adversary is} \rightarrow \text{SL 1} &\Rightarrow \text{Assign } SL_T = 1 \\
\text{If Adversary is} \rightarrow \text{SL 2} &\Rightarrow \text{Assign } SL_T = 2 \\
\text{If Adversary is} \rightarrow \text{SL 3} &\Rightarrow \text{Assign } SL_T = 3 \\
\text{If Adversary is} \rightarrow \text{SL 4} &\Rightarrow \text{Assign } SL_T = 4
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Achieved Security Level (SL-A) per FR
For each Foundational Requirement, determine the *achieved* level by reviewing implemented controls:

$$SL_A(FR_i) = \min(\text{Implemented Control Strength}, SL_T)$$

### Step 5 — Compare Achieved vs Target
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } SL_A(FR_i) &\geq SL_T \quad \Rightarrow \text{COMPLIANT} \\
\text{If } SL_A(FR_i) &< SL_T \quad \Rightarrow \text{NON-COMPLIANT} \rightarrow \text{Mitigate}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Compute Compliance Percentage
$$\text{Compliance \%} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{7} \mathbb{1}\big( SL_A(FR_i) \geq SL_T \big)}{7} \times 100\%$$

where $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function.

**Example Worked Derivation:**

> Suppose a **Steam Turbine Control** zone is assigned $SL_T = 3$ (sophisticated cybercriminal threat).
>
> Reviewing controls:
>
> | FR | SL-A |
> |----|------|
> | FR1 (Identification) | 3 |
> | FR2 (Use Control) | 2 |
> | FR3 (System Integrity) | 3 |
> | FR4 (Data Confidentiality) | 3 |
> | FR5 (Restricted Data Flow) | 1 |
> | FR6 (Timely Response) | 2 |
> | FR7 (Resource Availability) | 3 |
>
> $$\text{Compliance \%} = \frac{5}{7} \times 100\% \approx 71.4\%$$
>
> **Conclusion:** FR2, FR5, FR6 are non-compliant → add **role-based access control**, **network segmentation**, and **SIEM integration**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Standards to OT Lifecycle

| Lifecycle Phase | NIST CSF Function | IEC 62443 Document | Key Activity |
|-----------------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Design** | IDENTIFY | 62443-3-2 | Risk assessment & SL-T assignment |
| **Build** | PROTECT | 62443-4-1 / 4-2 | Secure development, component cert |
| **Integrate** | PROTECT | 62443-2-4 | Service provider requirements |
| **Operate** | DETECT/RESPOND | 62443-3-3 | SL monitoring, patching |
| **Audit** | All | 62443-2-1 | CSMS audits |
| **Recover** | RECOVER | 62443-3-3 FR7 | BCP/DR for OT |

## 3.3 Zone-Conduit Construction Algorithm

1. **Inventory** all assets (NIST CSF ID.AM).
2. **Group** by function → define **Zones** $Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_n$.
3. **Identify** all inter-zone communication paths → define **Conduits** $C_{ij}$ between $Z_i$ and $Z_j$.
4. **Assign** each conduit an **SL-T** equal to the higher of the two adjacent zones:
$$SL_T(C_{ij}) = \max\big(SL_T(Z_i),\; SL_T(Z_j)\big)$$
5. **Enforce** controls at each conduit (firewall, ACL, data diode).
6. **Verify** via penetration testing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NIST CSF — Five Core Functions (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["IDENTIFY\nAsset, Risk, Governance"] --> node2["PROTECT\nAccess, Training, Crypto"]
    node2 --> node3["DETECT\nAnomalies, SIEM, Logs"]
    node3 --> node4["RESPOND\nIR Plan, Mitigation"]
    node4 --> node5["RECOVER\nBCP, Restoration"]
    node5 -.feedback.-> node1
    classDef core fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffd700,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    class node1,node2,node3,node4,node5 core
```

## 4.2 IEC 62443 Zone & Conduit Model (Plant Network)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph corp["CORPORATE IT NETWORK - Purdue Level 5"]
        erp["ERP / Email Server"]
    end
    subgraph demil["DMZ - Purdue Level 4"]
        jump["Jump Server\nIndustrial Firewall"]
    end
    subgraph scada["SCADA ZONE - Purdue Level 3"]
        hmi["HMI Workstations\nSL Target 2"]
        hist["Historian Server\nSL Target 2"]
    end
    subgraph ctrl["CONTROL ZONE - Purdue Level 2"]
        dcs["DCS Controllers\nSL Target 3"]
        plc["Safety PLC\nSL Target 4"]
    end
    subgraph field["FIELD ZONE - Purdue Level 0 1"]
        sensor["Sensors and Actuators\nSL Target 2"]
    end
    erp ===|Conduit C15 SL 2| jump
    jump ===|Conduit C34 SL 2| hmi
    jump ===|Conduit C35 SL 2| hist
    hmi ===|Conduit C23 SL 3| dcs
    dcs ===|Conduit C24 SL 4| plc
    dcs ===|Conduit C12 SL 2| sensor
    classDef zone fill:#274d8a,stroke:#ffd700,color:#fff
    classDef cond fill:#5a1a1a,stroke:#ff5050,color:#fff
    class erp,jump,hmi,hist,dcs,plc,sensor zone
```

## 4.3 Standards Family Relationship Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA["OT SECURITY STANDARDS"] --> nist["NIST FAMILY"]
    rootA --> iec["IEC 62443 FAMILY"]
    rootA --> iso["ISO 27001 27019"]
    rootA --> nerc["NERC CIP"]
    nist --> csf["CSF Core\nIdentify Protect Detect Respond Recover"]
    nist --> sp82["SP 800 82\nICS Implementation Guide"]
    nist --> sp53["SP 800 53\nControl Catalog"]
    iec --> gen["1 1 General Concepts"]
    iec --> pol["2 1 CSMS\n2 4 Service Providers"]
    iec --> sys["3 1 Technologies\n3 2 Risk\n3 3 SLs and FRs"]
    iec --> com["4 1 SDLC\n4 2 Components"]
    classDef top fill:#003366,stroke:#ffd700,color:#fff
    classDef branch fill:#4a6fa5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class rootA top
    class nist,iec,iso,nerc branch
```

## 4.4 Risk-Based Control Selection Flow (NIST 800-30 + 800-53)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Asset Identified"] --> nodeB["Threat Catalogued"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Vulnerability Matched"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Likelihood and Impact Scored"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Risk = L x I Computed"]
    nodeE -->|"R is High"| nodeF["Apply NIST 800 53 High Baseline"]
    nodeE -->|"R is Moderate"| nodeG["Apply Moderate Baseline"]
    nodeE -->|"R is Low"| nodeH["Apply Low Baseline"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Document Residual Risk"]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    classDef hi fill:#8b0000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef md fill:#b8860b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef lo fill:#006400,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class nodeF hi
    class nodeG md
    class nodeH lo
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Distribution Pattern (Module 2 — Threat Prevention Standards):**
> - Part A: 2 × 3 = 6 marks (definition/conceptual)
> - Part B: 1 × 14 marks (full descriptive answer)

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**Define IEC 62443. List any three documents from the IEC 62443 series and state their purpose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
IEC 62443 is an international, multi-part standard developed by the IEC and ISA that defines requirements and processes for implementing and maintaining secure Industrial Automation and Control Systems (IACS). It is the globally recognized cybersecurity baseline for OT environments.

**Three documents (2 marks — ½ mark each for name, ½ mark for purpose):**

1. **IEC 62443-3-3** — Defines System Security Requirements and **Security Levels (SL 1–SL 4)** for IACS components and systems.
2. **IEC 62443-2-1** — Specifies the requirements for establishing, operating, and maintaining a **Cyber Security Management System (CSMS)** for asset owners.
3. **IEC 62443-4-1** — Defines process requirements for the **secure development lifecycle (SDL)** of IACS products.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**What is the NIST Cybersecurity Framework? Name its five core functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) is a voluntary, risk-based framework published by the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology that provides a policy framework of computer security guidance for organizations to assess and improve their ability to prevent, detect, and respond to cyberattacks. It is widely adapted for OT environments.

**Five Core Functions (2 marks — 2/5 each):**
1. **IDENTIFY** — Develop an organizational understanding to manage cybersecurity risk to systems, people, assets, data, and capabilities.
2. **PROTECT** — Develop and implement appropriate safeguards to ensure delivery of critical services.
3. **DETECT** — Define and implement activities to identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event.
4. **RESPOND** — Define and implement activities to take action regarding a detected cybersecurity incident.
5. **RECOVER** — Define and implement activities to maintain plans for resilience and restore capabilities or services impaired due to a cybersecurity incident.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often *list* standards but fail to **map** them to OT scenarios → expect 0 marks for "narrative" answers.
> - Always cite **document numbers** (e.g., "IEC 62443-3-3") — generic "IEC 62443" answers lose 1–2 marks.
> - In SL questions, students forget the **achieved vs target** distinction. Always compute $SL_A$ vs $SL_T$.
> - Use **block diagrams / mermaid-style sketches** wherever possible — they earn 1–2 extra "presentation" marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)** in detail. Discuss how the five core functions (**Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover**) are applied to secure an **OT / SCADA environment** in a power plant. Cite relevant **NIST SP 800-82** recommendations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Zone and Conduit model** of **IEC 62443-3-3**. For a refinery with three zones (Corporate, Control, Field), assign appropriate **Target Security Levels (SL-T)** and justify your assignment based on threat modelling. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer A:

**(a) NIST CSF for OT Power Plant (7 marks):**

The **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)** is a tiered, risk-based structure with three components: the **Framework Core**, **Implementation Tiers**, and **Framework Profile**.

**Architecture (2 marks):**
- **Framework Core** → Industry-standard cybersecurity activities organized into 5 functions, 23 categories, and 108 subcategories.
- **Implementation Tiers** → Describe the degree to which an organization's cybersecurity risk management practices exhibit the characteristics defined in the framework (Tier 1: Partial → Tier 4: Adaptive).
- **Framework Profile** → Alignment of Core with business requirements, risk tolerance, and resources.

**Application to Power Plant (5 marks):**

| Function | Power Plant OT Application | NIST SP 800-82 Reference |
|----------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| **IDENTIFY** (1 m) | Asset inventory of generators, transformers, IEDs, RTUs; map to **Purdue Model Levels 0–3**; perform cyber risk assessment per NIST SP 800-30 | Section 5 ("ICS Cybersecurity Program Development") |
| **PROTECT** (1 m) | Role-based access control on HMIs; network segmentation via industrial firewalls; multi-factor authentication for vendor remote access | Section 6.2 ("Network Segmentation") |
| **DETECT** (1 m) | Deploy OT-aware IDS (e.g., passive DPI for Modbus/DNP3); SIEM correlation; continuous monitoring of cyber-physical anomalies (e.g., frequency, voltage deviations) | Section 6.3 ("ICS Threat Detection") |
| **RESPOND** (1 m) | OT-specific Incident Response Plan (IRP); coordination with engineering & operations; preservation of process state for forensics | Section 7 ("Incident Response") |
| **RECOVER** (1 m) | Manual fallback procedures; validated backups of engineering configurations; black-start coordination | Section 8 ("Recovery and Restoration") |

**(b) Zone & Conduit Model for a Refinery (7 marks):**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
   ┌──────────────────────────┐
   │ ZONE A: CORPORATE IT     │  SL-T = 1
   │ (ERP, Email, Patches)    │
   └──────────┬───────────────┘
              │ Conduit C1 (SL-T = 2, Industrial Firewall + DMZ)
   ┌──────────▼───────────────┐
   │ ZONE B: PROCESS CONTROL  │  SL-T = 3
   │ (DCS, HMI, Historian)    │
   └──────────┬───────────────┘
              │ Conduit C2 (SL-T = 3, ACL + Uni-directional Gateway)
   ┌──────────▼───────────────┐
   │ ZONE C: FIELD            │  SL-T = 2
   │ (PLCs, RTUs, Sensors)    │
   └──────────────────────────┘
```

**Justification (4 marks — 1 m per zone + 1 m for conduit logic):**

- **Zone A (Corporate) — SL-T = 1:** Office workloads face only casual / non-targeted threats. As per IEC 62443-3-3, SL 1 = "casual / coincidental violation."
- **Zone B (Control) — SL-T = 3:** Sophisticated attackers (e.g., hacktivists targeting plant safety) with moderate resources, motivated by economic or reputational damage. Aligns with SL 3 definition.
- **Zone C (Field) — SL-T = 2:** Devices are physically isolated, simple hardware; threat limited to intentional violation with simple means (insider / careless contractor). Hence SL 2.
- **Conduit C1:** $SL_T = \max(1,3) = 3$, **demilitarized zone** with strict firewall rules.
- **Conduit C2:** $SL_T = \max(3,2) = 3$, **unidirectional gateway** (data diode) to prevent lateral movement from control to field compromise.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Compare **NIST SP 800-82** and **IEC 62443** with respect to: (i) origin and scope, (ii) structure and methodology, (iii) how each addresses OT-specific concerns such as legacy systems, safety, and real-time constraints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Security Levels (SL 1, SL 2, SL 3, SL 4)** in IEC 62443-3-3 with suitable examples. For a **Boiler Control System** in a thermal power plant, perform a step-by-step **risk assessment** to determine the appropriate $SL_T$ and evaluate whether the existing controls meet the requirement. Use the **Foundational Requirements (FR 1–FR 7)** in your analysis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer B:

**(a) Comparison of NIST SP 800-82 vs IEC 62443 (7 marks):**

| Dimension (mark weight) | **NIST SP 800-82** | **IEC 62443** |
|--------------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| **Origin & Scope** (2 m) | Published by **U.S. NIST**; *guidance document* (not a certification). Maps to FISMA, used globally. Scope: ICS/SCADA/DCS security. | Published jointly by **IEC & ISA**; *internationally ratified standard* with **certification schemes** (e.g., ISASecure). Scope: full IACS lifecycle. |
| **Structure & Methodology** (2 m) | Organized by **Purdue levels + risk management steps**; recommends a layered "defense-in-depth" model; cross-references SP 800-53 controls. | Multi-part series; structured as **General → Policies → System → Component**; introduces **Security Levels (SL 1–4)** and **Foundational Requirements (FR 1–7)**. |
| **OT-Specific Concerns** (3 m) | • **Legacy systems** — recommends compensating controls, isolation.<br>• **Safety** — integrates with IEC 61511 SIS.<br>• **Real-time** — emphasizes passive monitoring over active scanning. | • **Legacy systems** — assigns SL on a *per zone* basis, allowing older devices to live in lower-SL zones.<br>• **Safety** — explicit mapping to **IEC 61508/61511** functional safety.<br>• **Real-time** — defines **SL-T** thresholds so security controls do not exceed process timing budgets. |

**Conclusion:** NIST SP 800-82 is *prescriptive guidance* that organizations *follow*; IEC 62443 is a *standard* that organizations *certify against*. In practice, mature OT programs use **both** — IEC 62443 for compliance, NIST for risk-management practices.

**(b) Security Levels and Boiler Control Risk Assessment (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks — ½ m per SL):**
- **SL 1** — Protection against *casual / coincidental* violation (e.g., accidental misuse by an employee).
- **SL 2** — Protection against *intentional violation with simple means, low resources, and low motivation* (e.g., disgruntled insider with USB stick).
- **SL 3** — Protection against *intentional violation with sophisticated means, moderate resources, and high motivation* (e.g., hacktivist with custom malware).
- **SL 4** — Protection against *intentional violation with sophisticated means, extensive resources, and high motivation* (e.g., nation-state APT).

**Step-by-step Risk Assessment for Boiler Control (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Asset & SuC Definition (1 m):**
The SuC is the **Boiler Combustion & Steam-Temperature Control Loop** comprising:
- Sensors: Drum level, fuel flow, O₂ analyzer
- Controller: DCS controller (e.g., ABB 800xA)
- Actuators: Fuel valves, FD/ID fan VFDs

**Step 2 — Worst-Case Adversary (1 m):**
A hacktivist group targeting the plant for reputational damage → **maps to SL 3** (sophisticated means, moderate resources).
Hence, $\boxed{SL_T = 3}$.

**Step 3 — FR-by-FR Evaluation (2 m):**

| FR | Existing Control | SL-A | SL-T | Compliant? |
|----|------------------|------|------|------------|
| FR1 (Identification) | Username + password | 2 | 3 | ❌ |
| FR2 (Use Control) | Manual logout policy | 2 | 3 | ❌ |
| FR3 (System Integrity) | Manual config backup | 2 | 3 | ❌ |
| FR4 (Confidentiality) | TLS for engineering link | 3 | 3 | ✅ |
| FR5 (Restricted Data Flow) | VLAN segmentation | 3 | 3 | ✅ |
| FR6 (Timely Response) | No OT-specific SIEM | 1 | 3 | ❌ |
| FR7 (Resource Availability) | Redundant controllers | 3 | 3 | ✅ |

$$\text{Compliance \%} = \frac{4}{7} \times 100\% \approx 57.1\%$$

**Step 4 — Recommendations (1 m):**
1. Deploy **multi-factor authentication + role-based access** → raise FR1, FR2 to SL 3.
2. Implement **configuration integrity monitoring (tripwire)** → raise FR3 to SL 3.
3. Integrate **OT-aware SIEM (e.g., Dragos, Nozomi)** → raise FR6 to SL 3.

After remediation, **target compliance = 100%**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Standards & Best Practices — High-Yield Rapid Recap:**
>
> ✅ **NIST CSF = 5 Functions:** Identify → Protect → Detect → Respond → Recover (mnemonic: **"I Prefer Delicious Rasagullas"** or simply **IPDRR**).
>
> ✅ **NIST SP 800-82** is the *OT-specific* implementation guide; maps controls to the **Purdue Model (Levels 0–5)**.
>
> ✅ **NIST SP 800-53** is the universal *control catalog* (1000+ controls, 20 families).
>
> ✅ **IEC 62443** is the **global industrial cybersecurity standard** — multi-part series covering General, Policies, Systems, and Components.
>
> ✅ **Foundational Requirements (FR 1–7)** are mandatory categories in IEC 62443-3-3 — **Identification, Use Control, Integrity, Confidentiality, Restricted Flow, Timely Response, Availability**.
>
> ✅ **Security Levels SL 1 → SL 4** represent adversary capability — Casual → Intentional Simple → Intentional Sophisticated → Nation-State.
>
> ✅ **Zone = grouping of assets** with common security requirements. **Conduit = communication path between zones** with $SL_T(C_{ij}) = \max(SL_T(Z_i), SL_T(Z_j))$.
>
> ✅ **Target SL ($SL_T$) vs Achieved SL ($SL_A$):** $SL_A \geq SL_T$ per FR for compliance.
>
> ✅ **Defense-in-Depth** = multiple overlapping security layers (network, host, application, data, physical, policies).
>
> ✅ **Best Practices (not Standards):** CIS Controls, ISA Security Compliance Institute (ISCI) certifications, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS.
>
> ✅ **Purdue Model mapping for KTU:** L0 (Process) → L1 (Basic Control) → L2 (Supervisory) → L3 (Site Operations) → L3.5 (DMZ) → L4 (Enterprise) → L5 (External).
>
> ✅ **Risk Formula (NIST 800-30):** $R = L \times I$; **Residual Risk:** $R_{residual} = R_{inherent} - R_{controls}$.
>
> ✅ **Compliance Formula:** $\text{Compliance \%} = \dfrac{\text{Met FRs}}{7} \times 100\%$.
>
> ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always *cite document numbers* (e.g., 62443-3-3, 800-82 r3) and *use a diagram* to earn presentation marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Securing Wired and Wireless Access: Best practices for securing wired access in OT-IT networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Securing Wired and Wireless Access: Best practices for securing wired access in OT-IT networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Secured Wired Access in OT-IT Networks** refers to the comprehensive set of physical, logical, and administrative controls implemented to protect Ethernet-based communication infrastructure that interconnects Operational Technology (OT) systems with Information Technology (IT) systems. According to the **ISA/IEC 62443** (Industrial Automation and Control Systems Security) and **NIST SP 800-82r3** frameworks, wired access security in OT-IT converged environments encompasses the hardening of physical cabling, switches, routers, and endpoints to prevent unauthorized access, lateral movement, eavesdropping, injection attacks, and Denial-of-Service (DoS) conditions that could compromise safety, availability, and integrity of industrial processes.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, the topic aligns with the convergence of Purdue Model Levels 0–3 (OT side) and Levels 3.5–5 (IT side), where wired Ethernet remains the dominant Layer 1/2 transport technology. Securing this access means ensuring that only authenticated, authorized, and policy-compliant devices can plug into, transmit across, or receive data from the OT-IT converged network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (Syllabus Highlight):**
> "Securing wired access in OT-IT networks is the disciplined application of physical, data-link, and network-layer controls to ensure that every copper/fiber port, switch, and cable segment connecting IT infrastructure to OT assets enforces authentication, integrity, confidentiality, and availability as dictated by the organization's zoning and conduit policies."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large industrial manufacturing plant with multiple restricted zones — a chemical mixing room, a packaging hall, and a server room. Each zone has locked doors with badge readers. Visitors cannot simply walk in; they must authenticate, their access is logged, and certain rooms (like the chemical mixing room) require special clearance. Inside, CCTV cameras monitor who goes where, and every door is reinforced against tampering.

**Now translate this analogy to OT-IT wired networks:**

- **Locked doors with badge readers** → **802.1X port-based authentication** (only authorized devices get network access when plugged in)
- **CCTV cameras** → **Network monitoring, port mirroring (SPAN), and SIEM logging**
- **Reinforced doors** → **Physical port locks, disabled unused switch ports, tamper-evident cabinets**
- **Special clearance rooms** → **VLAN segmentation isolating OT zones from IT zones**
- **Visitor log** → **MAC address whitelisting and asset inventory databases**
- **Intercom between zones** → **Controlled conduits (firewall/DPI rules) between IT and OT networks**

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Wired access security is not just about a password on a switch — it is a *layered defense system* that begins with the physical cable in the conduit and ends with the cryptographic identity of the device transmitting bits on the wire. If any single layer is weak, an attacker can pivot from IT to OT (or vice versa) with potentially catastrophic physical-world consequences.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following metrics are universally used in securing wired access in industrial environments:

- **Port Density** — typically **24 or 48 ports per managed switch** in OT cabinets
- **802.1X Reauthentication Timer** — recommended **30 to 60 minutes** in OT zones
- **MAC Aging Time** — default **300 seconds**; OT zones often extend to **600–1800 seconds** for stability
- **Maximum Cable Length (Copper Ethernet)** — **100 meters** (IEEE 802.3 standard for Cat5e/Cat6)
- **VLAN ID Range** — **1 to 4094** (12-bit VID field per IEEE 802.1Q)
- **PoE Budget** — typically **15.4 W (PoE), 30 W (PoE+), 60–90 W (PoE++/Type 3/4)** per IEEE 802.3af/at/bt
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** target for OT switches — **< 4 hours** for safety-critical zones
- **Defense-in-Depth Layers (per NIST)** — typically **5 to 7 layers**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Wired Access Security in OT-IT Networks

Securing wired access is best understood as a **defense-in-depth** strategy built on five interdependent pillars. Each pillar addresses a different attack surface and complements the others — removing any one pillar significantly weakens the entire posture.

#### Pillar 1 — Physical Layer Security
- **Locked enclosures and cabinets** for all patch panels, switches, and junction boxes
- **Conduit and cable tray hardening** to prevent tapping or cutting
- **Tamper-evident seals** on unused ports and panel covers
- **Electromagnetic (EM) shielding** for cables in high-EMI industrial environments (e.g., near VFDs, motors, arc welders)
- **Surge protection and proper grounding** to prevent damage-induced faults that could be exploited

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** The most elegant 802.1X configuration is useless if an attacker simply unplugs a PLC and plugs in a rogue laptop. Physical access is root access.

#### Pillar 2 — Port-Level Controls (Data Link Layer)
- **802.1X Port-Based Network Access Control (PNAC)** — requires a supplicant (device), authenticator (switch), and authentication server (RADIUS/TACACS+)
- **MAC Address Filtering / Sticky MAC / MAC Authentication Bypass (MAB)** — fallback or primary authentication method
- **Port Security (Cisco-proprietary baseline, now standardized)** — limits maximum MAC addresses per port; can be `static`, `sticky`, or `dynamic`
- **BPDU Guard and Root Bridge Protection** — prevents rogue switches from disrupting Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)
- **Storm Control** — throttles broadcast, multicast, and unknown-unicast floods
- **DHCP Snooping** — prevents rogue DHCP servers from handing out malicious IP configurations
- **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** — validates ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table
- **IPv6 RA Guard / ND Inspection** — protects against rogue IPv6 router advertisements

#### Pillar 3 — Network Segmentation (Layer 2/3)
- **VLANs (Virtual LANs)** per IEEE 802.1Q — separate OT zones (e.g., Level 1 controllers, Level 2 SCADA, Level 3 manufacturing DMZ)
- **Private VLANs (PVLAN)** — isolate devices within the same subnet (e.g., all PLCs on VLAN 100 cannot talk to each other, only to the engineering station)
- **VRF (Virtual Routing and Forwarding)** — Layer 3 isolation within a single physical router
- **Firewalls between zones** — stateful inspection, application-layer gateways (e.g., Modbus/DNP3 deep packet inspection)
- **Unidirectional Gateways (Data Diodes)** — physically enforce one-way data flow from OT to IT, preventing inbound attacks

#### Pillar 4 — Identity, Authentication, and Authorization
- **RADIUS (RFC 2865)** — primary AAA protocol for OT-IT wired authentication
- **TACACS+ (Cisco proprietary, RFC 1492 bis)** — preferred for device administration (command-level authorization)
- **LDAP/Active Directory integration** — central identity store; commonly synchronized with OT IAM
- **Certificate-based authentication (802.1X-EAP-TLS)** — strongest, uses X.509 PKI; recommended for critical OT assets
- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** — users get only the permissions required for their role (e.g., operator vs. engineer vs. contractor)

#### Pillar 5 — Monitoring, Logging, and Response
- **SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) / RSPAN / ERSPAN** — mirror traffic for IDS/IPS sensors
- **NetFlow / sFlow / IPFIX** — telemetry for anomaly detection
- **Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS)** — signature-based (Snort, Suricata) and behavior-based
- **SIEM integration** — correlates switch logs, authentication logs, and flow records
- **Asset discovery and inventory** — continuous validation that only known devices exist on the network

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula or Rule | Unit / Value | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum devices per secure port | $N_{mac} = N_{auth} + N_{voice} + N_{mgmt}$ | Count (typically $\le 2$ for OT) | Port-security limit |
| 802.1X EAP-TLS key length | $L_{key} \ge 2048$ (RSA) or $L_{key} = 256$ (ECC) | bits | Certificate strength for OT supplicants |
| 802.1X reauthentication interval | $T_{reauth} = 1800 \text{ s}$ (30 min) – $3600 \text{ s}$ (60 min) | seconds | OT recommended range |
| Maximum cable reach (copper) | $L_{cable} \le 100$ | meters | IEEE 802.3u/ab Cat5e/Cat6 |
| Maximum cable reach (single-mode fiber) | $L_{fiber} \le 40{,}000$ | meters | OT long-haul backbones |
| VLAN ID space | $VID \in [1, 4094]$ | integer | IEEE 802.1Q 12-bit tag |
| MAC aging time (OT) | $T_{age} = 600 \text{ s} - 1800 \text{ s}$ | seconds | Stability for legacy OT devices |
| Broadcast storm threshold | $B_{thresh} = pps_{limit}$ | packets/sec | Storm-control upper bound |
| PoE power budget (Type 2) | $P_{PoE+} = 30$ | Watts per port | IP cameras, wireless APs |
| Spanning Tree port cost (1 Gbps) | $C_{stp} = 4$ | cost units | STP path selection |
| MTTR target (safety-critical) | $MTTR \le 4$ | hours | OT SLA |
| Defense-in-Depth layers | $L_{did} \in [5, 7]$ | layers | Per NIST CSF / IEC 62443 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These parameters appear in KTU exam questions on switch configuration, network design, and incident response scenarios. Mastering the table above allows a student to solve both 3-mark and 14-mark questions on securing wired access with confidence.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Port Security Violation Decision Logic

The IEEE 802.1X state machine, combined with port security, follows a deterministic decision flow when a new device attempts to gain wired access. The transition logic can be expressed as a state-based derivation.

Let:
- $S$ = current state of the port (AUTHORIZED, UNAUTHORIZED, RESTRICTED)
- $D_{mac}$ = MAC address of the detected device
- $T_{auth}$ = authentication result (TRUE if EAP success, FALSE otherwise)
- $L_{port}$ = learned MAC table for the port
- $N_{max}$ = maximum allowed MAC addresses per port

**Step 1 — Initial State**

When a port is administratively enabled, the initial state is:

$$S_0 = \text{UNAUTHORIZED}$$

This is the secure default. No traffic except EAPOL (Extensible Authentication Protocol over LAN) frames is allowed to pass.

**Step 2 — Device Attachment Detection**

When the switch detects a new frame source MAC address, it triggers the learning process:

$$D_{mac} \rightarrow L_{port}$$

The port checks whether the count of learned MACs exceeds the configured maximum:

$$N_{learned} = \vert L_{port} \vert$$

**Step 3 — Authentication Trigger**

If 802.1X is enabled, the switch (authenticator) sends an EAP-Request/Identity:

$$S_0 \xrightarrow{\text{EAP-Request/Identity}} S_1$$

The supplicant responds with EAP-Response/Identity containing its username or certificate.

**Step 4 — Authentication Server Validation**

The authenticator forwards the EAP exchange to the RADIUS server:

$$\text{Auth} = f(\text{EAP-Response}, \text{Credential}_{\text{DB}})$$

Where:
- $f(\cdot)$ is the AAA verification function
- $\text{Credential}_{\text{DB}}$ is the central identity store

**Step 5 — Authorization Decision**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } T_{auth} &= \text{TRUE} \Rightarrow S = \text{AUTHORIZED} \\
\text{If } T_{auth} &= \text{FALSE} \Rightarrow S = \text{UNAUTHORIZED}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Port Security Violation Check**

If port security is also enabled, the following rule applies:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } N_{learned} > N_{max} &\Rightarrow \text{Violation} = \text{TRIGGERED} \\
\text{Violation Mode} &\in \{\text{Protect}, \text{Restrict}, \text{Shutdown}\}
\end{aligned}$$

- **Protect** — drops violating frames silently (least disruptive)
- **Restrict** — drops frames and generates SNMP trap + log
- **Shutdown** — err-disables the port (most secure, used in OT)

**Step 7 — Reauthentication Timer**

After authorization, a timer begins:

$$T_{reauth} = T_{start} + \Delta t \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta t \in [1800, 3600] \text{ s}$$

Upon expiration, the port re-enters the authentication cycle, preventing session hijacking via MAC spoofing.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Wired Access Control

The following matrix maps common OT-IT scenarios to the most appropriate security control combinations. This is a high-yield framework for KTU exam answers.

| OT-IT Scenario | Primary Control | Secondary Control | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineer laptop docking at OT cabinet | 802.1X EAP-TLS | Port security $N_{max} = 1$ | Strong device identity, no rogue MACs |
| Legacy PLC (no 802.1X support) | MAC Authentication Bypass (MAB) | PVLAN isolation | Legacy devices still get least-privilege |
| Contractor temporary access | 802.1X EAP-PEAP + RBAC | Time-bound VLAN ACL | Limits access window and scope |
| Safety-critical zone (SIS) | Data diode + dedicated VLAN | Physically isolated cabling | Absolute one-way enforcement |
| Inter-zone IT-to-OT link | Industrial firewall with DPI | MACsec (802.1AE) encryption | Confidentiality + protocol filtering |
| Wireless bridge backhauled via wired AP port | 802.1X + PoE budgeting | SPAN to WIDS sensor | Prevents AP compromise pivoting |
| Engineering workstation jump box | RBAC + 802.1X + MFA | Session recording | Auditable privileged access |
| Remote vendor support | Jump server + TACACS+ command auth | Out-of-band VPN | Vendor never touches OT directly |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — VLAN Capacity Planning

A manufacturing plant has the following zones to be separated using VLANs on the OT-IT backbone:
- 4 production lines (PLCs + HMIs)
- 1 SCADA server farm
- 1 engineering workstation pool
- 1 manufacturing DMZ (historian, MES)
- 1 IT corporate network trunk
- 1 safety-instrumented system (SIS) zone

**Step 1 — Count required VLANs**

$$N_{VLAN} = 4 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 9 \text{ VLANs}$$

**Step 2 — Check available space**

The IEEE 802.1Q standard provides:

$$N_{max} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ VLANs} \quad (\text{usable} \in [1, 4094])$$

**Step 3 — Compute utilization ratio**

$$U = \frac{N_{VLAN}}{N_{max}} = \frac{9}{4094} \approx 0.0022 = 0.22\%$$

**Step 4 — Reserve space for growth and management**

Reserve 10% for management, voice, and future expansion:

$$N_{reserved} = 0.10 \times 4094 \approx 409 \text{ VLANs}$$

**Conclusion:** The design has ample headroom. The student should highlight in their KTU answer that *sufficient VLAN capacity is rarely the constraint — design discipline, naming conventions, and trunk configuration are.*

### 3.4 Step-by-Step MACsec (802.1AE) Encryption Strength Derivation

When MACsec is deployed on OT-IT trunk links, the encryption key lifecycle is governed by:

$$K_{session} = \text{KDF}(K_{CAK}, \text{Nonce}, \text{SCI})$$

Where:
- $K_{CAK}$ = Connectivity Association Key (pre-shared or derived from 802.1X)
- $\text{Nonce}$ = fresh random number per packet (or replay-window worth)
- $\text{SCI}$ = Secure Channel Identifier (transmitter identity)
- $\text{KDF}(\cdot)$ = Key Derivation Function (AES-CMAC-128 by default)

The integrity check value (ICV) for each frame is computed as:

$$ICV = \text{AES-128-CMAC}(K_{session}, \text{Frame} \mid \text{SCI} \mid \text{PN})$$

Where $\text{PN}$ is the packet number used for replay protection. This guarantees confidentiality, integrity, and anti-replay for every Ethernet frame on the wired link.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Purdue Model with Secured Wired Access Zones

```mermaid
graph TB

    MON[SIEM SPAN TAP]:::sec
    RAD[RADIUS TACACS+ Server]:::sec

    IT5 --- FW1
    IT4 --- FW1
    FW1 --- DMZ
    DMZ --- FW2
    FW2 --- OT3
    OT3 --- SWC
    SWC --- OT2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Each horizontal band represents a Purdue Model level. All inter-band wired links pass through at least one security control (firewall, data diode, or secured switch with 802.1X). The SIEM/Span taps and RADIUS/IAM servers are out-of-band but feed every enforcement point.

### 4.2 802.1X Authentication Flow for OT Wired Access

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DEV as OT Device Supplicant
    participant SW as OT Switch Authenticator
    participant RAD as RADIUS Server
    participant IAM as IAM Directory
    participant NET as OT Network Resource

    DEV->>SW: EAPOL Start
    SW->>DEV: EAP Request Identity
    DEV->>SW: EAP Response Identity username cert
    SW->>RAD: RADIUS Access Request
    RAD->>IAM: LDAP Lookup
    IAM-->>RAD: User Attributes Roles
    RAD->>RAD: Validate Credential Apply Policy
    alt Authentication Success
        RAD-->>SW: RADIUS Access Accept VLAN ACL
        SW-->>DEV: EAP Success
        SW->>NET: Port Authorized Forward Traffic
    else Authentication Failure
        RAD-->>SW: RADIUS Access Reject
        SW-->>DEV: EAP Failure
        SW->>SW: Port Remains Unauthorized
    end
    Note over SW,DEV: Reauthentication every 1800 to 3600 seconds
```

