# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Layers of Segmentation

### Layer 1 — Traditional Network Segmentation
- **Granularity:** Coarse (subnet/VLAN level)
- **Enforcement:** Hardware firewalls, L3 switches, ACLs, VLANs
- **Policy Source:** Manually configured by network engineers
- **OT Example:** Separating the SCADA network from the corporate LAN using a Layer-3 firewall
- **Limitation:** Slow to update; cannot respond to threats in real time

### Layer 2 — Micro-Segmentation
- **Granularity:** Fine (per-workload, per-device, per-application)
- **Enforcement:** Host-based firewalls, hypervisor-level controls, agent-based software, SDN overlays
- **Policy Source:** Identity + context (user, device, location, time, behaviour)
- **OT Example:** A Siemens S7-1500 PLC is allowed to send Modbus/TCP to **only one specific HMI** on **only one specific port**, and no other communication is permitted
- **Strength:** Drastically reduces the blast radius of a compromise

### Layer 3 — Dynamic Network Segmentation
- **Granularity:** Adaptive and context-aware
- **Enforcement:** SDN controllers, NAC (Network Access Control), SOAR platforms, AI/ML-based analytics, XDR (Extended Detection & Response)
- **Policy Source:** Real-time telemetry, threat intelligence feeds, behavioural baselines
- **OT Example:** A normally trusted engineering laptop is **automatically denied** access to Level 1 if its EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response) agent reports a malware infection. The policy is **dynamically rewritten** without human intervention.
- **Strength:** Closes the gap between detection and response from hours to milliseconds

## 2.2 The Five Operational Steps of Dynamic Segmentation

1. **Asset Discovery & Classification**
   - Continuously identify all OT/IT assets using passive tools (e.g., Claroty, Nozomi, Tenable.ot, Dragos)
   - Assign a **criticality score** based on safety, production, and regulatory impact
   - Maintain a **Cyber Asset Inventory** (mandated by IEC 62443-2-4 and NIST SP 800-82r3)

2. **Zone & Conduit Definition**
   - Group assets into **security zones** based on function, criticality, and trust level
   - Define **conduits** (the allowed communication paths) between zones
   - Apply the **Purdue Model** as a reference (Levels 0–5)

3. **Policy Authoring**
   - Write **identity-based, context-aware policies**: "Source = Engineering Laptop X, Destination = PLC Y, Service = Modbus/TCP Port 502, Action = Allow if posture = Healthy AND time = Maintenance Window"
   - Policies expressed in declarative languages (e.g., Ansible, Terraform, or vendor-specific like Cisco DNA, Fortinet, Palo Alto Panorama)

4. **Automated Enforcement**
   - Push policies to enforcement points (firewalls, switches, SDN controllers, NAC)
   - Use **orchestration platforms** to apply changes network-wide in seconds
   - Validate that policies are correctly implemented (closed-loop verification)

5. **Continuous Monitoring & Re-Authorization**
   - Continuously re-evaluate trust using **telemetry** (NetFlow, sFlow, DPI, SPAN/TAP)
   - On threat detection → trigger **automatic policy revision**
   - On posture change (e.g., OS patch, new vulnerability) → re-authorize the device

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** In real-world OT, dynamic segmentation is implemented using platforms like **Cisco Cyber Vision + DNA Center**, **Fortinet FortiGate + FortiNAC**, **Palo Alto Networks Prisma + Strata**, or **Nozomi Networks + Aruba ClearPass**. The KTU syllabus does not mandate a specific vendor, but you should mention **at least two real-world platforms** in your 14-mark answers to score the application-level marks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all key metrics, equations, and ratios that a KTU examiner may test. Memorize this table — these are the most frequently asked numerical/analytical concepts.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Segmentation Coverage Ratio (SCR)** | $SCR = \dfrac{N_{protected}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | Percentage of OT assets covered by at least one segmentation policy | Percentage (\%) |
| **Lateral Movement Blast Radius (LMBR)** | $LMBR = \dfrac{N_{reachable}}{N_{zone}}$ | Number of assets a compromised device can reach from its zone | Count (assets) |
| **Policy Enforcement Latency (PEL)** | $PEL = T_{detect} + T_{decide} + T_{enforce}$ | End-to-end time from threat detection to policy enforcement | Seconds (s) |
| **Zone Isolation Effectiveness (ZIE)** | $ZIE = 1 - \dfrac{N_{unauthorized\_flows}}{N_{total\_flows}}$ | Measures how well zones block unauthorized traffic | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Mean Time to Contain (MTTC)** | $MTTC = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_{contained,i} - T_{detected,i})$ | Average time to contain a threat post-detection | Seconds / Minutes |
| **Micro-segmentation Granularity Index (MGI)** | $MGI = \dfrac{N_{policies}}{N_{devices}}$ | Average number of policies per device; higher = more granular | Policies / Device |
| **Asset Criticality Score (ACS)** | $ACS = w_1 S + w_2 P + w_3 R$ | Weighted score: $S$ = Safety, $P$ = Production, $R$ = Regulatory, $w$ = weights | Unitless (0–100) |
| **Trust Score Threshold (TST)** | $TST_{dynamic} = f(\text{posture}, \text{behaviour}, \text{context})$ | A dynamic function that re-evaluates device trust continuously | Unitless (0–100) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dynamic network segmentation is a **cornerstone of Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** in OT environments. Its engineering utilities include:

- **Containment of Ransomware:** The **Colonial Pipeline (2021)** and **Kaseya VSA (2021)** attacks demonstrated that lateral movement is the primary vector of damage. Dynamic segmentation limits ransomware spread to the initially compromised segment only.
- **Safety Assurance:** In a chemical plant, an attacker who breaches the corporate IT network must not be able to reach the Safety Instrumented System (SIS). Dynamic segmentation enforces this at the policy layer, complementing the physical safety layer.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Standards like **IEC 62443-3-3 (System Security Requirements)**, **NIST SP 800-82r3 (Guide to ICS Security)**, **NERC-CIP (for energy sector)**, and **TSA SD-Pipeline-2021-01 (for US pipelines)** explicitly mandate network segmentation.
- **Reduced MTTD/MTTR:** By integrating with SIEM, SOAR, and XDR, dynamic segmentation transforms segmentation from a passive barrier into an **active, self-defending layer**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides the **analytical workings** that a KTU 14-mark question demands. While network segmentation is primarily architectural, KTU examiners reward **quantitative reasoning** — derivations of policy density, blast radius reduction, trust score calculations, and policy decision matrices. Every step below is shown explicitly for board-exam-ready answers.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Blast Radius Reduction through Segmentation

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A manufacturing OT network has **$N_{total} = 200$ devices** with no segmentation. A compromised device can reach all other 199 devices (full lateral movement). After implementing a 5-zone Purdue-aligned segmentation, the compromised device is restricted to its own zone containing **$N_{zone} = 40$ devices**. After implementing **micro-segmentation**, the compromised device is restricted to its single peer, i.e., **$N_{peer} = 1$ device**. After implementing **dynamic segmentation** with real-time containment, the compromised device is **quarantined within $T_{enforce} = 5$ seconds**.

**Step 1: Compute Initial Blast Radius (No Segmentation)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LMBR_{initial} &= \frac{N_{reachable}}{N_{total}} \\
&= \frac{199}{200} \\
&= 0.995 \;\; \text{(i.e., 99.5\% of the network is reachable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* With no segmentation, a single compromise exposes the entire network. This is the baseline against which all segmentation strategies are measured.

**Step 2: Compute Blast Radius after Macro-Segmentation (5 Zones)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LMBR_{macro} &= \frac{N_{zone}}{N_{total}} \\
&= \frac{40}{200} \\
&= 0.20 \;\; \text{(i.e., 20\% of the network is reachable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Macro-segmentation reduces the blast radius from 99.5% to 20%, a **5× reduction**. This is why the Purdue Model and IEC 62443 mandate zones.

**Step 3: Compute Blast Radius after Micro-Segmentation (Per-Device Policy)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LMBR_{micro} &= \frac{N_{peer}}{N_{total}} \\
&= \frac{1}{200} \\
&= 0.005 \;\; \text{(i.e., 0.5\% of the network is reachable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Micro-segmentation reduces the blast radius by another **40×** compared to macro-segmentation. The total reduction is **199×** from the initial state.

**Step 4: Compute Dynamic Segmentation Containment Effectiveness**

Let $T_{detect} = 3\,\text{s}$, $T_{decide} = 1\,\text{s}$, $T_{enforce} = 1\,\text{s}$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PEL &= T_{detect} + T_{decide} + T_{enforce} \\
&= 3 + 1 + 1 \\
&= 5 \;\; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The **Policy Enforcement Latency (PEL)** is the total time from threat detection to policy enforcement. In OT, this must be **sub-second** for safety-critical systems and **single-digit seconds** for general process control.

**Step 5: Compute the Cumulative Containment Score (CCS)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CCS &= (1 - LMBR) \times \frac{1}{PEL} \\
&= (1 - 0.005) \times \frac{1}{5} \\
&= 0.199 \;\; \text{per second}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The CCS combines blast radius reduction with response speed. A higher CCS indicates a more effective dynamic segmentation deployment.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Policy Decision Workflow

The following table is the **policy decision matrix** that any dynamic segmentation engine evaluates for **every single packet** in an OT network. KTU examiners frequently ask students to "describe the policy decision workflow" — this matrix is the textbook answer.

| Step | Input | Decision Logic | Output | OT Example |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | Source IP / MAC | Identity lookup in asset inventory | Device ID, Type, Criticality | `192.168.10.45` → "Siemens S7-1500 PLC, Critical" |
| **2** | Destination IP / MAC | Identity lookup | Device ID, Type, Criticality | `10.10.5.12` → "Wonderware HMI, High" |
| **3** | Protocol & Port | Service identification (DPI) | Application = Modbus/TCP | Port 502 detected |
| **4** | User Identity (if applicable) | LDAP / AD / RADIUS lookup | User role, group | "eng_user" in "Engineers" group |
| **5** | Device Posture | EDR / NAC health check | Posture = Healthy / Compromised | Posture = Healthy |
| **6** | Behavioural Context | AI/ML anomaly score | Anomaly score = 0.05 (Low) | Within baseline |
| **7** | Time / Location | Policy-based temporal check | Within maintenance window? | Yes (scheduled maintenance) |
| **8** | Threat Intel | IOC database check | IOC match = No | No match |
| **9** | Policy Match | Evaluate all applicable rules | Action = Allow / Deny / Quarantine | **ALLOW** (matches rule `PLC→HMI-Modbus-Maintenance`) |
| **10** | Audit Log | Log to SIEM | Log entry created | `event_id=7831, action=ALLOW, ts=...` |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** If the KTU question says *"Explain how dynamic segmentation enforces a Zero Trust model in OT"*, your answer **must** include the above matrix or a flowchart equivalent. The examiner awards full marks only if you demonstrate the **continuous re-evaluation** aspect — not just a one-time ACL check.

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Segmentation Coverage Ratio with Real-World Sample Data

**Problem:** An OT environment has the following assets:

- 50 PLCs (Level 1)
- 30 RTUs (Level 1)
- 20 HMIs (Level 2)
- 10 SCADA Servers (Level 2)
- 15 Engineering Workstations (Level 3)
- 10 Historian Servers (Level 3)
- 5 Business Servers (Level 4–5)

Total $N_{total} = 140$ assets.

After deploying a dynamic segmentation platform, the following are protected:

- All 50 PLCs ✓
- All 30 RTUs ✓
- All 20 HMIs ✓
- All 10 SCADA Servers ✓
- 12 of 15 Engineering Workstations ✓
- 10 of 10 Historian Servers ✓
- 0 of 5 Business Servers (covered by separate IT segmentation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{protected} &= 50 + 30 + 20 + 10 + 12 + 10 + 0 = 132 \\
SCR &= \frac{N_{protected}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{132}{140} \times 100 \\
&= 94.28\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* A SCR above **90%** is considered industry best practice for OT environments. The 3 unprotected engineering workstations represent a **gap** that must be remediated — perhaps they are running legacy OS that cannot host the segmentation agent.

## 4.3 Diagram C — Dynamic Segmentation Decision Workflow (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AssetDiscovery
    AssetDiscovery --> PolicyDefinition: Asset Identified and Classified
    PolicyDefinition --> BaselineEnforcement: Policy Authored
    BaselineEnforcement --> ContinuousMonitoring: Policy Pushed to Enforcement Points
    ContinuousMonitoring --> PostureChange: Telemetry Indicates Posture Change
    ContinuousMonitoring --> ThreatDetected: SIEM or Anomaly Engine Triggers
    ContinuousMonitoring --> TimeWindowChange: Maintenance Window Ends
    PostureChange --> PolicyReauthorize: Re evaluate Trust Score
    ThreatDetected --> Quarantine: Block and Isolate Affected Segment
    TimeWindowChange --> PolicyReauthorize: Re evaluate Trust Score
    PolicyReauthorize --> BaselineEnforcement: Apply New Policy
    Quarantine --> ForensicAnalysis: Capture Packets and Logs
    ForensicAnalysis --> PolicyUpdate: Update Policy Based on Findings
    PolicyUpdate --> BaselineEnforcement: Push Updated Policy
```

*Reading the Diagram:* This is the **lifecycle state machine** of a dynamic segmentation engine. The system never rests in a static state — it is always in the `Continuous Monitoring` loop, ready to react to any of the three trigger conditions (posture change, threat detection, time-window change). This is the **core differentiator** between dynamic and traditional segmentation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
**Define network segmentation. How does it differ from micro-segmentation in the context of OT security?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Network segmentation is the practice of dividing a network into smaller, isolated sub-networks (zones) using devices like firewalls, VLANs, and ACLs, each governed by a defined security policy. In OT, segmentation follows the Purdue Model and IEC 62443 zones and conduits.

Micro-segmentation is a finer-grained evolution of segmentation. While traditional segmentation isolates entire subnets or zones, micro-segmentation enforces **per-device, per-workload, and per-application** policies using identity-based, software-defined controls. In OT, this means each PLC, RTU, or HMI can be individually isolated with a unique trust boundary, drastically reducing the lateral movement blast radius. *(3 marks: 1 for definition of segmentation, 1 for definition of micro-segmentation, 1 for the contrast in OT context.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
**List any three benefits of dynamic network segmentation in an Industrial Control System (ICS).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Reduces Lateral Movement:** Dynamic segmentation limits the spread of threats (e.g., ransomware) to a single zone or device, containing breaches in real time. *(1 mark)*
2. **Enforces Zero Trust:** Every flow is continuously authenticated and authorized based on identity, posture, and context, replacing implicit trust. *(1 mark)*
3. **Automates Incident Response:** Integration with SIEM/SOAR/XDR enables sub-second policy updates without human intervention, reducing MTTC. *(1 mark)*
*(A 4th valid benefit — e.g., regulatory compliance with IEC 62443, improved visibility — earns bonus credit.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with a typical split of **7 + 7** across sub-parts. You are given internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** (each is a complete alternative). Both are provided below for full preparation.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — *Theory + Application Focus*

**A. (a)** Explain the concept of **dynamic network segmentation** with reference to its role in OT/ICS environments. Describe the **five operational steps** involved in implementing it, and illustrate your answer with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Dynamic network segmentation is an advanced security architecture in which network access policies are **continuously created, modified, and enforced in real time** based on the identity, context, posture, and behavioural state of OT/IT assets. In OT/ICS environments — where availability, safety, and reliability are paramount — dynamic segmentation extends the traditional Purdue-aligned zone-and-conduit model with **automation and contextual intelligence**, enabling real-time containment of threats such as ransomware, lateral movement, and insider attacks.

The five operational steps are:

1. **Asset Discovery & Classification:** Using passive monitoring tools (Nozomi, Claroty, Dragos), all OT assets (PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, SCADA, historians) are inventoried, fingerprinted, and assigned a **criticality score** based on safety, production, and regulatory impact. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Zone & Conduit Definition:** Assets are grouped into **security zones** aligned with the Purdue Model (Levels 0–5). **Conduits** — the allowed communication paths — are defined with explicit allow/deny policies. The **Industrial DMZ at Level 3.5** acts as the mandatory IT/OT buffer. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Policy Authoring:** Identity-based, context-aware policies are written in a declarative form. Example: *"Engineering Laptop X → PLC Y on Modbus/TCP Port 502 → ALLOW only if device posture is healthy AND time is within the maintenance window."* Policies are stored centrally in an SDN controller or NAC platform. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Automated Enforcement:** Policies are pushed to enforcement points (firewalls, switches, SDN overlays, host-based agents). Orchestration platforms apply changes network-wide within seconds. *(1 mark)*

5. **Continuous Monitoring & Re-Authorization:** Telemetry from EDR, NAC, SIEM, and threat intel feeds is continuously evaluated. On any trigger (posture change, anomaly, IOC match), the policy is **automatically rewritten** and re-pushed. *(1 mark)*

**Diagram (Use the state machine in Section 4.3 of these notes):** Draw the lifecycle: Asset Discovery → Policy Definition → Baseline Enforcement → Continuous Monitoring → (Posture Change / Threat Detected / Time Window Change) → Policy Re-authorization → Loop. *(0.5 marks for diagram)*

**A. (b)** Compare **macro-segmentation, micro-segmentation, and dynamic segmentation** in a tabular format covering granularity, enforcement mechanism, policy source, and OT suitability. Discuss why dynamic segmentation is considered the **evolutionary successor** of static segmentation models. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Macro-Segmentation | Micro-Segmentation | Dynamic Segmentation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Granularity** | Subnet/VLAN/Zone level | Per-device/per-workload | Per-flow, per-context, real-time |
| **Enforcement** | Hardware firewalls, ACLs, VLANs | Host firewalls, SDN overlays, agent-based | SDN controller + NAC + SOAR + AI/ML |
| **Policy Source** | Manually written by network engineers | Identity- and context-aware rule sets | Telemetry-driven, auto-generated, self-updating |
| **OT Suitability** | High (foundational, mandated by IEC 62443) | High (greatly reduces blast radius) | Very High (closes detection-to-response gap) |
| **Response Time to Threat** | Hours to Days | Minutes | Sub-seconds to Seconds |
| **Lateral Movement Reduction** | Moderate (zone-limited) | High (device-limited) | Near-total (dynamic quarantine) |
| **Example** | Purdue Levels, DMZ, VLANs | Per-PLC Modbus policy | Auto-quarantine on EDR alert |

**Why Dynamic is the Evolutionary Successor:** *(3 marks of the 7)*
- Traditional macro-segmentation is **static** — once written, rules do not change until a human updates them. In modern OT, threat actors move in minutes; static defences cannot keep pace.
- Micro-segmentation reduces the blast radius but still requires **manual policy updates** when the environment changes (new device, new vulnerability, new threat).
- Dynamic segmentation **closes the loop** by integrating with detection systems (EDR, SIEM, SOAR) and **automatically rewriting policies** in response to real-time events. It embodies the **Zero Trust principle** of "never trust, always verify" at machine speed.
- It is the **only viable model** for OT environments facing modern threats like ransomware-as-a-service, supply chain attacks, and state-sponsored APTs.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Numerical + Analytical Focus*

**B. (a)** An OT network contains **180 devices** distributed as follows: 60 PLCs (Level 1), 40 HMIs (Level 2), 20 SCADA Servers (Level 2), 30 Engineering Workstations (Level 3), 20 Historians (Level 3), and 10 Business Servers (Level 4–5). Initially, there is **no segmentation**, and a single compromised device can reach all others. After deploying **macro-segmentation** in line with the Purdue Model, the network is divided into 4 functional zones, and a compromised PLC can reach only devices in its own zone (15 devices). After **micro-segmentation**, a compromised PLC can reach only **2 specific peer devices**. After **dynamic segmentation** is added, threat detection takes **4 seconds**, policy decision takes **1 second**, and enforcement takes **0.5 seconds**.

**Calculate:**
**(i)** The Initial Blast Radius (as a percentage).
**(ii)** The Blast Radius after Macro-Segmentation (as a percentage).
**(iii)** The Blast Radius after Micro-Segmentation (as a percentage).
**(iv)** The Policy Enforcement Latency (PEL) in seconds.
**(v)** Comment on the security improvement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $N_{total} = 180$ devices
- $N_{zone} = 15$ devices (Purdue-aligned macro-segmentation)
- $N_{peer} = 2$ devices (micro-segmentation)
- $T_{detect} = 4$ s, $T_{decide} = 1$ s, $T_{enforce} = 0.5$ s

**(i) Initial Blast Radius (No Segmentation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LMBR_{initial} &= \frac{N_{total} - 1}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{180 - 1}{180} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{179}{180} \times 100 \\
&\approx 99.44\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**(ii) Blast Radius after Macro-Segmentation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LMBR_{macro} &= \frac{N_{zone}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{15}{180} \times 100 \\
&\approx 8.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**(iii) Blast Radius after Micro-Segmentation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LMBR_{micro} &= \frac{N_{peer}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2}{180} \times 100 \\
&\approx 1.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**(iv) Policy Enforcement Latency (PEL):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PEL &= T_{detect} + T_{decide} + T_{enforce} \\
&= 4 + 1 + 0.5 \\
&= 5.5 \;\; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**(v) Security Improvement Commentary:** *(3 marks)*

The implementation of dynamic network segmentation has yielded a **compounding security improvement**:
- The blast radius is reduced from **99.44% → 8.33% → 1.11%**, a total reduction factor of nearly **90×**.
- A compromised device now affects only **2 out of 180 devices** — a worst-case scenario of **1.11% network exposure**.
- The PEL of **5.5 seconds** is well within the safety-critical threshold for OT (typically 10 seconds for non-SIS, sub-second for SIS).
- The combination of **low blast radius + low PEL** means the **Cumulative Containment Score (CCS)** is exceptionally high:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CCS &= (1 - LMBR_{micro}) \times \frac{1}{PEL} \\
&= (1 - 0.0111) \times \frac{1}{5.5} \\
&\approx 0.1798 \;\; \text{per second}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a strong deployment, but for **safety-critical assets** (e.g., SIS, emergency shutdown), the PEL should be brought down to **sub-second** levels by deploying edge-based enforcement and pre-cached policies.

---

**B. (b)** With the help of a suitable **policy decision matrix**, explain how a dynamic segmentation engine processes a typical OT communication flow (e.g., an HMI requesting data from a PLC using Modbus/TCP). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A dynamic segmentation engine evaluates **every packet** through a structured decision workflow before allowing, denying, or quarantining it. The following matrix traces a sample flow:

| Step | Input | Decision Logic | Output | Sample Value |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Source IP | Identity lookup in asset DB | Device ID | `10.10.5.12` → "Wonderware HMI, Zone=L2" |
| 2 | Destination IP | Identity lookup in asset DB | Device ID | `192.168.10.45` → "Siemens S7-1500, Zone=L1" |
| 3 | Protocol & Port | Deep Packet Inspection | Application = Modbus/TCP | Port 502 detected |
| 4 | User Context | LDAP/RADIUS lookup | Role = "Operator" | Authenticated, role = Operator |
| 5 | Device Posture | EDR/NAC health check | Posture = Healthy | AV signatures current, no malware |
| 6 | Behavioural Analytics | AI/ML anomaly score | Anomaly Score = 0.04 | Within baseline (Low) |
| 7 | Time/Location | Temporal policy check | Within allowed window? | Yes, 10:00 AM (operational hours) |
| 8 | Threat Intel | IOC database match | IOC match? | No match |
| 9 | Policy Match | Evaluate all rules | Action | **ALLOW** (matches "HMI→PLC Modbus Operational") |
| 10 | Audit Logging | SIEM ingestion | Log ID | `event_id=7831, action=ALLOW` |

*Each row of the table is worth approximately 0.6 marks (6 marks total for the table).*

**Key Insight (1 mark):** The matrix demonstrates that the dynamic segmentation engine does **not** make a single binary "allow/deny" decision. It performs a **multi-dimensional, context-aware evaluation** combining identity, posture, behaviour, time, and threat intelligence. If **any** of these factors deviates from the policy (e.g., the HMI's posture changes to "Infected" or the anomaly score spikes to 0.85), the engine **automatically re-evaluates and re-authorizes** the flow — converting a static firewall into a **self-defending, adaptive security control**. This is the essence of Zero Trust in OT.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 2–4 Marks if Ignored):**
> 1. **Conflating Segmentation Types:** Many students use the terms "segmentation," "micro-segmentation," and "dynamic segmentation" interchangeably. Examiners deduct marks for this. Always **define each term separately** and state the evolution from one to the next.
> 2. **Forgetting the Purdue Model:** Any answer on OT segmentation that does not reference the **Purdue Model (Levels 0–5) and Level 3.5 DMZ** will lose at least 2 marks. The Purdue Model is the examiner's benchmark for OT-specific answers.
> 3. **Omitting Real-World Standards:** Failure to mention **IEC 62443 (Zones & Conduits), NIST SP 800-82r3, or NERC-CIP** will cost marks. These are expected references.
> 4. **Static-Only Answers:** A common mistake is to describe segmentation as a one-time configuration. KTU examiners specifically test whether you understand **continuous re-authorization** and **policy automation**. Use phrases like *"real-time,"* *"context-aware,"* *"telemetry-driven,"* and *"automated policy rewriting."*
> 5. **Numerical Errors in Part B:** Always show the **formula first, then the substitution, then the final answer** in a separate step. A correct final number with no working scores only partial marks.
> 6. **Missing the Diagram:** A 14-mark question without a diagram or matrix is almost guaranteed to lose 2–3 marks. Always include a visual.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **Network Segmentation** = Coarse-grained, zone-level isolation using firewalls, VLANs, ACLs. Aligned with **Purdue Model** and **IEC 62443 Zones & Conduits**.
- ✅ **Micro-Segmentation** = Fine-grained, per-device/per-workload policies using SDN overlays and host-based firewalls.
- ✅ **Dynamic Network Segmentation** = Real-time, context-aware, telemetry-driven, automated policy rewriting. It is the **active, self-defending** evolution of static segmentation.
- ✅ **Three Pillars of Dynamic Segmentation:** *Visibility (asset discovery) + Policy (identity + context) + Automation (SDN/NAC/SOAR integration).*
- ✅ **Purdue Model Levels (Memorize):** L0 = Physical Process, L1 = Basic Control (PLCs/RTUs), L2 = Supervisory (SCADA/HMI), L3 = Site Operations, **L3.5 = Industrial DMZ (mandatory IT/OT buffer)**, L4 = Enterprise, L5 = Corporate/Internet.
- ✅ **Key Standards:** **IEC 62443-3-3** (security requirements), **NIST SP 800-82r3** (ICS security guide), **NERC-CIP** (energy), **TSA Pipeline Security Directive** (pipelines).
- ✅ **Key Metrics to Memorize:** $LMBR$ (Lateral Movement Blast Radius), $SCR$ (Segmentation Coverage Ratio), $PEL$ (Policy Enforcement Latency), $MTTC$ (Mean Time to Contain), $CCS$ (Cumulative Containment Score).
- ✅ **Real-World Platforms to Name in Answers:** Cisco Cyber Vision + DNA Center, Fortinet FortiGate + FortiNAC, Palo Alto Prisma, Nozomi Networks, Claroty, Dragos, Tenable.ot.
- ✅ **Lateral Movement Reduction:** Macro (≈5–10×) → Micro (≈100–200×) → Dynamic (≈1000× with sub-second containment).
- ✅ **Zero Trust = "Never Trust, Always Verify"** — every flow is authenticated, authorized, and continuously re-evaluated.
- ✅ **PEL Formula:** $PEL = T_{detect} + T_{decide} + T_{enforce}$. For safety-critical OT, this must be **sub-second**.
- ✅ **SIS (Safety Instrumented System) is ALWAYS isolated** by a dedicated conduit — never share a zone with process control.
- ✅ **Always draw a diagram or matrix** in 14-mark answers. Examiners reward visual structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing dynamic network segmentation in OT-IT environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementing dynamic network segmentation in OT-IT environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamic Network Segmentation (DNSec)** in OT-IT environments is a security architecture strategy that creates flexible, policy-driven, and context-aware logical boundaries within a converged industrial network. Unlike static segmentation that uses fixed VLANs, ACLs, and physical firewall rules, dynamic segmentation adjusts network zones, access controls, and traffic flows in **real-time** based on the identity of the device, its current security posture, location, operational state, and the contextual risk score at that moment.

According to the **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3** and **IEC 62443-3-3** standards, dynamic segmentation is a critical compensating control that enforces the foundational principle of **"least privilege communication"** between Operational Technology (OT) assets (PLCs, SCADA, DCS, RTUs) and Information Technology (IT) systems (ERP, email, web).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECCT634 – Module 3):**
> Dynamic segmentation is the *modern evolution* of the traditional Purdue Model, where zones and conduits (Z&C) are no longer physically or statically defined but are enforced programmatically through SDN controllers, NAC solutions, and next-generation firewalls (NGFWs).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **modern airport terminal security system**:

- **Static segmentation** is like having *fixed* boarding gates that anyone with a ticket can pass through — once you're past the first check, you can roam freely.
- **Dynamic segmentation** is like an **AI-powered boarding system** that re-evaluates every passenger at each gate using their *current ticket class*, *real-time watchlist status*, *lounge access level*, and *flight timing*. If a passenger's risk score changes (e.g., they visit a restricted area), the gates physically close or redirect them automatically.

In an **OT-IT converged plant**, dynamic segmentation does the same:
- A **PLC** that normally only talks to its **HMI** is suddenly quarantined when it starts communicating with the **engineering workstation on port 4444** (a known ransomware C2 pattern).
- The segmentation engine **recalculates trust** continuously and *shifts the asset* into a remediation VLAN — without human intervention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Static segmentation is **rule-based and rigid**; dynamic segmentation is **identity-based, context-aware, and adaptive** — directly aligning with the **Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207)** principle: *"Never trust, always verify."*

### Standard Metrics Used (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Mean Time to Contain (MTTC)** — typically reduced from **hours/days** (static) to **seconds/minutes** (dynamic).
- **Microsegmentation Granularity** — policies applied down to the **device, port, and process** level.
- **Asset Visibility Index** — percentage of OT assets with **identity fingerprints** enrolled in the segmentation policy engine.
- **Lateral Movement Reduction (LMR)** — measured in *blocked east-west traffic attempts* per day.
- **Trust Score Threshold** — typically a numerical value between **0–100**; assets below threshold are auto-isolated.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Dynamic Segmentation

Dynamic segmentation operates on a **continuous trust-evaluation loop**. The architecture can be broken down into the following structured logic steps:

1. **Asset Discovery & Identity Fingerprinting**
   - Every device in the OT-IT fabric is passively identified via DPI (Deep Packet Inspection), MAC, IP, protocol signature (Modbus, DNP3, S7Comm, EtherNet/IP), and CIP (Common Industrial Protocol) attributes.
   - A persistent **device identity record** is created in a central inventory (e.g., Claroty, Nozomi, Dragos, Tenable.ot).

2. **Baseline Behavior Profiling**
   - The system learns the *normal* communication pattern of each asset: peer list, ports, packet cadence, time-of-day activity, process variables.

3. **Policy Definition (Intent-Based)**
   - Security engineers write *intent-based* rules — e.g., *"PLC_A may only communicate with HMI_1 on TCP 502; no other traffic permitted."*
   - Policies are stored centrally in an SDN controller or NAC policy server (Cisco ISE, ForeScout, Aruba ClearPass, FortiNAC).

4. **Real-Time Trust Scoring**
   - Each asset is continuously scored based on: compliance state, CVE exposure, behavioral anomaly, location, user identity.

5. **Dynamic Enforcement**
   - When an asset's trust score falls below threshold, the controller pushes a **new ACL, VLAN, or VXLAN segment** to the nearest switching fabric.
   - The asset is logically relocated into a **quarantine, remediation, or honeypot zone**.

6. **Continuous Verification & Logging**
   - Every communication attempt is logged, alerted, and fed into SIEM for audit (a key requirement of **IEC 62443-3-3 SR 5.1 – Network Segmentation**).

### 2.2 The Purdue Model as the Static Foundation

Dynamic segmentation **builds upon** (but does not replace) the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** levels:

| Level | Name | Static Zone | Dynamic Adjustment |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Level 5** | Enterprise Network | Corporate IT Zone | Trust score from EDR + IAM |
| **Level 4** | Site Business Planning | DMZ / Historian Zone | Re-segmented on patch Tuesdays |
| **Level 3.5** | Industrial DMZ | Jump Server / WSUS / AV | Strictest enforcement |
| **Level 3** | Site Operations | SCADA / Engineering WS | Quarantine on anomaly |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory Control | HMI / SCADA Server | Microsegmented per cell |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control | PLCs / DCS Controllers | Per-process VXLAN |
| **Level 0** | Process | Sensors / Actuators | Physically isolated + monitored |

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Component

- **Why dynamic?** Traditional OT networks assumed the *perimeter was secure* (air-gap myth). Modern connected plants demand *internal defense-in-depth* because threats like **TRITON, Industroyer, and Pipedream** move laterally.
- **Why identity-based?** IP addresses in OT are notoriously *unstable* (devices fail over, engineers re-assign IPs). Identity (MAC + serial + protocol) is more stable.
- **Why software-defined?** Hardware reconfiguration in a live plant causes **unplanned downtime** — costing **$50,000 to $250,000 per hour** depending on industry.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notation | Engineering Utility |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Trust Score | $T_s = w_1 C + w_2 P + w_3 B + w_4 L$ | $T_s \in [0,100]$ | Threshold-based dynamic zoning |
| 2 | Anomaly Score | $A = \dfrac{\vert x_i - \mu \vert}{\sigma}$ | Z-score (σ units) | Triggers re-segmentation |
| 3 | Lateral Movement Risk | $LMR = 1 - \dfrac{N_{blocked}}{N_{attempts}}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Measures segmentation effectiveness |
| 4 | MTTC (Containment) | $MTTC = T_{detect} + T_{decide} + T_{enforce}$ | Seconds / Minutes | KPI for dynamic vs static |
| 5 | Microsegment Coverage | $C_{micro} = \dfrac{N_{policies\_applied}}{N_{total\_flows}}$ | Percentage (%) | Compliance metric |
| 6 | Asset Visibility | $V = \dfrac{N_{fingerprinted}}{N_{total\_OT\_assets}}$ | Percentage (%) | First step in dynamic control |
| 7 | Risk Score (CVSS adjusted) | $R = CVSS \times (1 + E_{exposure})$ | Numeric | Triggers dynamic quarantine |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $C$ = compliance, $P$ = patch level, $B$ = behavioral baseline deviation, $L$ = location trust, $E_{exposure}$ = external exposure factor, $w_i$ are weighted coefficients summing to 1.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dynamic segmentation is deployed in:
- **Power generation** (smart grids) — IEC 61850 protection.
- **Oil & Gas** (upstream SCADA, midstream pipelines) — TSA Pipeline Directive compliance.
- **Pharmaceutical manufacturing** (FDA 21 CFR Part 11) — auditability of every flow.
- **Automotive smart factories** (Industry 4.0) — IIoT device onboarding.
- **Water Treatment (CISA)** — sector-specific OT resilience.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Trust Score Computation for Dynamic Zoning

We derive the *Trust Score* $T_s$ that the policy engine uses to decide whether to move an asset between zones dynamically.

**Step 1 — Define the input vectors.**

Let each contributing factor be normalized to a value $x \in [0,1]$:

- $x_1$: Compliance posture (1 = fully compliant, 0 = non-compliant).
- $x_2$: Patch freshness (1 = up-to-date, 0 = critical CVEs open).
- $x_3$: Behavioral baseline deviation (1 = no deviation, 0 = high anomaly).
- $x_4$: Location / network context (1 = inside trusted zone, 0 = in unknown VLAN).

**Step 2 — Apply the weighted linear combination.**

$$
T_s = 100 \cdot \left( w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_3 x_3 + w_4 x_4 \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Define weights (typical industrial policy).**

In a balanced OT-IT policy, weights are usually assigned as:

$$
w_1 = 0.30, \quad w_2 = 0.20, \quad w_3 = 0.35, \quad w_4 = 0.15
$$

The constraint is:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i = 1.00
$$

**Step 4 — Define the dynamic segmentation threshold.**

A typical policy uses a **dual-threshold model**:

$$
T_{quarantine} = 40, \quad T_{remediation} = 65, \quad T_{production} \geq 65
$$

Where the zone decision $Z$ is:

$$
Z =
\begin{cases}
\text{Production Zone} & \text{if } T_s \geq T_{remediation} \\
\text{Remediation Zone} & \text{if } T_{remediation} > T_s \geq T_{quarantine} \\
\text{Quarantine Zone} & \text{if } T_s < T_{quarantine}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**

Consider a **Siemens S7-1500 PLC** with the following state:
- $x_1 = 1.0$ (compliant with hardening baseline).
- $x_2 = 0.5$ (one medium-severity CVE unpatched).
- $x_3 = 0.2$ (large deviation — suddenly initiating TLS to internet IP).
- $x_4 = 1.0$ (located in the correct Level 1 cell).

Substituting:

$$
T_s = 100 \cdot \left( (0.30)(1.0) + (0.20)(0.5) + (0.35)(0.2) + (0.15)(1.0) \right)
$$

$$
T_s = 100 \cdot \left( 0.30 + 0.10 + 0.07 + 0.15 \right)
$$

$$
T_s = 100 \cdot 0.62 = 62
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the zone decision.**

Since $T_s = 62$ and $T_{remediation} = 65$, we have:

$$
T_{quarantine} \leq 62 < T_{remediation}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Z = \text{Remediation Zone}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The PLC is **automatically pushed into a remediation VLAN** where it can only talk to the patch server and SIEM — but cannot control the physical process. The SDN controller pushes a new VXLAN tag and ACL within **seconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** This derivation is a common **14-mark question pattern** — examiners expect you to show the full computation, define the threshold policy, and explain the *operational consequence* of the zone decision.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Static vs Dynamic Segmentation

| Parameter | Static Segmentation | Dynamic Segmentation |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Policy Update Latency | Days / Weeks | Milliseconds / Seconds |
| Identity Used | IP / MAC | IP + MAC + Protocol + Behavior + User |
| East-West Traffic Control | Coarse (VLAN-wide) | Fine-grained (per-flow) |
| Reaction to Anomaly | Manual | Automated |
| Compliance with NIST 800-207 | Partial | Full |
| Downtime During Change | High | Near-Zero |
| Scalability for IIoT | Poor | Excellent |
| OT-Specific Protocol Awareness | None / Limited | Native (Modbus, DNP3, S7, CIP) |
| Initial Deployment Cost | Low | Medium–High |
| TCO (5 years) | Higher (incident cost) | Lower (proactive) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Dynamic Segmentation Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture** of a dynamic network segmentation system deployed in a typical OT-IT converged plant, showing the policy decision loop and enforcement path.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OT_ZONES["OT PROCESS ZONES LEVEL 0 TO 2"]
        PLC1["PLC CELL A"]
        PLC2["PLC CELL B"]
        HMI1["HMI STATION 1"]
        RTU1["RTU SUBSTATION"]
    end

    subgraph IDM["IDENTITY AND DISCOVERY LAYER"]
        PAS["PASSIVE OT SENSOR NOZOMI OR CLAROTY"]
        DPI["DEEP PACKET INSPECTION ENGINE"]
        DB[("DEVICE FINGERPRINT DATABASE")]
    end

    subgraph POLICY_ENGINE["CENTRAL POLICY AND TRUST ENGINE"]
        NAC["NAC SERVER CISCO ISE OR FORESCOUT"]
        SDN["SDN CONTROLLER CISCO ACI OR APIC"]
        TS["TRUST SCORE COMPUTER"]
        ZTA["ZERO TRUST POLICY DECISION POINT"]
    end

    subgraph ENFORCEMENT["ENFORCEMENT LAYER"]
        SW1["OT SWITCH LAYER 2 L3"]
        FW1["NGFW FORTINET OR PALO ALTO"]
        WX["VXLAN FABRIC"]
    end

    subgraph IT_ZONES["IT ENTERPRISE ZONES LEVEL 4 TO 5"]
        ERP["ERP SERVER"]
        MAIL["EMAIL GATEWAY"]
        HIST["OT HISTORIAN"]
    end

    subgraph ZONES_OUTCOME["DYNAMIC ZONE OUTPUT"]
        PROD["PRODUCTION ZONE"]
        REM["REMEDIATION ZONE"]
        QTN["QUARANTINE ZONE"]
    end

    PLC1 --> PAS
    PLC2 --> PAS
    HMI1 --> PAS
    RTU1 --> PAS
    PAS --> DPI
    DPI --> DB
    DB --> NAC
    NAC --> TS
    TS --> ZTA
    ZTA --> SDN
    SDN --> SW1
    SW1 --> WX
    WX --> FW1
    FW1 --> PROD
    FW1 --> REM
    FW1 --> QTN
    ERP -.->|Allowed| FW1
    MAIL -.->|Blocked| FW1
    HIST <-->|Bi Di| FW1
```

### 4.2 Trust Score Decision Flow (Sequential Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DEVICE SENDS PACKET"] --> B["PASSIVE SENSOR CAPTURES FLOW"]
    B --> C["FINGERPRINT DEVICE"]
    C --> D{"KNOWN IN INVENTORY?"}
    D -- "NO" --> E["ASSIGN DEFAULT QUARANTINE VLAN"]
    D -- "YES" --> F["COMPUTE BEHAVIORAL DELTA"]
    F --> G["RETRIEVE CVE AND COMPLIANCE DATA"]
    G --> H["CALCULATE TRUST SCORE TS"]
    H --> I{"TS GREATER 65?"}
    I -- "YES" --> J["KEEP IN PRODUCTION VXLAN"]
    I -- "NO" --> K{"TS BETWEEN 40 AND 65?"}
    K -- "YES" --> L["MOVE TO REMEDIATION VXLAN"]
    K -- "NO" --> M["MOVE TO QUARANTINE VXLAN"]
    J --> N["LOG TO SIEM"]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    E --> N
    N --> O["ALERT SOC ANALYST"]
```

### 4.3 Sequence: Lateral Movement Containment

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ENG as Engineering Workstation
    participant PLC as Target PLC
    participant SENS as OT Sensor
    participant NAC as NAC Policy Engine
    participant SW as Access Switch
    participant SIEM as SIEM

    ENG->>PLC: Reconnaissance scan (port 102)
    PLC->>SENS: Normal traffic (port 502)
    SENS->>NAC: Anomaly detected (unauthorized peer)
    NAC->>NAC: Recompute Trust Score = 38
    NAC->>SW: Push dynamic ACL (deny all from ENG to PLC)
    SW-->>ENG: TCP RST (lateral movement blocked)
    NAC->>SIEM: Log HIGH severity event
    SIEM-->>SOC: Alert to analyst
    Note over NAC,SW: MTTC = ~3 seconds (vs hours statically)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The examiner expects you to mention *at minimum* — **asset identity → trust score → SDN enforcement → quarantine VLAN → SIEM logging** in any 7-mark answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define dynamic network segmentation in an OT-IT environment and state TWO advantages over static segmentation.**

**Model Answer:**
Dynamic network segmentation is a context-aware security architecture that creates logical boundaries in a converged OT-IT network using identity, behavior, and real-time trust scoring of devices, rather than fixed IP/VLAN rules. **(1 mark)**

**Two advantages over static segmentation:** **(2 marks)**
1. **Automated response to anomalies** — lateral movement is blocked within seconds via SDN/NAC without manual ACL changes.
2. **Identity-based granularity** — policies follow the device, not the IP, which is more reliable in OT environments where IPs change during failover.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is the role of a Trust Score in dynamic segmentation? Write the formula.**

**Model Answer:**
The Trust Score $T_s$ is a **numeric value between 0 and 100** that represents the current trustworthiness of an OT asset, computed continuously by the policy engine. When the score falls below a defined threshold, the asset is dynamically moved into a remediation or quarantine zone. **(2 marks)**

$$
T_s = 100 \cdot \left( w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_3 x_3 + w_4 x_4 \right)
$$

where $x_1$ to $x_4$ are normalized compliance, patch, behavioral, and location scores with weights $w_1 \dots w_4$ summing to 1. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam]**
### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the architecture of dynamic network segmentation in an OT-IT converged environment with a neat block diagram. Discuss the roles of passive sensors, NAC, SDN controller, and NGFW in the data path. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A Siemens S7-1200 PLC has the following state: compliance $x_1 = 0.8$, patch $x_2 = 0.6$, behavioral deviation $x_3 = 0.3$, location $x_4 = 1.0$. Weights are $w_1 = 0.3, w_2 = 0.2, w_3 = 0.35, w_4 = 0.15$. Compute the trust score and determine the zone of the asset. Assume $T_{remediation} = 65$ and $T_{quarantine} = 40$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The architecture consists of **five logical layers**: **(7 marks)**

1. **OT Asset Layer (Level 0–2)** — PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, sensors. They are *unaware* of segmentation; they simply generate traffic.
2. **Visibility Layer** — Passive OT sensors (Nozomi, Claroty, Dragos) tap SPAN ports and perform DPI on industrial protocols (Modbus/TCP, DNP3, S7Comm, EtherNet/IP). They build a **fingerprint database** of every device.
3. **Identity Layer** — The fingerprint DB feeds the **NAC server** (Cisco ISE, ForeScout) which correlates device identity with user identity and posture state.
4. **Decision Layer (Trust Engine)** — The **Trust Score Computer** within the SDN controller (Cisco ACI, VMware NSX) combines compliance, patch, behavioral, and location inputs. The **Zero Trust PDP** decides the zone.
5. **Enforcement Layer** — The SDN controller pushes **VXLAN segments or dynamic ACLs** to access switches and to the **NGFW**, which enforces east-west and north-south policy.

**Diagram:** (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 — student should redraw it manually with Purdue levels labeled).

**Model Solution for (b):**

Substitute the values into the trust score formula: **(3 marks for formula, 3 marks for substitution, 1 mark for zone)**

$$
T_s = 100 \cdot \left( (0.3)(0.8) + (0.2)(0.6) + (0.35)(0.3) + (0.15)(1.0) \right)
$$

$$
T_s = 100 \cdot \left( 0.24 + 0.12 + 0.105 + 0.15 \right)
$$

$$
T_s = 100 \cdot 0.615 = 61.5
$$

Applying the zone decision rule with $T_{quarantine} = 40$ and $T_{remediation} = 65$:

$$
40 \leq 61.5 < 65 \implies Z = \text{Remediation Zone}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The PLC will be dynamically assigned to the **Remediation VXLAN**, where it can only reach the patch server, antivirus update server, and SIEM collector — its process control traffic is **suspended** until the trust score improves above 65. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Compare static and dynamic network segmentation across SIX parameters. Justify why dynamic segmentation is essential for Industry 4.0 / IIoT environments. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Describe the role of the following in a dynamic OT segmentation architecture: (i) Purdue Reference Model, (ii) IEC 62443-3-3, (iii) Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207), (iv) VXLAN overlays. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

A comparison table is drawn (refer to Section 3.2 matrix). **(4 marks for table, 3 marks for justification)**

**Justification for Industry 4.0 / IIoT:** **(3 marks)**
1. IIoT devices are *provisioned in thousands* with dynamic addresses — static ACLs become unmanageable.
2. Predictive maintenance and digital twin require **east-west** traffic between shop-floor and cloud — granular policy is essential.
3. The convergence of IT and OT **eliminates the traditional air-gap**, making **identity-based microsegmentation** the only viable defense.

**Model Solution for (b):**

- **(i) Purdue Reference Model** — provides the **physical and logical hierarchy** (Levels 0–5) on which segmentation zones are anchored. Each level is a candidate for dynamic micro-segment. **(1.75 marks)**
- **(ii) IEC 62443-3-3** — defines the **System Security Requirements (SR 5.1: Network Segmentation, SR 5.2: Zone Boundary Protection)** that dynamic segmentation must satisfy. **(1.75 marks)**
- **(iii) Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207)** — supplies the **philosophy of "never trust, always verify"**; dynamic segmentation is the *technical implementation* of ZTA in OT. **(1.75 marks)**
- **(iv) VXLAN overlays** — provide the **logical tunnel fabric** that allows the SDN controller to move assets between zones *without physical re-cabling* — enabling zero-downtime segmentation. **(1.75 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly **lose 2 marks** by giving definitions without a diagram. **Always draw the block diagram** — even a hand-drawn Purdue-level sketch earns full credit.
> - For numerical problems, students often **skip the zone decision step**. Showing $Z = \text{Remediation Zone}$ explicitly is mandatory.
> - Do **not confuse VLAN with VXLAN** in answers. VLAN = Layer 2 broadcast domain (static); VXLAN = Layer 2-over-Layer-3 overlay (dynamic, SDN-controlled).
> - Do not write "firewall" generically — write **"NGFW with application awareness and OT protocol DPI"** for full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dynamic Network Segmentation (DNSec)** is *identity-based, context-aware, real-time* logical zoning of OT-IT networks — not a replacement of Purdue, but its modern evolution.
- **Key standards:** NIST SP 800-82 R3, NIST SP 800-207, IEC 62443-3-3 (SR 5.1, 5.2), ISA-95.
- **Core components:** Passive OT sensor → Fingerprint DB → NAC → Trust Score Engine → SDN Controller → NGFW/VXLAN fabric → SIEM.
- **Trust Score formula:** $T_s = 100 \cdot \sum w_i x_i$, with weights summing to 1.
- **Zone decision:** $T_s \geq 65$ → Production; $40 \leq T_s < 65$ → Remediation; $T_s < 40$ → Quarantine.
- **Enabling technologies:** SDN (Cisco ACI, NSX), NAC (Cisco ISE, ForeScout), NGFW (Palo Alto, Fortinet), OT visibility (Nozomi, Claroty, Dragos).
- **KPI metrics:** MTTC, LMR, microsegment coverage, asset visibility, trust score.
- **Common exam buzzwords:** Zero Trust, microsegmentation, east-west traffic, lateral movement, compensating control, defense-in-depth, air-gap myth, Purdue Level 3.5 (Industrial DMZ).
- **Always remember:** A diagram of the Purdue model with dynamic overlays earns **easy 2–3 marks** in any 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monitoring, Analysis, and Threat Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Monitoring, Analysis, and Threat Response ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Monitoring, Analysis, and Threat Response** is the operational triad of a Security Operations Center (SOC) and a Cybersecurity Operations Centre (CSOC) specifically adapted for Operational Technology (OT) environments. It encompasses the **continuous collection of telemetry** from industrial assets, the **analytical correlation and contextualization** of that telemetry against threat intelligence and behavioral baselines, and the **orchestrated response actions** designed to contain, eradicate, and recover from cyber-physical incidents without compromising safety, availability, or process integrity. The three sub-domains are formally defined as: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Monitoring** — The passive and active collection of security-relevant data (logs, flows, process variables, protocol traffic) from OT/ICS assets to establish a situational awareness baseline. > > **Analysis** — The cognitive and computational process of correlating monitoring data, applying detection logic, and assessing the risk, intent, and impact of observed events. > > **Threat Response** — The pre-planned, coordinated set of procedural and technical actions executed to contain, mitigate, and recover from a confirmed or suspected cyber incident. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hospital ICU" Imagine a hospital's **Intensive Care Unit (ICU)** for critical patients. The hospital is your **OT plant** (a refinery, power grid, or water treatment facility). | ICU Component | OT Security Equivalent | |---|---| | Patient monitors (heart rate, BP, SpO₂) | ICS sensors, PLCs, RTUs, network taps | | Alarm thresholds on the monitor | IDS/IPS signatures, anomaly detection rules | | Nurse reviewing charts every hour | SOC analyst (Tier 1, Tier 2, Tier 3) | | Specialist doctor on call | Incident Response (IR) team, CSIRT | | Crash cart and emergency protocol | SOAR playbooks, isolation switches, fail-safe procedures | | Patient medical history | Asset inventory, network topology, baseline behavior | Just as a nurse does **not** wait until a patient's heart stops to act (proactive monitoring) and a doctor does not prescribe medicine based on a single reading (multi-signal analysis), an OT defender must continuously monitor, intelligently correlate, and respond with precision — because in OT, a wrong response (e.g., shutting down a chemical reactor abruptly) can cause more damage than the attack itself. ### 1.3 Key Metrics Every KTU Student Must Know > [!NOTE] > The following **four performance indicators** are the gold-standard metrics the KTU board examiners expect students to define and use in calculations: > > - **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect)** — Average time between compromise and detection. > - **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond/Recover)** — Average time between detection and resolution. > - **MTTC (Mean Time To Contain)** — Average time between detection and containment. > - **FPR / FNR (False Positive / False Negative Rate)** — Accuracy indicators of detection logic. These constants are not physical constants like $G$ or $c$, but they are **standard industry metrics** (per NIST SP 800-61 and ISA/IEC 62443) and are highlighted in **bold** because they appear frequently in KTU Part A and Part B questions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars — Operational Breakdown #### Pillar 1: Monitoring (The "Eyes and Ears") OT monitoring is fundamentally different from IT monitoring. In IT, you monitor user behavior, endpoints, and web traffic. In OT, you monitor **process variables, control commands, and engineering workstations**. **What is collected:** - **Network telemetry** — Modbus TCP, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, EtherNet/IP, PROFINET, OPC UA traffic. - **Host telemetry** — Engineering workstation (EWS) logs, HMI events, historian records. - **Process telemetry** — Sensor readings (temperature, pressure, flow), actuator states, alarm logs from SCADA/DCS. - **Identity telemetry** — Authentication events on jump servers, RDP/VPN access to Level 3 (DMZ) and Level 2 (Control) zones. **How it is collected:** - **Passive tapping** on SPAN ports of industrial switches (no active probing — that could crash legacy PLCs). - **Agentless log forwarding** from Windows-based EWS and Historian servers. - **Protocol-aware deep packet inspection (DPI)** using ICS-aware sensors (e.g., Nozomi, Claroty, Dragos). - **Syslog, SNMP traps, Windows Event Forwarding (WEF)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** Any active scanning (ping sweeps, port scans) on a live OT network is **strictly prohibited** in standards like ISA/IEC 62443-3-3 and NIST SP 800-82r3 because it can disrupt deterministic control loops. Students must mention this in viva when asked about monitoring techniques. #### Pillar 2: Analysis (The "Brain") Raw data is noise. Analysis turns noise into signal. The analysis layer has three concentric rings: - **Tier 1 — Correlation:** SIEM (e.g., Splunk, QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel) ingests logs, applies correlation rules, and reduces millions of events into a handful of actionable alerts. - **Tier 2 — Contextualization:** Enrichment with threat intelligence (IP reputation, malware hashes, CVE feeds), asset criticality (which PLC controls what process), and topology (which zone, which Purdue Level). - **Tier 3 — Hunting & Forensics:** Proactive threat hunting using hypotheses, plus root-cause investigation if an incident is confirmed. **Detection Methodologies (a KTU favorite):** | Methodology | How It Works | OT Suitability | Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | **Signature-based** | Matches known patterns (e.g., TRITON/Trisis malware IOCs) | High for known threats | Zero-day blind | | **Anomaly-based** | Statistical deviation from a learned baseline | High for OT (process is repetitive) | High FPR during seasonal shifts | | **Stateful / Protocol-aware** | Validates that a Modbus/DNP3 packet is semantically legal | Very high (best for OT) | Vendor-specific tuning needed | | **Behavioral (ML/AI)** | User-and-entity behavior analytics (UEBA) on EWS | Moderate | Requires clean training data | #### Pillar 3: Threat Response (The "Muscle") The response layer follows the **NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Lifecycle**: 1. **Preparation** — IR plan, playbooks, contact tree, isolated jump kits. 2. **Detection & Analysis** — Confirm the alert is a true positive. 3. **Containment, Eradication & Recovery** — Isolate, remove, restore. 4. **Post-Incident Activity** — Lessons learned, IOC update, rule tuning. > [!NOTE] > In OT, containment is **safety-first, not speed-first**. A network isolation that triggers an unsafe process shutdown (e.g., uncommanded release of a pressure relief valve) is a worse outcome than the cyber attack itself. This is a recurring KTU board question: *"Why can't we just 'pull the plug' on an infected OT system?"* ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **board-exam relevant**. Memorize the variables and units — the examiners love a 3-mark "define and compute" question on these. | # | Formula | Variable Legend | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $MTTD = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{detect,i} - t_{compromise,i})$ | $t_{detect}$ = detection timestamp, $t_{compromise}$ = compromise timestamp, $N$ = incidents | **Hours** or **Minutes** | | 2 | $MTTR = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{resolved,i} - t_{detect,i})$ | $t_{resolved}$ = incident closed timestamp | **Hours** | | 3 | $MTTC = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{contained,i} - t_{detect,i})$ | $t_{contained}$ = threat isolated timestamp | **Hours** | | 4 | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $FP$ = False Positives, $TN$ = True Negatives | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | | 5 | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | $FN$ = False Negatives, $TP$ = True Positives | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | | 6 | $TPR \ (\text{Recall}) = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Sensitivity / Detection Rate | Dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive Predictive Value | Dimensionless | | 8 | $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision ($P$) and Recall ($R$) | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | | 9 | $Risk\ Score = Likelihood \times Impact$ | Both rated 1–5 in qualitative scales | 1 to 25 (or 1–10 scale) | | 10 | $Detection\ Coverage = \dfrac{\text{Detected Threats}}{\text{Total Known Threats}} \times 100$ | Expressed as percentage | **%** | | 11 | $Alert\ Volume\ Reduction = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Actionable Alerts}}{\text{Raw Events}}$ | SIEM efficiency metric | 0 to 1 (higher is better) | | 12 | $SOC\ Analyst\ Workload = \dfrac{\text{Alerts per day}}{\text{Analyst count} \times \text{Shift hours}}$ | Used in staffing calculations | Alerts / analyst-hour | > [!NOTE] > In the table above, all vertical bar symbols used for absolute value (e.g., $|x|$) are written using the LaTeX `\vert` command to preserve Markdown table integrity. This is a strict KTU note-formatting rule. ### 2.3 Real-World Utility These metrics are not academic — they are contractual **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)** in modern Managed Security Service Providers (MSSPs) and OT-specific SOCs. For example, a utility company may contractually require $MTTD < 15$ minutes for Level 1 OT alerts. The formulas above are how those SLAs are measured and reported to regulators (e.g., NERC CIP-008 for North American power grids, CEA (Cyber Emergency) norms in India). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Confusion Matrix and F1 Score for an OT IDS **Problem Statement:** An OT Intrusion Detection System (IDS) was deployed at a water-treatment plant for 30 days. The following table summarizes its performance: | | **Actual Attack (Positive)** | **Actual Normal (Negative)** | |---|---|---| | **IDS Alerted (Positive)** | $TP = 42$ | $FP = 8$ | | **IDS Did Not Alert (Negative)** | $FN = 3$ | $TN = 947$ | Calculate the **False Positive Rate, False Negative Rate, Recall, Precision, and F1 Score**. Comment on the IDS's operational suitability for a safety-critical OT environment. #### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the totals.** $$N_{total} = TP + FP + FN + TN = 42 + 8 + 3 + 947 = 1000\ alerts$$ **Step 2 — Compute the False Positive Rate.** $$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$ Substituting the values: $$FPR = \frac{8}{8 + 947} = \frac{8}{955} = 0.00838$$ Expressed as a percentage: $$FPR \approx 0.84\%$$ **Step 3 — Compute the False Negative Rate.** $$FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$$ $$FNR = \frac{3}{3 + 42} = \frac{3}{45} = 0.0667$$ Expressed as a percentage: $$FNR \approx 6.67\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > In OT, **FNR is more dangerous than FPR** because a missed attack (FN) can lead to physical damage, while a false alarm (FP) only causes analyst fatigue. The 6.67% FNR here is borderline — the board will expect you to **recommend tuning** to bring it below 2%. **Step 4 — Compute Recall (True Positive Rate).** $$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{42}{42 + 3} = \frac{42}{45} = 0.9333$$ **Step 5 — Compute Precision.** $$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{42}{42 + 8} = \frac{42}{50} = 0.84$$ **Step 6 — Compute the F1 Score.** $$F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$$ Substituting $P = 0.84$ and $R = 0.9333$: $$F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.84 \times 0.9333}{0.84 + 0.9333}$$ Compute the numerator: $$0.84 \times 0.9333 = 0.7840$$ Compute the denominator: $$0.84 + 0.9333 = 1.7733$$ Therefore: $$F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7840}{1.7733} = 2 \cdot 0.4421 = 0.8842$$ **Step 7 — Interpret the result.** | Metric | Value | Operational Verdict | |---|---|---| | $FPR$ | 0.84% | Excellent (low alert fatigue) | | $FNR$ | 6.67% | **Concerning** for safety-critical OT | | $Recall$ | 93.33% | Good but not best-in-class | | $Precision$ | 84.00% | Good | | $F1$ | 88.42% | Acceptable, retune to > 95% | --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — MTTD and MTTR Calculation **Problem Statement:** A SOC analyst team recorded the following four incidents in a quarter. Compute the **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** and the **Mean Time To Respond (MTTR)** in hours. | Incident | Compromise Time | Detection Time | Resolved Time | |---|---|---|---| | I-01 | Day 1, 09:00 | Day 1, 11:30 | Day 2, 14:00 | | I-02 | Day 5, 02:00 | Day 5, 02:45 | Day 5, 06:00 | | I-03 | Day 12, 18:00 | Day 13, 06:00 | Day 14, 18:00 | | I-04 | Day 20, 12:00 | Day 20, 15:00 | Day 21, 09:00 | #### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute Time-to-Detect (TTD) for each incident.** $$TTD_{I-01} = 11\!:\!30 - 09\!:\!00 = 2.5\ hours$$ $$TTD_{I-02} = 02\!:\!45 - 02\!:\!00 = 0.75\ hours$$ $$TTD_{I-03} = 06\!:\!00_{(Day\ 13)} - 18\!:\!00_{(Day\ 12)} = 12.0\ hours$$ $$TTD_{I-04} = 15\!:\!00 - 12\!:\!00 = 3.0\ hours$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Mean Time To Detect.** $$MTTD = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} TTD_i$$ $$MTTD = \frac{2.5 + 0.75 + 12.0 + 3.0}{4} = \frac{18.25}{4} = 4.5625\ hours$$ **Step 3 — Compute Time-to-Resolve (TTR) for each incident.** $$TTR_{I-01} = 14\!:\!00_{(Day\ 2)} - 11\!:\!30_{(Day\ 1)} = 26.5\ hours$$ $$TTR_{I-02} = 06\!:\!00 - 02\!:\!45 = 3.25\ hours$$ $$TTR_{I-03} = 18\!:\!00_{(Day\ 14)} - 06\!:\!00_{(Day\ 13)} = 36.0\ hours$$ $$TTR_{I-04} = 09\!:\!00_{(Day\ 21)} - 15\!:\!00_{(Day\ 20)} = 18.0\ hours$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Mean Time To Respond.** $$MTTR = \frac{26.5 + 3.25 + 36.0 + 18.0}{4} = \frac{83.75}{4} = 20.9375\ hours$$ **Step 5 — Interpret against SLAs.** If the plant's contractual SLA is $MTTD \leq 4$ hours and $MTTR \leq 24$ hours, the team is **just above SLA on MTTD (4.56 h)** and **within SLA on MTTR (20.94 h)**. The board expects a recommendation: **tune detection rules for Incident I-03** (which alone contributed 12 h to the mean). --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Detection Engineering Trade-offs > [!NOTE] > This matrix is a KTU-style synthesis students are expected to produce in 14-mark Part B questions. It compares the **three dominant detection approaches** in OT. | Dimension | Signature-based | Anomaly-based | Protocol-aware / DPI | |---|---|---|---| | **Detection of known threats** | Excellent | Good | Excellent | | **Detection of zero-days** | Poor | Excellent | Moderate | | **False Positive Rate** | Low (1–3%) | High (10–25%) | Very Low (<1%) | | **False Negative Rate** | High for novel attacks | Low | Low | | **OT-friendliness (passive)** | Yes | Yes | Yes (most relevant) | | **Computational cost** | Very low | Moderate to high | High (deep parsing) | | **Tuning effort** | Low | High | Moderate | | **Best use case** | Known malware, IT-OT pivot | Insider threat, slow attacks | Unauthorized commands on Modbus/DNP3 | | **KTU example attack** | TRITON/TRISIS hash detection | Stuxnet-like payload deviation | Writing to a read-only register | ### 3.4 Step-by-Step Incident Response Procedure (OT-Specific) The following is the **canonical, board-correct procedure** for handling an OT incident. Memorize it. **Phase 1 — Preparation (Pre-incident)** - Maintain an isolated **IR jump kit** (laptop with forensic tools, never connected to the corporate network). - Pre-define **playbooks** for the top 5 OT scenarios (ransomware on EWS, unauthorized PLC programming, HMI screen takeover, historian exfiltration, safety-instrumented-system tampering). - Conduct **table-top exercises** every quarter. **Phase 2 — Detection & Analysis** - Triage the alert: is it a true positive? Use the **confusion matrix metrics** (Section 3.1). - Determine the **Purdue Level** of the affected asset (Level 0–1 = process; Level 2 = control; Level 3 = operations; Level 3.5 = DMZ; Level 4–5 = enterprise). - Engage **process engineers** early — they understand whether the anomaly is cyber or a legitimate process disturbance. **Phase 3 — Containment, Eradication & Recovery** - **Short-term containment:** Network-level isolation (block VLAN, ACL on firewall). **Do not** power-cycle PLCs. - **Eradication:** Re-image Windows EWS, reset PLC ladder logic to last known-good backup, rotate credentials, patch CVE. - **Recovery:** Validate safety functions, run the process in **manual mode** for a defined period, return to automatic after verification. **Phase 4 — Post-Incident Activity** - Write the **Incident Report** (timeline, root cause, IOCs, lessons learned). - Update the **SIEM correlation rules** to catch the same attack pattern. - Update the **asset inventory** and **network diagram**. - Brief the **safety board** and, if regulated, the **sectoral CERT** (e.g., CERT-In in India). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 OT Monitoring, Analysis & Response — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph lvl0["Purdue Level 0 to 1 - Physical Process"] sensorA["Temperature Sensor"] sensorB["Pressure Sensor"] actuatorA["Motor Actuator"] end subgraph lvl2["Purdue Level 2 - Control"] plcA["PLC Controller A"] plcB["PLC Controller B"] rtuA["RTU Unit"] end subgraph lvl3["Purdau Level 3 - Operations"] hmiA["HMI Workstation"] scadaA["SCADA Server"] histA["Process Historian"] end subgraph dmz["Purdue Level 3.5 - Industrial DMZ"] jumpA["Jump Server"] patchA["WSUS Patch Relay"] end subgraph mon["Monitoring Layer - Passive Sensors"] tapA["Network TAP SPAN"] dpiA["ICS DPI Sensor"] siemA["SIEM Collector"] end subgraph ana["Analysis Layer - SOC"] tier1A["Tier 1 Alert Triage"] tier2A["Tier 2 Correlation and Enrichment"] tier3A["Tier 3 Threat Hunting and Forensics"] tiA["Threat Intelligence Platform"] end subgraph resp["Response Layer - SOAR and IR"] soaA["SOAR Playbook Engine"] irA["Incident Response Team"] safeA["Safety and Process Engineer"] end sensorA --> plcA sensorB --> plcB actuatorA --> plcA plcA --> hmiA plcB --> hmiA rtuA --> scadaA hmiA --> scadaA scadaA --> histA hmiA --> jumpA jumpA --> patchA plcA --> tapA plcB --> tapA scadaA --> tapA tapA --> dpiA dpiA --> siemA siemA --> tier1A tiA --> tier2A tier1A --> tier2A tier2A --> tier3A tier3A --> soaA tier1A --> soaA soaA --> irA irA --> safeA safeA --> plcA ``` ### 4.2 NIST Incident Response Lifecycle (OT-Adapted) ```mermaid flowchart LR prepA["Preparation
Playbooks and IR Kit"] --> detA detA["Detection and Analysis
SIEM Tier 1 to Tier 3"] --> contA contA["Containment
Network Isolation and VLAN Block"] --> eraA eraA["Eradication
Reimage and Patch"] --> recA recA["Recovery
Manual Mode to Auto Mode"] --> postA postA["Post Incident
Lessons Learned and Rule Update"] --> prepA ``` ### 4.3 Threat Intelligence Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TB planA["Planning and Direction
Define Intelligence Requirements"] --> collA collA["Collection
OSINT ISAC Vendor Feeds"] --> procA procA["Processing
Normalize Deduplicate"] --> anaA anaA["Analysis
Correlate with Internal Telemetry"] --> dissA dissA["Dissemination
Push to SIEM and Firewalls"] --> feedA feedA["Feedback
Refine Requirements"] --> planA ``` ### 4.4 Alert Correlation Flow (SIEM Internal Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TB rawA["Raw Events from OT Sensors"] --> normA["Normalization to Common Schema"] normA --> corrA["Correlation Engine
Rule Based and ML"] corrA --> enrA["Enrichment
Asset Criticality Threat Intel"] enrA --> priorA["Prioritization
Risk Score and Severity"] priorA --> triA["Triage Queue for SOC Analyst"] triA --> decA{"True Positive?"} decA -- Yes --> soaA["Trigger SOAR Playbook"] decA -- No --> tuneA["Tune Rule and Update Feedback Loop"] soaA --> irA["Incident Response Activation"] tuneA --> corrA ``` > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams above use the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** style mandated by the V10 protocol for topics that involve complex physical drawings (e.g., plant layouts, sensor placements). Node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, and contain no reserved Mermaid keywords, ensuring clean compilation in any Markdown renderer. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define the terms Monitoring, Analysis, and Threat Response in the context of OT cybersecurity. Why are these three treated as a single integrated discipline in modern Security Operation Centers?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Monitoring (1 mark):** The continuous, passive collection of security-relevant telemetry — network packets, host logs, process variables, and authentication events — from Purdue Levels 0–3.5 of an OT environment, without active probing that could disturb deterministic control loops. - **Analysis (1 mark):** The cognitive and computational process of correlating monitored events, applying signature-, anomaly-, and protocol-aware detection logic, and enriching alerts with threat intelligence and asset criticality to determine true positives. - **Threat Response (1 mark):** The pre-planned, coordinated execution of containment, eradication, and recovery actions — orchestrated via SOAR playbooks and guided by safety-first OT principles — to neutralize confirmed threats while preserving process integrity. They are treated as one discipline because **isolated monitoring produces alert fatigue, isolated analysis produces uninformed decisions, and isolated response is reckless** — only their tight integration delivers actionable, safe, and timely defense. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *List and briefly explain any four Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) used to measure the effectiveness of an OT Security Operations Center.* **Model Answer (4 × 0.75 = 3 marks):** 1. **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect)** — Average lag between an attack's compromise and its detection by the SOC. Lower is better. 2. **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond/Recover)** — Average time from detection to full resolution. Lower is better. 3. **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — Fraction of benign events wrongly flagged as malicious. Drives analyst fatigue. 4. **Detection Coverage** — Percentage of known threat techniques (mapped to MITRE ATT&CK for ICS) that the SOC can detect. 5. *(Bonus point if asked for 4, but board typically asks for any 4)* — **MTTC (Mean Time To Contain)** — Time from detection to threat isolation. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Lifecycle in detail. For each phase, describe **two OT-specific considerations** that differentiate OT incident response from traditional IT incident response. **(b) [7 Marks]** A newly deployed OT IDS at a manufacturing plant produced the following one-month statistics: TP = 180, FP = 20, FN = 12, TN = 9788. Compute the FPR, FNR, Precision, Recall, and F1 Score. Comment on whether the IDS is operationally suitable for a safety-critical plant and recommend **two specific tuning actions**. --- **Model Answer for Question A:** **(a) NIST IR Lifecycle with OT-Specific Considerations (7 marks = 1 mark per phase + 0.5 marks per OT-specific point × 4 = 7)** **1. Preparation (1 mark):** - Definition: Establishing IR capability, plans, communications, and tooling **before** an incident. - *OT consideration 1 (0.5):* IR jump kit must be **air-gapped** and pre-loaded with offline forensic tools, as OT networks rarely have internet access for tool download during a crisis. - *OT consideration 2 (0.5):* Playbooks must be **co-signed by process/safety engineers** — IR actions that bypass safety PLCs (SIS) are illegal under IEC 61511. **2. Detection & Analysis (1 mark):** - Definition: Identifying precursors and indicators, correlating events, and confirming incidents. - *OT consideration 1 (0.5):* Analysts must distinguish **cyber anomalies from process upsets** — a pressure spike may be a stuck valve, not Stuxnet. - *OT consideration 2 (0.5):* Detection must use **protocol-aware sensors** (Modbus, DNP3) — generic IT IDS misses ICS-specific attacks. **3. Containment, Eradication & Recovery (1 mark):** - Definition: Limiting damage, removing the threat, and restoring systems. - *OT consideration 1 (0.5):* **No "pull the plug"** — abrupt shutdowns can damage equipment (e.g., pump cavitation, reactor runaway). - *OT consideration 2 (0.5):* **Preserve PLC ladder logic** as forensic evidence before re-flashing; PLC programs are intellectual property and evidence. **4. Post-Incident Activity (1 mark):** - Definition: Documenting, learning, and improving. - *OT consideration 1 (0.5):* Findings must be shared with **safety authorities and sector CERTs** (mandatory in regulated sectors). - *OT consideration 2 (0.5):* Update the **OT asset inventory and network diagram** — OT environments change slowly, so post-incident updates are durable improvements. --- **(b) Confusion Matrix Calculation (7 marks = 1 mark per metric + 1.5 marks for recommendation)** **Given:** $TP = 180,\ FP = 20,\ FN = 12,\ TN = 9788$. **Step 1 — False Positive Rate (1 mark):** $$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{20}{20 + 9788} = \frac{20}{9808} = 0.00204 = 0.20\%$$ **Step 2 — False Negative Rate (1 mark):** $$FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP} = \frac{12}{12 + 180} = \frac{12}{192} = 0.0625 = 6.25\%$$ **Step 3 — Precision (1 mark):** $$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90$$ **Step 4 — Recall (1 mark):** $$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 12} = \frac{180}{192} = 0.9375$$ **Step 5 — F1 Score (1 mark):** $$F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.90 \times 0.9375}{0.90 + 0.9375} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.84375}{1.8375} = 2 \cdot 0.4592 = 0.9184$$ **Step 6 — Operational Commentary and Tuning Recommendations (2 marks):** The F1 score of **0.9184 (91.84%)** is acceptable but not best-in-class for a safety-critical plant.
