The **FNR of 6.25%** is the primary concern, as 12 missed attacks could cause physical damage. **Tuning actions:** 1. **Tighten anomaly thresholds** using the first 14 days of clean baseline data to reduce false negatives on slow reconnaissance activities. 2. **Add protocol-aware signatures** for the specific PLCs in use (e.g., Allen-Bradley, Siemens S7) — the current IDS may be IT-centric and missing ICS-layer attacks. 3. **Implement Tier-2 correlation** to combine low-severity events that, together, indicate an attack (reduces FNR through chain detection). --- #### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam Style, 2024 Scheme] **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of an OT Security Operations Center**. Label and explain the responsibilities of the **Monitoring, Analysis, and Response** layers. Highlight the unique role of **protocol-aware DPI** in OT monitoring. **(b) [7 Marks]** During a six-month period, an OT SOC recorded five major incidents. The TTD and TTR (in hours) are: (12, 30), (2, 8), (24, 60), (6, 14), (3, 10). Calculate the **MTTD, MTTR, and MTTC** (assume $t_{contain} = t_{detect} + \frac{1}{2} \times TTR$ for this calculation). Evaluate whether the team meets an industry SLA of MTTD ≤ 6 hours and MTTR ≤ 24 hours. --- **Model Answer for Question B:** **(a) OT SOC Architecture (7 marks):** - **Block diagram (3 marks):** A four-layer architecture: *Assets* → *Sensors* → *Analysis* → *Response*. *(Draw a labeled block diagram similar to the Mermaid in Section 4.1. If hand-drawn, mark each layer with arrows showing data flow upward and actions flowing downward.)* - **Monitoring Layer (1.5 marks):** Passive SPAN taps, ICS-aware DPI sensors, syslog forwarders from EWS and Historian. These collect network packets, host logs, and process variables without active probing. **Protocol-aware DPI** parses the payload of industrial protocols (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 104, OPC UA) and validates that each command is semantically legal in the current process state — e.g., flagging a "write to register" when the system is in read-only mode. - **Analysis Layer (1.5 marks):** SIEM ingestion and correlation, Tier-1 triage, Tier-2 enrichment with threat intel (e.g., Dragos, Mandiant ICS feeds) and asset criticality, Tier-3 threat hunting and forensics. Outputs actionable, prioritized alerts. - **Response Layer (1 mark):** SOAR playbooks for automated first-response (e.g., VLAN quarantine, account disable, snapshot capture), escalation to human IR team, and coordination with safety/process engineers for any action that affects the physical process. **(b) MTTD, MTTR, MTTC Calculation (7 marks = 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 for commentary):** **Step 1 — MTTD (2 marks):** $$MTTD = \frac{12 + 2 + 24 + 6 + 3}{5} = \frac{47}{5} = 9.4\ hours$$ **Step 2 — MTTR (2 marks):** $$MTTR = \frac{30 + 8 + 60 + 14 + 10}{5} = \frac{122}{5} = 24.4\ hours$$ **Step 3 — MTTC (1 mark):** $$MTTC = \frac{1}{5} \sum \left( t_{detect} + \frac{1}{2} TTR \right) = \frac{1}{5}\sum\left( TTD_i + 0.5 \cdot TTR_i \right)$$ | Incident | $TTD$ | $TTR$ | $TTC = TTD + 0.5 \cdot TTR$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 12 | 30 | $12 + 15 = 27$ | | 2 | 2 | 8 | $2 + 4 = 6$ | | 3 | 24 | 60 | $24 + 30 = 54$ | | 4 | 6 | 14 | $6 + 7 = 13$ | | 5 | 3 | 10 | $3 + 5 = 8$ | $$MTTC = \frac{27 + 6 + 54 + 13 + 8}{5} = \frac{108}{5} = 21.6\ hours$$ **Step 4 — SLA Evaluation (2 marks):** | Metric | Computed | SLA | Status | |---|---|---|---| | MTTD | 9.4 h | ≤ 6 h | **Violated** | | MTTR | 24.4 h | ≤ 24 h | **Violated (marginal)** | | MTTC | 21.6 h | (No SLA given) | — | **Conclusion:** The team **fails both SLAs**. The dominant contributor is Incident 3 (TTD = 24 h, TTR = 60 h) — an outlier that must be root-caused. **Recommendations:** (i) tune SIEM rules for the attack pattern observed in Incident 3, (ii) pre-stage IR tooling and access at the affected site, (iii) conduct a lessons-learned review for Incident 3 specifically. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make in this topic (and where the board cuts marks):** > > 1. **Confusing MTTR with MTTC** — Many students use them interchangeably. MTTR is full resolution; MTTC is only containment. The examiner specifically tests this distinction. > 2. **Suggesting "unplug the infected OT system" as a containment strategy** — This is the **#1 answer that gets zero marks** for OT. Always qualify with "isolate at the network level first, then coordinate with safety engineers." > 3. **Using FPR and FNR interchangeably** — They are complements, not synonyms. Get the formula direction right: $FPR = FP/(FP+TN)$, $FNR = FN/(FN+TP)$. > 4. **Forgetting the OT-unique constraint: no active scanning.** Mentioning "run a Nessus scan on the OT network" as a monitoring technique loses full marks for that sub-question. > 5. **Skipping the formula units in the final answer** — Always state hours, percentage, or dimensionless in the conclusion line. The board deducts 0.5 marks for missing units. > 6. **Writing generic IT-SOC answers** — The KTU board for this specific course (PECCT634) penalizes generic IT answers. Use OT-specific terminology: Purdue Levels, ICS protocols, SIS, historian, EWS, safety PLCs. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Monitoring, Analysis, and Threat Response (Module 3):** > > - Monitoring, Analysis, and Response is the **SOC triad** — they are inseparable; an integrated SOC delivers them as one pipeline. > - The **four detection methodologies** are signature-based, anomaly-based, stateful/protocol-aware, and behavioral (ML/UEBA). Know the trade-offs of each. > - **Protocol-aware DPI** is the gold standard for OT monitoring — it parses Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, EtherNet/IP, PROFINET, and OPC UA payloads. > - **Active scanning is forbidden** on live OT networks (ISA/IEC 62443). Use passive SPAN taps. > - **MTTD, MTTR, MTTC, FPR, FNR, Precision, Recall, F1** are the eight formulas that will appear on the exam. Memorize the variable legend for each. > - **FNR is more critical than FPR** in OT — a missed attack can cause physical damage; a false alarm only causes analyst fatigue. > - The **NIST IR Lifecycle** has four phases: Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment/Eradication/Recovery → Post-Incident Activity. > - The **OT-specific IR rule**: never power-cycle PLCs; never bypass Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS); always preserve ladder logic as evidence. > - The **Threat Intelligence Lifecycle** has six steps: Planning → Collection → Processing → Analysis → Dissemination → Feedback. > - **Mermaid-friendly block diagrams** (functional architecture, IR lifecycle, TI lifecycle) are board-acceptable substitutes for physical drawings. > - **Risk Score = Likelihood × Impact** (both 1–5, giving a 1–25 range) is the qualitative scoring formula used in OT asset criticality. > - **Detection Coverage** against **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** is the modern KPI — know at least three techniques from the ATT&CK for ICS matrix (e.g., T0836 Modify Parameter, T0855 Unauthorized Command Message, T0832 Manipulation of View). > - **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response)** is the automation layer of the response pillar — know that it executes playbooks that integrate SIEM, firewall, EDR, and ticketing systems. > - **Asset criticality + threat intel + topology context** is what differentiates a Tier-2 OT analyst from a Tier-1 IT analyst. Mention this in any "differentiate" question.

topics.title: Monitoring and Threat Detection: Importance of continuous monitoring in integrated OT-IT networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Monitoring and Threat Detection: Importance of continuous monitoring in integrated OT-IT networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Continuous Monitoring (CM)** in the context of integrated **Operational Technology (OT)** and **Information Technology (IT)** networks is defined as the persistent, real-time, and automated observation, collection, correlation, and analysis of security-relevant data from heterogeneous network assets, endpoints, control systems (PLCs, RTUs, SCADA, DCS), sensors, logs, and communication channels to detect, alert on, and respond to cyber threats, operational anomalies, policy violations, and process deviations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Continuous monitoring is the *uninterrupted vigilance* mechanism that provides situational awareness across the **IT/OT convergence plane**, ensuring that security telemetry from corporate IT systems, industrial control systems (ICS), and physical process layers are unified, normalized, and analyzed through a single analytical lens (typically a **SIEM** or **SOAR** platform).

Formally, a continuous monitoring program is governed by the **NIST SP 800-137** specification, which defines it as the *"ongoing awareness of information security, vulnerabilities, and threats to support organizational risk management decisions."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Hospital ICU Monitor" for Industry

Imagine a large industrial chemical plant is like a **patient in a hospital ICU (Intensive Care Unit)**:

- **The patient (OT network)** has vital organs (PLCs, RTUs, sensors) producing signals (telemetry, control commands, process variables).
- **The IT network** is the hospital's administrative and record-keeping system.
- **Continuous monitoring** is the **ICU monitor** — it constantly tracks heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation, and temperature. The moment any value goes abnormal, it **beeps** (alert), **logs** the event (log retention), and notifies the **nurse/doctor** (SOC analyst).
- If the monitor is **disconnected**, the patient may "silently" deteriorate — just like a **cyberattack on an OT network** can cause process damage, equipment failure, or even physical harm without any visible sign until it is too late.

This is precisely why continuous monitoring is **non-negotiable** in integrated OT-IT environments — it converts invisible digital behavior into actionable, time-stamped security intelligence.

## 1.3 Why Continuous Monitoring is Critical in OT-IT Networks

| Aspect | Pure IT Network | Integrated OT-IT Network |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Asset | Data confidentiality | **Safety, availability, process integrity** |
| Downtime Tolerance | Hours acceptable | **Seconds to minutes can be catastrophic** |
| Asset Lifetime | 3–5 years | **15–30 years (legacy systems)** |
| Patch Cadence | Frequent | **Rare / disruptive** |
| Monitoring Priority | Confidentiality leaks | **Anomalies in control commands** |

> [!NOTE]
> In OT environments, the famous cybersecurity triad shifts to the **AIC triad** — **Availability, Integrity, Confidentiality** — and continuous monitoring primarily defends **Availability and Integrity** because human safety and physical production depend on them.

## 1.4 Key Monitoring Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

- **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect):** Average time between compromise and detection. Industry benchmark: **< 24 hours** for IT, **< 1 hour** for critical OT zones.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond):** Average time between detection and containment.
- **FPR (False Positive Rate):** Noise level in alerts.
- **Coverage:** Percentage of assets under active monitoring (target: **100%** for crown-jewel OT assets).
- **TPR (True Positive Rate) / Detection Rate:** Effectiveness of detection rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Highlight (KTU Board Favourite):** *Continuous monitoring is the difference between an **incident** and a **disaster** in OT environments. IT breaches lose data; OT breaches can lose lives.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Continuous Monitoring (NIST SP 800-137)

1. **Asset Discovery & Inventory** — Knowing *what* is on the network (passive vs. active scanning).
2. **Vulnerability & Configuration Management** — Knowing *how weak* each asset is.
3. **Event & Log Collection (Telemetry Pipeline)** — Knowing *what is happening*.
4. **Analysis, Correlation & Response** — Knowing *what it means and what to do*.

## 2.2 Architectural Layers of OT-IT Continuous Monitoring

The integrated monitoring architecture is a **defense-in-depth stack** consisting of:

- **Layer 1 — Physical & Field Devices:** Sensors, actuators, smart instruments (Modbus, HART, Profibus protocols).
- **Layer 2 — Control Layer:** PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers.
- **Layer 3 — Supervisory Layer:** SCADA HMIs, Engineering Workstations, Historian servers.
- **Layer 4 — Operations & IT Convergence Layer:** MES, ERP, jump servers, remote access.
- **Layer 5 — Enterprise IT & Cloud:** Email, identity (Active Directory), EDR feeds.
- **Central Analytical Platform:** SIEM + SOAR + Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP).
- **Human Layer:** 24×7 SOC (Security Operations Center) analysts.

## 2.3 Threat Detection Methodologies

Continuous monitoring enables **two fundamental detection paradigms**:

### A. Signature-Based Detection
- Compares observed events against a **known-bad pattern database** (e.g., Snort rules, YARA rules, ICS-CERT advisories).
- High precision, low false positives, but **zero-day blind**.
- Example: Detecting a known **TRITON/Trisis** malware hash on an engineering workstation.

### B. Anomaly-Based Detection (Behavior-Based)
- Builds a **statistical baseline** of "normal" network and process behavior.
- Flags deviations (e.g., a PLC suddenly receiving a firmware update at 3 AM, or a Modbus register being written that has never been written before).
- Catches **zero-days** and **insider threats**, but has higher false positives.

### C. Hybrid / Stateful Detection
- Combines signatures + behavior + threat intelligence + ICS protocol awareness.
- Used in modern **ICS-aware IDS** like Claroty, Nozomi, Dragos, and Microsoft Defender for IoT.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Mathematical Form | Description | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Time to Detect | $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_{\text{detect},i} - T_{\text{incident},i})}{n}$ | Average delay between incident occurrence and detection | Hours; KTU target $\leq 1$ hr for OT |
| Mean Time to Respond | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_{\text{resolved},i} - T_{\text{detect},i})}{n}$ | Average delay between detection and containment | Hours |
| False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}}$ | Ratio of false alarms to total actual negatives | 0 to 1; goal $\leq 0.05$ |
| True Positive Rate (Recall) | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | Detection effectiveness | 0 to 1; goal $\geq 0.95$ |
| Precision | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | Quality of alerts | 0 to 1 |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{TPR}}{\text{Precision} + \text{TPR}}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall | 0 to 1 |
| Risk Score | $R = P \times I \times (1 - C)$ | Risk = Probability × Impact × (1 - Control strength) | Qualitative / Numeric |
| Asset Coverage | $\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{N_{\text{monitored}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | % of assets under active monitoring | $\%$; target 100% for critical |
| Log Retention | $L_{\text{retention}} \geq 365$ days | Minimum log storage (NIST/PCI-DSS) | Days |
| Detection Velocity | $V_d = \dfrac{N_{\text{incidents detected}}}{T_{\text{window}}}$ | Detection throughput | Incidents / hour |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Point:** Absolute value bars in formulas above use $\vert$ and $\mid$ — never use the unescaped vertical pipe $\vert$ inside markdown tables, as it breaks the table parser.

## 2.5 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering Practice

- **MTTD/MTTR** directly translate to **financial loss avoidance** — every hour of undetected compromise in a power grid can cost millions.
- **FPR management** prevents **alert fatigue**, which is the #1 reason SOC analysts miss real attacks.
- **Asset Coverage** ensures **no blind spots** in Purdue Model Level 0–1 zones where physical damage originates.
- **Risk Score $R$** drives **prioritization** of remediation budgets in industrial plants.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids:** Continuous monitoring detects False Data Injection (FDI) attacks on smart meters.
- **Manufacturing:** Detects manipulation of robotic arm trajectories (kinematic anomalies).
- **Oil & Gas (Upstream):** Monitors wellhead RTUs for unauthorized setpoint changes.
- **Water Treatment:** Detects chemical dosing attacks (e.g., the Oldsmar, Florida water plant attack of 2021).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing the Risk Score $R$ for an OT Asset

The KTU 2024 board often asks students to **compute the risk score** of an OT asset given probability, impact, and control strength. Below is the exhaustive derivation.

### Step 1 — Define the Three Input Parameters

- Let $P$ be the **probability of threat occurrence** (0 to 1).
- Let $I$ be the **impact severity** (0 to 1, where 1 = catastrophic).
- Let $C$ be the **strength of existing controls** (0 to 1, where 1 = fully controlled).

### Step 2 — Define the Risk Function

The risk is intuitively: *likelihood × consequence*, reduced by the effectiveness of the existing control. The reduction factor is $(1 - C)$ because if $C = 1$ (perfect control), risk becomes zero.

$$R = P \times I \times (1 - C)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Evaluate

Assume an OT historian server has:
- $P = 0.6$ (60% chance of being targeted due to its exposure on the DMZ)
- $I = 0.9$ (catastrophic impact — historical data loss can halt production for weeks)
- $C = 0.3$ (only basic antivirus, no network segmentation)

### Step 4 — Algebraic Substitution

Substitute each numeric value into the equation:

$$R = (0.6) \times (0.9) \times (1 - 0.3)$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the Inner Bracket

$$(1 - 0.3) = 0.7$$

### Step 6 — Multiply the First Two Terms

$$0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54$$

### Step 7 — Final Multiplication

$$R = 0.54 \times 0.7$$

$$\boxed{R = 0.378}$$

### Step 8 — Interpretation Scale

A risk score of 0.378 falls in the **"Medium-High"** band per the standard industrial risk matrix (Critical $\geq 0.50$, High $\geq 0.25$, Medium $\geq 0.10$, Low $< 0.10$).

**Conclusion:** The historian server requires **immediate** deployment of a continuous monitoring agent (e.g., ICS-aware NDR), jump-server enforcement, and network micro-segmentation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) for a Plant

### Step 1 — Define the Incident Timeline

| Incident $i$ | $T_{\text{incident}}$ (timestamp) | $T_{\text{detect}}$ (timestamp) | Delta (hours) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2024-01-10 02:00 | 2024-01-10 04:30 | 2.5 |
| 2 | 2024-01-11 14:00 | 2024-01-12 11:00 | 21.0 |
| 3 | 2024-01-15 22:15 | 2024-01-15 23:00 | 0.75 |

### Step 2 — Write the Generic MTTD Formula

$$\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_{\text{detect},i} - T_{\text{incident},i})}{n}$$

### Step 3 — Expand the Summation

For $n = 3$ incidents:

$$\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{(T_{\text{detect},1} - T_{\text{incident},1}) + (T_{\text{detect},2} - T_{\text{incident},2}) + (T_{\text{detect},3} - T_{\text{incident},3})}{3}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Time Deltas

$$\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{2.5 + 21.0 + 0.75}{3}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Numerator

$$2.5 + 21.0 + 0.75 = 24.25$$

### Step 6 — Perform Final Division

$$\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{24.25}{3} = 8.083\ \text{hours}$$

### Step 7 — Round and Interpret

$$\boxed{\text{MTTD} \approx 8.08\ \text{hours}}$$

This is far above the KTU-recommended **< 1 hour** target for critical OT zones, indicating an urgent need for **24×7 SOC operations** and **automated correlation rules**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Detection Effectiveness Trade-off (ROC Concept)

The KTU board may ask how to compute **precision, recall, and F1-score** for an OT IDS.

### Step 1 — Define the Confusion Matrix Counts

Assume in a 7-day window, an ICS-aware IDS produces:
- $\text{TP} = 45$ (real attacks correctly flagged)
- $\text{FP} = 5$ (legitimate traffic wrongly flagged)
- $\text{FN} = 3$ (real attacks missed)
- $\text{TN} = 947$ (legitimate traffic correctly passed)

### Step 2 — Compute Total Actuals and Predictions

$$\text{Actual Positives} = \text{TP} + \text{FN} = 45 + 3 = 48$$

$$\text{Predicted Positives} = \text{TP} + \text{FP} = 45 + 5 = 50$$

### Step 3 — Compute Precision

$$\text{Precision} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} = \dfrac{45}{50} = 0.90$$

### Step 4 — Compute Recall (TPR)

$$\text{Recall} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \dfrac{45}{48} = 0.9375$$

### Step 5 — Compute F1-Score

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute Numeric Values

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.90 \times 0.9375}{0.90 + 0.9375}$$

### Step 7 — Multiply Numerator

$$0.90 \times 0.9375 = 0.84375$$

### Step 8 — Add Denominator

$$0.90 + 0.9375 = 1.8375$$

### Step 9 — Final Division and Scaling

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.84375}{1.8375} = 2 \times 0.4592 = 0.9184$$

$$\boxed{F_1 \approx 0.92}$$

**Interpretation:** The IDS has **excellent detection balance** (F1 > 0.9), suitable for production deployment in OT zones.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Continuous Monitoring Architecture for Integrated OT-IT Networks

The diagram below depicts the **end-to-end monitoring pipeline** from the physical field layer up to the SOC analyst, following the **Purdue Reference Model** layered approach.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FieldLayer: Sensors and Actuators]:::field --> B[ControlLayer: PLCs and RTUs and DCS]:::control
    B --> C[SupervisoryLayer: SCADA HMI and Engineering Workstations and Historian]:::super
    C --> D[DMZConvergence: Jump Server and Firewalls]:::dmz
    D --> E[EnterpriseITLayer: AD and ERP and Email and EDR]:::ent
    E --> F[CentralLogCollector: Syslog and WinRM and ICS Protocol Tap]:::collector
    F --> G[SIEMPlatform: Correlation and Dashboards]:::siem
    F --> H[ICSAwareNDR: Passive Deep Packet Inspection]:::ndr
    F --> I[EDRCloud: Endpoint Telemetry]:::edr
    G --> J[SOCAnalyst: 24x7 Triage and Incident Response]:::soc
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[SOAREngine: Automated Playbook Execution]:::soar
    K --> L[ThreatIntelPlatform: STIX and TAXII Feeds]:::tip
    L --> G
    K --> M[ResponseActions: Host Isolation and Network Block]:::response
    M --> A

    classDef control fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f59f00,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Threat Detection Decision Flow

This diagram illustrates the **logical decision flow** inside a SIEM correlation engine when processing a single OT alert.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RawLogIngested] --> B{IsSourceAnOTAsset?}
    B -- No --> C[RouteToITSecurityWorkflow]
    B -- Yes --> D{IsProtocolICS}
    D -- No --> E[QuarantineandAlert]
    D -- Yes --> F{AnomalyScoreAboveThreshold}
    F -- No --> G[LogandBaselineUpdate]
    F -- Yes --> H{CrossCorrelateWithThreatIntel}
    H -- NoMatch --> I[SOCAnalystManualTriage]
    H -- Match --> J[TriggerSOARPlaybook]
    I --> J
    J --> K[ContainmentActionsTaken]
    K --> L[PostIncidentReviewandForensics]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Fallback Description)

For students who find Mermaid complex, here is the textual mapping of the same architecture:

1. **Telemetry Sources (bottom-up):** Field → Control → Supervisory → DMZ → Enterprise IT.
2. **Collection Layer:** Logs and packets are tapped non-invasively and forwarded.
3. **Analytical Layer:** SIEM correlates; NDR performs deep packet inspection of Modbus/Profinet/DNP3; EDR monitors endpoint behavior.
4. **Decision Layer:** SOC analyst reviews; SOAR executes playbooks for known patterns.
5. **Response Layer:** Automated isolation, blocking, and forensic preservation.
6. **Feedback Loop:** Threat intelligence and lessons learned feed back into SIEM rules and NDR baselines.

> [!NOTE]
> The **feedback loop** is what makes monitoring *continuous* — it is not a one-time scan; it is a self-improving cyber defense system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**

### Question 1: Define Continuous Monitoring in the context of integrated OT-IT networks. Why is it more critical in OT than in pure IT environments? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Continuous Monitoring is the persistent, real-time observation and analysis of security events across an organization's IT and OT assets to detect threats, vulnerabilities, and policy violations. In OT environments, it is **more critical** than in pure IT because OT systems directly control physical processes where cyber incidents can lead to **loss of human life, environmental damage, and production shutdown**, unlike IT where the primary impact is data loss. Additionally, OT systems have **longer lifespans, infrequent patching, and legacy protocols** that lack native security, making real-time monitoring the primary defense layer.

*(Expected length: 8–10 lines. Board expects the AIC vs CIA triad distinction.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between signature-based and anomaly-based detection in OT networks. Give one example of each. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Matches against known attack patterns | Detects deviations from learned baseline |
| Zero-Day Coverage | Poor | Good |
| False Positives | Low | Higher |
| Example (OT) | Detecting TRITON malware hash on HMI | Alerting on PLC receiving firmware write at 3 AM |

*(Board expects at least one concrete OT example like Modbus, DNP3, or TRITON/Stuxnet.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**

### Question A: Continuous Monitoring in Integrated OT-IT Networks (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the architectural components of a continuous monitoring system for an integrated OT-IT network. Discuss the role of SIEM, ICS-aware NDR, and SOC. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A continuous monitoring system for integrated OT-IT networks is a **layered, defense-in-depth architecture** comprising the following components:

1. **Telemetry Collection Layer:**
   - **Passive Network Taps** at Purdue Levels 0–3 capture ICS traffic (Modbus TCP, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, Profinet, EtherNet/IP) without disrupting operations.
   - **Agent-based log forwarders** (syslog, Windows Event Forwarding) collect logs from engineering workstations, historians, and HMI servers.
   - **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)** agents on Windows-based OT assets provide process, registry, and file telemetry.

2. **Analytical Layer — SIEM (Security Information and Event Management):**
   - Normalizes and correlates logs from IT (firewall, AD, VPN) and OT (PLC, HMI, historian).
   - Applies **correlation rules** (e.g., multiple failed logins on an engineering workstation + unusual outbound traffic to a Tor exit node).
   - Maintains **long-term log retention** (≥ 365 days as per NIST/PCI-DSS) for forensic and compliance needs.

3. **Analytical Layer — ICS-Aware NDR (Network Detection and Response):**
   - Performs **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** of industrial protocols to detect protocol-level attacks (e.g., unauthorized write commands, malformed function codes).
   - Builds a **baseline of normal ICS communication** and flags anomalies such as new devices, new register accesses, or unusual command frequencies.
   - Examples: Claroty, Nozomi, Dragos, Microsoft Defender for IoT.

4. **Decision Layer — SOC (Security Operations Center):**
   - **24×7 human analysts** triage alerts, perform root-cause analysis, and escalate to incident response.
   - Maintains **runbooks** and **escalation procedures** specific to OT incidents (e.g., safe state shutdown vs. full isolation).

5. **Response Layer — SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response):**
   - Executes **automated playbooks** (e.g., "Isolate engineering workstation and notify plant manager").

6. **Intelligence Layer — Threat Intel Platform (TIP):**
   - Ingests **ICS-CERT advisories, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** mappings, and vendor-specific threat intel via **STIX/TAXII** feeds.

The **integration** of these components provides a **single pane of glass** for the security team, enabling detection of cross-domain attacks (e.g., a phishing email in IT leading to lateral movement into OT).

---

#### (b) Compute the MTTD for a manufacturing plant over a 30-day period given the incident table below. Also calculate the risk score for a critical PLC with P = 0.7, I = 0.95, and C = 0.2. Comment on the results. (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

| Incident # | $T_{\text{incident}}$ | $T_{\text{detect}}$ | Delta (hours) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Day 1, 00:00 | Day 1, 02:30 | 2.5 |
| 2 | Day 5, 10:00 | Day 5, 14:00 | 4.0 |
| 3 | Day 12, 06:00 | Day 13, 18:00 | 36.0 |
| 4 | Day 20, 22:00 | Day 21, 00:30 | 2.5 |
| 5 | Day 28, 15:00 | Day 28, 16:00 | 1.0 |

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply MTTD Formula:**

$$\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{\sum \Delta_i}{n} = \dfrac{2.5 + 4.0 + 36.0 + 2.5 + 1.0}{5}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the numerator:**

$$2.5 + 4.0 + 36.0 + 2.5 + 1.0 = 46.0\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 3 — Divide by number of incidents:**

$$\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{46.0}{5} = 9.2\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 4 — Risk Score Calculation:**

Given $P = 0.7$, $I = 0.95$, $C = 0.2$:

$$R = P \times I \times (1 - C) = 0.7 \times 0.95 \times (1 - 0.2)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the bracket:**

$$(1 - 0.2) = 0.8$$

**Step 6 — Multiply $P \times I$:**

$$0.7 \times 0.95 = 0.665$$

**Step 7 — Final multiplication:**

$$R = 0.665 \times 0.8 = 0.532$$

**Step 8 — Comment on Results:**

- **MTTD = 9.2 hours** is **unacceptable** for a manufacturing plant (target ≤ 1 hour). The outlier incident (36 hours) suggests a major detection gap that requires investigation.
- **Risk Score $R = 0.532$** falls in the **"Critical"** band, demanding immediate deployment of an ICS-aware NDR, micro-segmentation, and 24×7 SOC monitoring.

---

### Question B: Threat Detection Methodologies and Metrics (14 Marks)

#### (a) Compare signature-based, anomaly-based, and hybrid threat detection approaches for OT environments. Discuss how MITRE ATT&CK for ICS supports continuous monitoring. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Basis | Known IOC/pattern database | Statistical/ML baseline | Both combined |
| Zero-Day Detection | No | Yes | Yes |
| False Positives | Low | High | Medium-Low |
| Computational Cost | Low | High | Medium-High |
| OT Suitability | Good for known malware (e.g., Stuxnet) | Good for process anomalies | Best for modern SOCs |

**MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** provides a **curated knowledge base** of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) specifically mapped to industrial control systems. It supports continuous monitoring by:

- Mapping detected events to **specific techniques** (e.g., T0836 — Modify Parameter).
- Enabling **threat-informed defense** — SOC analysts prioritize monitoring of high-probability TTPs.
- Providing a **common language** between IT SOC and OT engineers.

---

#### (b) An OT IDS reports the following over a week: TP = 30, FP = 6, FN = 4, TN = 960. Compute Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Comment on whether this IDS is suitable for deployment in a critical OT zone. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Precision:**

$$\text{Precision} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} = \dfrac{30}{30 + 6} = \dfrac{30}{36} = 0.8333$$

**Step 2 — Compute Recall (TPR):**

$$\text{Recall} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \dfrac{30}{30 + 4} = \dfrac{30}{34} = 0.8824$$

**Step 3 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply Precision and Recall:**

$$0.8333 \times 0.8824 = 0.7353$$

**Step 5 — Sum Precision and Recall:**

$$0.8333 + 0.8824 = 1.7157$$

**Step 6 — Compute the ratio and scale:**

$$F_1 = 2 \times \dfrac{0.7353}{1.7157} = 2 \times 0.4286 = 0.8572$$

**Step 7 — Round and Comment:**

$$\boxed{F_1 \approx 0.86}$$

**Comment:** An F1-score of 0.86 is **good but not excellent**. For a critical OT zone (e.g., nuclear, pharma, or power), the recommended F1 threshold is **≥ 0.90**. The IDS should undergo **further tuning of anomaly thresholds** and **enrichment with OT-specific signatures** (e.g., Modbus, DNP3 attack patterns) before production deployment in safety-critical assets.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Continuous" aspect:** Students often describe monitoring as periodic scanning. KTU board explicitly expects *real-time, persistent* observation — emphasize **24×7 SOC + SIEM correlation**.
> 2. **Ignoring OT-specific protocols:** Mentioning only "firewalls and antivirus" loses 2–3 marks. Always cite **Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870, Profinet** and **Purdue Model levels**.
> 3. **Conflating MTTD with MTTR:** These are distinct metrics. MTTD = incident to detection; MTTR = detection to resolution.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps in numericals:** Always show every algebraic step — partial marks are awarded per step.
> 5. **Not citing frameworks:** Reference **NIST SP 800-137, NIST CSF, IEC 62443, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** wherever relevant to earn framework-recognition marks.
> 6. **Ignoring human/physical safety context:** OT is not just cybersecurity — it is **safety + security + operations**. Always frame answers in this triple context.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU Exam):**

- **Definition:** Continuous monitoring = persistent, real-time, automated observation of IT + OT assets for threat detection.
- **Governing Standard:** NIST SP 800-137.
- **OT Priority Triad:** **AIC** (Availability, Integrity, Confidentiality) — not CIA.
- **Key Metrics:** MTTD, MTTR, FPR, TPR, F1-Score, Asset Coverage, Risk Score $R = P \times I \times (1 - C)$.
- **Detection Paradigms:** Signature-based (known bad) + Anomaly-based (behavioral) + Hybrid.
- **Core Components:** SIEM + ICS-aware NDR + EDR + SOAR + 24×7 SOC + Threat Intel (STIX/TAXII).
- **Purdue Model Awareness:** Monitoring must cover **Levels 0–5** with strict demilitarized zones (DMZ) between IT and OT.
- **Protocol Awareness:** Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, BACnet — ICS-aware tools must understand these.
- **Key Frameworks:** NIST CSF, NIST SP 800-137, IEC 62443, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS, ISA/IEC 62443-3-3.
- **Real-World Anchors:** Stuxnet (2010), TRITON (2017), Oldsmar Water Plant (2021), Colonial Pipeline (2021).
- **Best Practice Formula:** Continuous Monitoring = **(Visibility × Correlation × Response Speed × Coverage)** — weakness in any factor degrades the entire security posture.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Monitoring and Threat Detection in OT-IT Networks**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools and techniques for monitoring OT and IT systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tools and techniques for monitoring OT and IT systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monitoring of OT and IT Systems** is the continuous, systematic observation, collection, correlation, and analysis of network traffic, system logs, device telemetry, and operational parameters across Information Technology (IT) and Operational Technology (OT) environments, with the objective of detecting anomalies, identifying cyber threats, ensuring process integrity, and maintaining situational awareness over the cyber-physical infrastructure.

In the context of **PECCT634 – OT Threat Prevention**, monitoring bridges two traditionally siloed domains:

- **IT Monitoring** – Focuses on confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data and business systems (servers, endpoints, applications, cloud workloads).
- **OT Monitoring** – Focuses on safety, reliability, continuity, and physical process integrity of industrial control systems (PLCs, RTUs, SCADA, DCS, IIoT sensors).

The integration of both under a unified monitoring framework is referred to as **IT/OT Converged Monitoring**, a core pillar of **ISA/IEC 62443** and the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference:** The **Purdue Model (ISA-99)** defines a **6-level architecture (Levels 0–5)** that maps physical processes (Level 0) up to enterprise networks (Level 5). Monitoring strategies differ fundamentally at each Purdue Level.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Body-Scan

Imagine a large modern hospital:

- The **IT systems** are the *administrative wing* — patient records, billing, email, scheduling. If breached, there is data loss and reputational harm.
- The **OT systems** are the *intensive care unit (ICU)* — ventilators, infusion pumps, MRI machines, power generators. If breached, *people can die*.

**Monitoring tools act like the hospital's central telemetry system:**

1. **Vital sign monitors (heart rate, BP, SpO₂)** → Real-time **anomaly detection sensors** in OT (vibration, temperature, flow rate).
2. **CCTV cameras & access logs** → **Network taps, SPAN ports, SIEM logs** in IT.
3. **The central nursing station dashboard** → A **Security Operations Center (SOC)** dashboard aggregating SIEM, SOAR, and OT-specific feeds.
4. **Periodic blood tests & scans** → **Vulnerability scans and active assessments**.
5. **Quarantine wards & isolation protocols** → **Firewalls, IPS, network segmentation**.

Just as doctors do not rely on a single instrument, OT/IT monitoring uses a **layered, defense-in-depth telemetry architecture**.

---

## 1.3 Why Monitoring Is Non-Negotiable in OT Threat Prevention

> [!NOTE]
> **Three driving imperatives** make monitoring a mandatory syllabus topic in PECCT634:
> 1. **Detection latency reduction** – Stuxnet dwell time was ~**17 months** before discovery.
> 2. **Compliance mandates** – NERC-CIP, IEC 62443, NIS2 Directive.
> 3. **Safety-of-life** – Unlike IT, OT failures cascade into physical destruction (e.g., TRITON/TRISIS attack on Schneider Triconex SIS, 2017).

---

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Monitoring Domains (At a Glance)

| Domain | Primary Target | Typical Data Source |
|---|---|---|
| Network Monitoring | Packets, Flows | TAPs, SPAN, NetFlow/sFlow |
| Endpoint Monitoring | Host logs, processes | EDR, Sysmon, syslog |
| Log & Event Monitoring | Logs from devices/apps | SIEM aggregation |
| Protocol Monitoring (OT) | ICS protocols | DPI on Modbus/DNP3/Profinet |
| Asset & Configuration | Inventory, baselines | CMDB, passive discovery |
| Behavioral / Anomaly | Deviations from baseline | ML/AI engines |
| Physical Process Monitoring | Sensor values, actuator states | Historian, SCADA tags |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of OT/IT Monitoring

### Pillar 1 — Passive Network Monitoring (The "Silent Listener")
- Operates by **duplicating traffic** via a **Test Access Point (TAP)** or **Switched Port Analyzer (SPAN port)**.
- Does **not inject packets**, hence **zero operational risk** on live OT networks.
- Used to discover assets, map topology, and baseline normal protocol behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In OT environments, *passive* monitoring is **strongly preferred** over active scanning because protocols like Modbus TCP and DNP3 have no authentication — any active probe can disrupt controllers.

### Pillar 2 — Active Monitoring & Scanning
- Sends probe packets (ICMP, TCP SYN, UDP).
- Tools: **Nmap, Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS**.
- Risk: Can crash legacy PLCs; restricted in production OT zones.

### Pillar 3 — Log Aggregation & SIEM
- **SIEM = Security Information and Event Management**.
- Functions: **C**ollect → **N**ormalize → **C**orrelate → **A**lert → **R**etain.
- Examples: **Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic SIEM, AlienVault OSSIM**.

### Pillar 4 — Intrusion Detection / Prevention (IDS/IPS)
- **IDS** = detection only (passive).
- **IPS** = detection + inline blocking (active).
- Signature-based vs Anomaly-based vs Stateful Protocol Analysis.
- **OT-specific IDS:** Nozomi Networks, Claroty, Dragos, Tripwire, Microsoft Defender for IoT (formerly CyberX).

### Pillar 5 — Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) / Extended Detection & Response (XDR)
- Monitors host-level: process trees, file integrity, registry, kernel calls.
- **XDR** extends EDR by correlating endpoint, network, identity, and cloud telemetry.

### Pillar 6 — ICS Protocol Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)
- Decodes industrial protocols byte-by-byte: **Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, IEC 61850 (GOOSE/MMS), Profinet, EtherNet/IP, BACnet, OPC UA**.
- Detects malformed packets, unauthorised function codes, replay attempts.

---

## 2.2 Core Analytical Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **mathematically verifiable metrics** examiners may expect in numerical or descriptive Part B questions.

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Formula / Expression | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D$ | **Mean Time to Detect** a threat | $D = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{detect,i} - t_{incident,i})$ | hours / days |
| $R$ | **Mean Time to Respond** | $R = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{resolve,i} - t_{detect,i})$ | hours / days |
| $MTTD + MTTR$ | **Mean Time to Recover** (MTTR) | $MTTR_{total} = D + R$ | hours / days |
| $C_{cov}$ | **Monitoring Coverage Ratio** | $C_{cov} = \dfrac{A_{monitored}}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$ | percent (%) |
| $FPR$ | **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | ratio (0–1) |
| $FNR$ | **False Negative Rate** | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | ratio (0–1) |
| $T$ | **Throughput of Sensor** | $T = \dfrac{P_{bytes}}{t_{interval}}$ | bytes/sec |
| $\sigma^{2}$ | **Anomaly Score Variance** | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_{i} - \mu)^{2}$ | (unit)$^{2}$ |
| $z$ | **Z-score for anomaly** | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | dimensionless |
| $H$ | **Shannon Entropy of traffic** | $H = -\sum_{i} p_{i} \log_{2} p_{i}$ | bits |
| $P_{loss}$ | **Packet Loss Ratio** | $P_{loss} = \dfrac{P_{dropped}}{P_{sent}}$ | ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not use the vertical pipe `|` inside markdown tables.** In prose, the absolute value of a metric $x$ is written $\vert x \vert$, e.g. $\vert z \vert > 3$ indicates a 3-sigma anomaly.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in OT Engineering

1. **Power Grid Operator** — Uses DPI on IEC 60870-5-104 to detect unauthorised GOOSE messages and prevent blackouts.
2. **Oil & Gas Refinery** — Uses passive asset discovery to maintain an **OT asset inventory** mandated by **IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1** (Identification).
3. **Smart Manufacturing** — Uses **OPC UA monitoring** to ensure deterministic, secure M2M communication on the shop floor.
4. **Water Treatment Utility** — Uses historian data correlation with network logs to detect process anomalies (e.g., pH sensor drift vs. SCADA command injection).

---

## 2.4 Comparative Analysis: IT vs OT Monitoring

| Parameter | IT Monitoring | OT Monitoring |
|---|---|---|
| Priority | **CIA** – Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability | **AIC** – Availability, Integrity, Confidentiality (note the inversion) |
| Latency Tolerance | Seconds to minutes | **Milliseconds to seconds** (real-time control) |
| Patch Cadence | Weekly / monthly | **Rarely** (often never during asset lifetime) |
| Protocol Visibility | HTTP, DNS, SMB, TLS | Modbus, DNP3, Profinet, IEC 61850 |
| Active Scanning | Routine | **Forbidden in Level 0–2** |
| Asset Lifespan | 3–5 years | **15–30+ years** |
| Failure Consequence | Data breach | Physical destruction, loss of life |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Workflows & Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Workflow — How a SIEM Processes an OT Alert

This section walks through the **complete analytical pipeline** that a SIEM follows when an OT anomaly is detected, in the **exhaustive step-by-step** style required for a 14-mark KTU answer.

### Step 1 — Data Ingestion (Log Collection)
- Sources: firewalls, switches (syslog), Windows event logs, Linux auditd, ICS protocol probes, EDR agents, historians.
- Collection mechanisms:
  - **Agent-based** (Splunk Forwarder, Wazuh Agent, Sysmon).
  - **Agentless** (syslog, SNMP traps, NetFlow, SPAN/TAP).
- The raw data is buffered in a **collector queue** to prevent loss during burst events.

### Step 2 — Parsing & Normalization
Each log event is parsed into a common schema, typically **Key-Value Pairs (KVP)** or **JSON**.

$$
E_{norm} = \{ ts, src, dst, proto, action, user, asset\_id, severity \}
$$

For an OT-specific event, additional fields appear:

$$
E_{ot} = \{ ts, plc\_id, function\_code, register\_addr, value, coil\_state \}
$$

### Step 3 — Correlation Engine
The correlation engine applies **rules, statistical models, and ML** to group related events. A common correlation form is:

$$
C = \bigcap_{i=1}^{k} E_{i} \;\text{where}\; \Delta t(E_{i}, E_{j}) \le \tau
$$

In words: *an alert is generated if $k$ related events occur within a time window $\tau$.*

**Example rule (Pseudo-correlation):**
IF (modbus_write_to_register AND src_ip NOT IN whitelist) AND (new_asset_discovery) AND ($\Delta t < 60s$) THEN escalate_critical.

### Step 4 — Enrichment
The event is enriched with:
- **Threat intelligence feeds** (STIX/TAXII, MISP).
- **Asset criticality** from CMDB.
- **Geolocation** of source IP.
- **Vulnerability context** (CVE matching via CPE).

### Step 5 — Alert Scoring & Prioritisation
Using a risk-prioritised model:

$$
Risk = Likelihood \times Impact
$$

For OT:

$$
Risk_{ot} = P_{exploit} \times (C_{safety} + C_{financial} + C_{env})
$$

where $C_{safety}$ weighs heavily (often the dominant term).

### Step 6 — Notification & Response Orchestration
- Pushed to **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation & Response)** for playbooks.
- For critical OT alerts: page on-call engineer, isolate PLC VLAN via SDN controller, snapshot historian data for forensics.

### Step 7 — Retention & Forensics
- Indexed and stored for **at least 1 year** (NERC-CIP requirement: 3 years for some assets).
- Used for **timeline reconstruction** in post-incident analysis.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Anomaly Detection via Z-Score

This derivation is the **most-likely KTU numerical sub-question** for 7 marks.

**Given:** A Modbus register reports values $x_{1}, x_{2}, \dots, x_{N}$ over $N$ time samples.
**Assumption:** Values under normal operation follow a Gaussian distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$.

**Step 1 — Compute the arithmetic mean:**

$$
\mu = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_{i}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the population variance:**

$$
\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_{i} - \mu)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the standard deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — For a new sample $x_{new}$, compute the Z-score:**

$$
z = \dfrac{x_{new} - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the decision rule:**

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Anomaly}, & \text{if } \vert z \vert > 3 \\ \text{Normal}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Interpretation:** A threshold of 3 corresponds to a **99.7% confidence interval** under the Gaussian assumption — i.e., values outside $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ occur by chance only 0.3% of the time.

**Engineering note:** For OT, $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are recomputed using an **exponential moving average (EMA)** to handle process drift:

$$
\mu_{t} = \alpha x_{t} + (1 - \alpha)\mu_{t-1}, \quad 0 < \alpha \le 1
$$

where $\alpha$ is the smoothing factor (typical $\alpha = 0.1$ to $0.3$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection by Use Case

| Use Case | Recommended IT Tool | Recommended OT Tool | KTU Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Asset Discovery | Nmap, Advanced IP Scanner | Nozomi Guardian, Claroty CTD, Dragos | Passive safe discovery in OT |
| Log Aggregation | Splunk, QRadar, Elastic | OT-native SIEM, Sentinel, Splunk + ICS Add-on | Unified IT/OT view |
| IDS (Signature) | Snort, Suricata | Dragos, Nozomi, Claroty | ICS-aware signatures |
| Vulnerability Mgmt | Nessus, Qualys | Tenable.ot, Claroty, SilentDefense | Protocol-aware CVE matching |
| EDR/XDR | CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender | Defender for Endpoint + Defender for IoT | Cross-domain correlation |
| Protocol Analysis | Wireshark, Zeek | Wireshark + ICS dissectors, Nozomi DPI | Decode Modbus/DNP3 |
| Threat Intel | MISP, Anomali | MISP + ICS-CERT advisories, Dragos WorldView | OT-specific IOCs |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Workflow — Network TAP vs SPAN Port

| Step | Network TAP | SPAN Port |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Placement | In-line, between switch and device | Configured on the switch |
| 2. Traffic capture | **Full-duplex, all frames, no drops** | Best-effort, may drop under load |
| 3. Failure behaviour | **Fail-open** (traffic continues) | Switch-dependent |
| 4. OT suitability | **High** | Medium (use only if TAP infeasible) |
| 5. Cost | Higher | Zero (built-in) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Converged IT/OT Monitoring Architecture (Purdue-Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Level 0 - Physical Process Sensors and Actuators] --> L1[Level 1 - PLC and RTU Controllers]
    L1 --> L2[Level 2 - SCADA HMI and Engineering Workstations]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 - OT DMZ Historian and Jump Server]
    L3 --> FW1[Industrial Firewall]
    FW1 --> L4[Level 4 - Enterprise Network]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 - Cloud and Internet]

    TAP1[Passive TAP L1-L2] --> DPI1[ICS DPI Engine]
    TAP2[Passive TAP L2-L3] --> DPI2[ICS DPI Engine]
    FW1 --> LOG1[Syslog Forwarder]
    L4 --> LOG2[EDR and EPP Telemetry]
    L5 --> LOG3[Cloud Audit Logs]

    DPI1 --> SIEM[Central SIEM Platform]
    DPI2 --> SIEM
    LOG1 --> SIEM
    LOG2 --> SIEM
    LOG3 --> SIEM

    SIEM --> SOAR[SOAR Orchestrator]
    SIEM --> SOC[SOC Analyst Dashboard]
    SIEM --> ML[Anomaly Detection ML Engine]
    SOAR --> ACT1[Auto-Isolate VLAN]
    SOAR --> ACT2[Page On-Call Engineer]
    SOAR --> ACT3[Snapshot Historian Data]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The diagram shows a **defence-in-depth telemetry architecture**. Passive TAPs feed the DPI engine at the OT-lower zones (L1–L2) where active scanning is forbidden. Higher-level telemetry joins at the SIEM, ensuring **single-pane-of-glass** visibility.

---

## 4.2 SIEM Processing Pipeline (Logical Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ingest - Logs and Packets] --> B[Parse and Normalize]
    B --> C[Enrich with Threat Intel and CMDB]
    C --> D[Correlate Rules and ML Models]
    D --> E{Alert Score Above Threshold}
    E -- Yes --> F[Generate Incident in SOAR]
    E -- No --> G[Archive to Cold Storage]
    F --> H[Notify SOC Analyst]
    F --> I[Execute Playbook - Block IP or Isolate VLAN]
    F --> J[Retain for 1 Year plus Forensics]
```

---

## 4.3 Anomaly Detection Decision Flow (Z-Score Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start - Receive New Sample x_new] --> M1[Retrieve Baseline Mean mu]
    M1 --> M2[Retrieve Baseline Std Dev sigma]
    M2 --> C[Compute z = x_new - mu over sigma]
    C --> Q{Is abs z greater than 3}
    Q -- Yes --> A1[Raise Anomaly Alert]
    Q -- No --> A2[Mark as Normal]
    A1 --> E1[Update SOC Dashboard]
    A1 --> E2[Trigger SOAR Playbook]
    A2 --> E3[Continue Monitoring]
```

---

## 4.4 Tool Layering Schematic — The Monitoring Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1[Governance Layer]
        A1A[NIST CSF and IEC 62443 Policy]
        A1B[Asset Inventory and CMDB]
    end
    subgraph A2[Collection Layer]
        A2A[Network TAPs and SPAN]
        A2B[Host Agents EDR and Sysmon]
        A2C[ICS Protocol Probes]
    end
    subgraph A3[Analysis Layer]
        A3A[SIEM Correlation Engine]
        A3B[Anomaly Detection ML]
        A3C[Threat Intelligence Feeds]
    end
    subgraph A4[Response Layer]
        A4A[SOAR Playbooks]
        A4B[Manual SOC Analyst]
        A4C[Automatic Network Isolation]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term SIEM. List any four of its core functional capabilities.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)** is a security solution that aggregates and analyses activity from multiple sources across an organisation's IT and OT infrastructure to provide real-time alerting, log management, and incident response support.

**Four core capabilities (½ mark each):**
1. **Log collection & aggregation** from heterogeneous sources (syslog, agents, API).
2. **Normalization** of events into a unified schema.
3. **Correlation** of events using rules and behavioural analytics.
4. **Alerting & dashboarding** for SOC analysts.
5. **Long-term retention & forensic search** of historical data.
6. **Compliance reporting** (PCI-DSS, NERC-CIP, IEC 62443).

*(Any four carry full 2 marks; definition carries 1 mark.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between passive and active monitoring. Why is passive monitoring preferred in OT environments?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Passive Monitoring | Active Monitoring |
|---|---|---|
| Method | Copies traffic via TAP/SPAN | Sends probe packets |
| Risk to OT | **Zero** (no injection) | High (can crash PLCs) |
| Coverage | Live traffic only | Endpoint visibility via probes |
| Discovery | Behavioural, not enumerative | Port/version enumeration |

**Why preferred in OT (1 mark):**
OT protocols (Modbus, DNP3, Profinet) were designed without authentication. Any active probe can be interpreted as a valid command, potentially causing **physical process disruption, equipment damage, or safety incidents**. Passive monitoring provides visibility without perturbing the deterministic control loop.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has an internal choice — i.e. students answer **either** Q(A) **or** Q(B). Both choices are designed to test equal cognitive depth (Apply / Analyse / Evaluate levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Architecture & Tool Selection**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **converged IT/OT Security Operations Center (SOC)** that monitors both IT and OT systems. Identify the key components at each Purdue Level and justify why a **layered, passive-first** approach is mandated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the following monitoring tool categories, giving **two specific commercial/open-source tools** for each and **one OT-specific deployment limitation**: **(7 marks)**
   1. Network TAP vs SPAN
   2. SIEM vs SOAR
   3. IT-IDS vs OT-IDS

**Model Solution A(a):**

1. **Converged SOC architecture** – introduce with a brief on **unified visibility** (1 mark).
2. **Purdue Level mapping** – present the diagram (Section 4.1) with components: TAP, DPI, SIEM, SOAR, SOC (3 marks).
3. **Justification of passive-first** – because of protocol fragility, asset longevity, and safety-of-life (2 marks).
4. **Conclusion** – mention compliance (IEC 62443-3-3 SR 6.2) (1 mark).

**Model Solution A(b):**
- For each sub-part, present a **2-row comparison table** with **two named tools** and **one OT limitation** (≈ 2.3 marks each × 3 = 7 marks).

| Sub-part | Tools | OT Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| TAP vs SPAN | **Network TAP** (e.g., Garland Technology, ProfiShark); **SPAN** (Cisco, HPE) | Active SPAN reconfiguration can cause micro-bursts missed by sensors in deterministic networks. |
| SIEM vs SOAR | **SIEM** (Splunk, QRadar); **SOAR** (Cortex XSOAR, Splunk SOAR) | SOAR playbooks must be **manually validated** before OT auto-execution, as mis-isolation can shut down a plant. |
| IT-IDS vs OT-IDS | **IT-IDS** (Snort, Suricata); **OT-IDS** (Nozomi, Dragos, Claroty) | IT-IDS has no awareness of Modbus/DNP3 function codes; produces noise on OT traffic. |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Techniques, Metrics & Anomaly Detection**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 3, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Describe the **SIEM processing pipeline** in detail, with reference to log ingestion, parsing/normalization, correlation, enrichment, and alert scoring. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Modbus temperature sensor in a refinery reports the following 10 readings (in °C):
   $65, 67, 66, 64, 66, 67, 65, 66, 67, 66$

   A new reading of **85 °C** arrives. Using the **Z-score anomaly detection** method, determine whether the new reading should be flagged as an anomaly at a **3-sigma** threshold. Show all derivations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution B(a):**

1. **Ingestion** – describe agents, syslog, NetFlow, TAP feeds (1.5 marks).
2. **Parse / Normalize** – common schema, JSON/KVP (1 mark).
3. **Correlation** – rule-based, statistical, ML (1.5 marks).
4. **Enrichment** – threat intel, CMDB, geo-IP (1 mark).
5. **Alert scoring** – risk = likelihood × impact; OT risk weighs safety heavily (1.5 marks).
6. **Retention / SOAR handoff** (0.5 marks).

**Model Solution B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the mean $\mu$:**

$$
\mu = \dfrac{65+67+66+64+66+67+65+66+67+66}{10} = \dfrac{659}{10} = 65.9
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the variance $\sigma^{2}$:**

$$
\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10}(x_{i} - 65.9)^{2}
$$

Calculating each squared deviation:
- $(65 - 65.9)^{2} = 0.81$
- $(67 - 65.9)^{2} = 1.21$
- $(66 - 65.9)^{2} = 0.01$
- $(64 - 65.9)^{2} = 3.61$
- $(66 - 65.9)^{2} = 0.01$
- $(67 - 65.9)^{2} = 1.21$
- $(65 - 65.9)^{2} = 0.81$
- $(66 - 65.9)^{2} = 0.01$
- $(67 - 65.9)^{2} = 1.21$
- $(66 - 65.9)^{2} = 0.01$

Sum of squared deviations = $0.81 + 1.21 + 0.01 + 3.61 + 0.01 + 1.21 + 0.81 + 0.01 + 1.21 + 0.01 = 8.90$

$$
\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{8.90}{10} = 0.89
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the standard deviation $\sigma$:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{0.89} \approx 0.943
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Z-score for $x_{new} = 85$:**

$$
z = \dfrac{85 - 65.9}{0.943} = \dfrac{19.1}{0.943} \approx 20.25
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the 3-sigma decision rule:**

$$
\vert z \vert = 20.25 \gg 3
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The new reading lies **20.25 standard deviations** above the mean — far outside the 3-sigma threshold. The reading **must be flagged as a critical anomaly**, triggering immediate SOC investigation. In a real refinery, this could indicate a **sensor fault, process upset, or malicious command injection** (e.g., someone writing an illegal value into a Modbus register).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often **skip Step 2's per-element expansion**. For full 7 marks, you must show **all 10 squared deviations explicitly**, sum them, and then divide by $N$. Quick calculator-only answers lose at least 1 mark for "missing working."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Must Memorise Before Exam**

- ✅ **IT vs OT priority order** is **AIC** in OT (Availability → Integrity → Confidentiality), opposite of IT.
- ✅ **Passive monitoring** is the **gold standard** for OT Levels 0–2 of the Purdue Model.
- ✅ **SIEM = C-N-C-A-R**: **C**ollect, **N**ormalize, **C**orrelate, **A**lert, **R**etain.
- ✅ **SOAR ≠ SIEM**: SIEM detects; SOAR responds/orchestrates.
- ✅ **Z-score threshold of 3** corresponds to **99.7% confidence** in Gaussian models.
- ✅ **TAP > SPAN** for OT because of fail-open behaviour and full-duplex capture.
- ✅ **OT-specific tools** to name in answers: **Nozomi Networks, Claroty, Dragos, Microsoft Defender for IoT, Tenable.ot**.
- ✅ **ICS protocols** to mention: **Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, IEC 61850, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA, BACnet**.
- ✅ **MTTD + MTTR = MTTR_total** (Mean Time to Recover).
- ✅ **Compliance anchors**: **IEC 62443, NIST CSF, NERC-CIP, NIS2 Directive**.
- ✅ **Key formulae to revise**:
  - $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum x_{i}$
  - $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum (x_{i} - \mu)^{2}$
  - $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$
  - $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$
  - $H = -\sum p_{i} \log_{2} p_{i}$
- ✅ **Real-world attack references** to cite (examiners love context): **Stuxnet (2010), TRITON/TRISIS (2017), Industroyer/CrashOverride (2016), Havex (2014)**.
- ✅ Always state the **CIA-triad inversion** when discussing OT — easy 1-mark pickup.
- ✅ Always draw the **Purdue Model** in architecture questions — 2–3 marks for a labelled diagram.
- ✅ Avoid using **active scanning tools** (Nessus, Nmap) in OT without explicit authorisation and an isolated VLAN.

---

**End of Module 3 — Tools and Techniques for Monitoring OT and IT Systems (PECCT634)**

topics.title: Techniques for analyzing security data from integrated networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Techniques for analyzing security data from integrated networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **OT (Operational Technology) Threat Prevention**, the *techniques for analyzing security data from integrated networks* refer to the systematic, multi-disciplinary methodologies used to collect, normalize, correlate, and interpret heterogeneous telemetry generated across converged **IT (Information Technology)**, **OT (Operational Technology)**, and **IoT (Internet of Things)** domains. These techniques transform raw bytes, packets, logs, flow records, and process variable traces into actionable security intelligence capable of detecting, characterizing, and attributing cyber-physical threats against industrial control systems (ICS), SCADA architectures, DCS, and PLC networks.

The integrated network under analysis typically spans the **Purdue Model / ISA-95** hierarchy — from Level 0 (sensors, actuators, field devices) through Level 1 (PLCs, RTUs), Level 2 (HMI, SCADA), Level 3 (Manufacturing Operations / Historian), Level 3.5 (DMZ), and Levels 4–5 (Enterprise IT). Security data from this stack is fundamentally **heterogeneous**, **high-velocity**, **high-volume**, and **time-sensitive**, demanding specialized analytical pipelines distinct from classical enterprise SOC tooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECCT634 – Module 4):** *Techniques for analyzing security data* include SIEM, log and event correlation, deep packet inspection (DPI) tailored to OT protocols (Modbus/TCP, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, IEC 61850, OPC UA, EtherNet/IP, PROFINET, BACnet), anomaly detection, behavioral baselining, threat intelligence fusion, and machine-learning-based analytics operating on integrated IT-OT telemetry.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard)

| Term | Expansion | OT-Specific Meaning |
|------|-----------|---------------------|
| **SIEM** | Security Information & Event Management | Centralized log aggregation + correlation engine |
| **SOAR** | Security Orchestration, Automation & Response | Playbook-driven incident response automation |
| **NTA** | Network Traffic Analysis | North-South and East-West flow analysis |
| **DPI** | Deep Packet Inspection | Parsing of OT application-layer protocol payloads |
| **UEBA** | User & Entity Behavior Analytics | Behavioral fingerprinting of engineers, devices, processes |
| **TIP** | Threat Intelligence Platform | STIX/TAXII feed ingestion |
| **NDR** | Network Detection & Response | AI-driven anomaly detection on wire data |
| **ICS-IDS** | Industrial Control System IDS | Signature/anomaly detection for OT protocols |
| **EDR** | Endpoint Detection & Response | Asset-level telemetry (e.g., Engineering Workstation) |
| **DMZ** | Demilitarized Zone | IT-OT buffer zone (ISA-95 Level 3.5) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The ICU Monitor of the Plant

Imagine a hospital **Intensive Care Unit (ICU)**. A single patient is connected to ten devices — ECG, pulse oximeter, blood pressure, ventilator, infusion pump. Each device streams its own proprietary waveform. A senior doctor doesn't look at one device; she looks at the **integrated dashboard** where all waveforms are overlaid, time-synchronized, and cross-correlated.

A sudden drop in oxygen saturation **+** rising heart rate **+** elevated respiratory rate is *not* three independent alarms — it is one combined clinical picture pointing to respiratory failure.

OT security analytics work the **exact same way**:
- A spike in Modbus traffic on the engineering LAN **+** an anomalous write to a PLC register from an unauthorized IP **+** an IT-side alert about a phishing email targeting an HMI operator is **one combined cyber-physical incident** — a multi-stage ICS attack.
- The "doctor" is the **SIEM correlation engine**, the "waveforms" are the IT-OT telemetry streams, and the "diagnostic skill" is the **correlation, baselining, and enrichment logic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Just as a doctor would never treat a single ECG lead in isolation, an OT security analyst must fuse IT logs, OT process values, and network metadata to detect sophisticated attacks that hide between domains (e.g., the **TRITON/TRISIS** attack of 2017 that targeted Schneider Triconex SIS via IT-OT pivot).

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU Board Exams

- It directly maps to **Course Outcome CO4** of PECCT634 (analyze security data from integrated networks for threat prevention).
- It is the bridge between *detection theory* (Modules 1–3) and *incident response* (Module 5).
- It is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU ESE because it allows examiners to test both **architectural understanding** and **analytical methodology**.
- Real-world incidents (Stuxnet, Industroyer, TRITON, Colonial Pipeline, Oldsmar Water) all hinge on the techniques covered here.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics in Bold (Must Memorize)

- **MTTD — Mean Time To Detect:** $\text{MTTD} = t_{\text{detect}} - t_{\text{compromise}}$
- **MTTR — Mean Time To Respond:** $\text{MTTR} = t_{\text{resolve}} - t_{\text{detect}}$
- **MTTC — Mean Time To Contain**
- **FPR — False Positive Rate:** $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$
- **TPR — True Positive Rate (Recall/Sensitivity):** $\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **F1-Score:** $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$
- **Precision:** $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **Throughput:** measured in **EPS (Events Per Second)** for SIEMs
- **Log volume:** measured in **GB/day** or **TB/day**
- **OT-specific KPI:** **Process Deviation Index (PDI)** = deviation of measured process variable from expected baseline, expressed in $\sigma$ (standard deviations).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Security Data Analytics Pipeline

Modern OT security analytics operate on a **standardized five-stage pipeline**. KTU examiners expect students to be able to draw and label this pipeline.

### Stage 1 — Data Collection (Ingestion Layer)

Telemetry is gathered from every tier of the Purdue Model using purpose-built collectors:

- **Level 0–1 (Field & Control):** Passive taps, ICS-IDS sensors (e.g., Nozomi Guardian, Claroty, Dragos), SPAN ports on industrial switches, ICS protocol-aware DPI probes.
- **Level 2 (Supervisory):** HMI/SCADA event logs, historian exports (PI, Wonderware), engineering workstation EDR.
- **Level 3 (Operations):** MES logs, OPC UA broker logs, jump-server logs, RDP/SSH session recordings.
- **Level 3.5 (DMZ):** Firewall logs (Cisco ASA, Fortinet, Palo Alto), VPN concentrators, WAF, ICS firewall (e.g., Tofino/Schweitzer).
- **Level 4–5 (Enterprise IT):** AD/LDAP logs, EDR (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne), email security, DLP, cloud logs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collection must be passive, not inline, for Levels 0–1** to avoid introducing latency or single-points-of-failure into the process control loop. KTU examiners **deduct marks** if students recommend inline IDS on the process bus.

### Stage 2 — Normalization & Parsing (Data Lake Layer)

Raw events arrive in **dozens of incompatible formats**: Syslog (RFC 5424), CEF (Common Event Format), LEEF (Log Event Extended Format), PCAP, NetFlow v5/v9, IPFIX, OT-specific logs (Modbus register dumps, DNP3 event objects, IEC 61850 GOOSE/SMV, OPC UA AuditEvents).

Normalization converts them into a **canonical schema**, typically:
- Timestamp (UTC, RFC 3339)
- Source IP / MAC / Asset ID
- Destination IP / MAC / Asset ID
- Protocol (e.g., Modbus/TCP, DNP3)
- Function Code (e.g., FC 5 = Write Single Coil)
- Register/Object address
- User identity
- Action (READ, WRITE, EXECUTE)
- Outcome (SUCCESS, FAILURE)
- Severity (0–10)
- Raw payload (base64, optional)

The SIEM uses **parsers** (e.g., LogPoint, Splunk, IBM QRadar, Elastic, ArcSight) to transform vendor-specific formats into this schema. A **failure to correctly parse an OT protocol** is the single most common cause of missed detections in industrial environments.

### Stage 3 — Correlation (Analytical Engine)

This is the **brain** of the platform. Correlation rules combine multiple low-fidelity events into high-fidelity incidents. KTU expects three categories:

| Correlation Type | Mechanism | OT Example |
|------------------|-----------|------------|
| **Rule-Based (Signature)** | IF-THEN logic on parsed fields | "If DNP3 FC 6 (Write) to RTU in Substation 7 from non-engineering VLAN → ALERT" |
| **Statistical** | Threshold, standard deviation, z-score | "If pressure in Reactor R-101 deviates > 3σ from 7-day baseline → ALERT" |
| **Behavioral / ML** | Clustering, classification, time-series | One-Class SVM trained on normal Modbus traffic flags anomalies |
| **Stateful** | Tracks session/protocol state | Detects a Modbus transaction violating master-slave semantics |

A **correlation rule in pseudocode**:
```text
IF (count(failed_login, user=op_engineer, target=HMI_3, window=5min) ≥ 5)
  AND (geo_source ≠ "Kerala, IN")
  AND (asset_criticality(HMI_3) = "Tier-1")
THEN severity = CRITICAL
  AND trigger playbook("ICS-Account-Lockdown")
```

### Stage 4 — Enrichment & Contextualization

Events are enriched with **threat intelligence** (STIX/TAXII feeds, ICS-CERT advisories, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS mappings), **asset inventory context** (CMDB, Purdue level, safety instrumented system flag), and **vulnerability context** (CVE → affected firmware version). This converts a "what" into a "so what."

### Stage 5 — Presentation, Response & Forensics

Dashboards, alerts, ticketing, and SOAR playbooks. The **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** matrix is the standard visualization framework (11 tactic categories: Initial Access, Execution, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Evasion, Discovery, Lateral Movement, Collection, Command and Control, Inhibit Response Function, Impair Process Control, Damage to Property and Equipment).

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundations — The "Why" Behind Each Technique

### A. Statistical Process Control (SPC) for OT

Borrowed from quality engineering. Uses **Western Electric Rules** and **Shewhart control charts**. A process variable $X_t$ is normal if it stays within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$. An attacker manipulating a setpoint will cause a **sustained shift** detectable as a pattern of consecutive points on one side of the mean.

### B. Information-Theoretic Anomaly Detection (Shannon Entropy)

Entropy measures the **randomness** of protocol traffic. Normal OT traffic is highly regular (one master polling 20 slaves cyclically). An attack — port scan, C2 beacon, exfiltration — increases entropy. The formula:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i) \quad \text{(bits)}$$

A sudden entropy drop or rise on an OT segment is a strong anomaly signal.

### C. Bayesian Inference for OT Alert Correlation

When multiple weak alerts fire, the system computes the **posterior probability** that a real attack is in progress:

$$P(A \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E)}$$

This solves the "alert fatigue" problem in OT — operators cannot investigate 10,000 alerts/day, so Bayesian correlation elevates only high-posterior chains.

### D. Machine Learning Paradigms for OT

| Paradigm | Algorithm Examples | OT Use Case |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Supervised** | Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM, Neural Nets | Classify known attack families (DoS, MITM, replay) on labelled datasets (e.g., SWaT, WADI, BATADAL, HIL-based) |
| **Unsupervised** | k-Means, DBSCAN, Isolation Forest, Autoencoders | Detect zero-days; no labels needed |
| **Semi-supervised** | One-Class SVM, Deep Autoencoders | "Normal-only" training on historian data |
| **Reinforcement** | Deep Q-Networks | Adaptive honeypot orchestration |
| **Time-Series** | LSTM, GRU, Transformer | Forecast process variables; residual = anomaly |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT recommend Deep Learning alone for Level 0–1 OT. It violates IEC 62443 latency requirements and cannot be retrained continuously in a stable process. Use **physics-based** detection at Level 0–1 and ML at Level 2–3.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Precision | $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $0 \le P \le 1$ |
| 2 | Recall (TPR) | $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $0 \le R \le 1$ |
| 3 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$ | $0 \le F_1 \le 1$ |
| 4 | False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | $0 \le \text{FPR} \le 1$ |
| 5 | True Negative Rate (Specificity) | $\text{TNR} = \frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $0 \le \text{TNR} \le 1$ |
| 6 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+TN+FN}$ | $0 \le \text{Acc} \le 1$ |
| 7 | Matthews Correlation Coefficient | $\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | $-1 \le \text{MCC} \le 1$ |
| 8 | ROC-AUC | $\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR})\, d(\text{FPR})$ | $0.5 \le \text{AUC} \le 1$ |
| 9 | Z-Score (Process Deviation) | $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $\vert z \vert \ge 3$ ⇒ alarm |
| 10 | Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_i p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | bits |
| 11 | Conditional Entropy | $H(X \mid Y) = -\sum_{i,j} p(x_i,y_j) \log_2 p(x_i \mid y_j)$ | bits |
| 12 | Information Gain (ID3) | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | bits |
| 13 | Bayes Posterior | $P(A \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid A) P(A)}{P(E)}$ | $[0,1]$ |
| 14 | Log-Likelihood Ratio | $\text{LLR} = \log \frac{P(E \mid H_1)}{P(E \mid H_0)}$ | nats |
| 15 | KL Divergence (anomaly score) | $D_{KL}(P \parallel Q) = \sum_i P(i) \log \frac{P(i)}{Q(i)}$ | nats |
| 16 | Kalman Innovation (residual) | $\tilde{y}_t = y_t - H \hat{x}_{t \mid t-1}$ | engineering units |
| 17 | Sliding Window Rate | $r_t = \frac{1}{w} \sum_{i=t-w+1}^{t} \mathbb{1}\{x_i = \text{event}\}$ | events/sec |
| 18 | Kullback-Leibler Detector Threshold | $D_{KL} > \tau$ ⇒ anomaly | $\tau$ is calibrated from baseline |
| 19 | Isolation Forest Anomaly Score | $s(x,n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$ | $0 \le s \le 1$ |
| 20 | One-Class SVM decision | $f(x) = \text{sign}((w \cdot \Phi(x)) - \rho)$ | $+1$ normal, $-1$ anomaly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint Reminder:** Throughout the formulas above, vertical bars such as $\vert z \vert$ are rendered using the LaTeX `\vert` command, never the raw markdown pipe `|`, to preserve table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power utilities** use SIEM + DPI on IEC 60870-5-104 / DNP3 to detect grid-manipulation attempts (e.g., the 2015 Ukraine attack).
- **Oil & Gas** operators fuse historian (OSIsoft PI) trends with firewall logs to catch **Stuxnet-style** PLC reprogramming.
- **Smart manufacturing (Industry 4.0)** uses ML on PROFINET/EtherNet/IP traffic to detect machine tampering.
- **Water treatment (Oldsmar, 2021)** — proper anomaly detection on the SCADA HMI would have alerted when the NaOH setpoint jumped from 100 ppm to 11,100 ppm.
- **Nuclear & Safety Instrumented Systems (IEC 61508/61511)** — formal verification + invariant checking on SIS signals.

| **ISO 27019** | Information security for process control |
