> 6. **Omitting the Knowledge Persistence / Feedback Loop step.** Examiners explicitly check for the iterative nature of hunting. A 6-step list that does not loop back to Step 1 is incomplete — **lose 1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision summary — read this 30 minutes before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Threat Hunting** is **proactive**, **hypothesis-driven**, and **human-led**; it is *not* triggered by an alert.
- ✅ **Three Hunting Pillars:** (1) Hypothesis-driven, (2) IoC-based retrospective, (3) Anomaly/baseline-driven.
- ✅ **Six-Step Operational Loop:** Trigger → Recon → Hypothesis → Data → Query → Analysis → Conclusion → Knowledge Persistence → *(loop)*.
- ✅ **Hypothesis Structure:** $H = \{S, T, E, A, P\}$ — Subject, Target, Environment, Action, Preconditions.
- ✅ **Maturity Model:** HM0 (reactive) → HM1 (ad-hoc) → HM2 (procedural) → HM3 (innovative) → HM4 (leading).
- ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** has **12 tactic categories** and **~80 techniques** — must know the IDs (TA0108, TA0109, TA0110, TA0111, TA0112, TA0113, TA0114, TA0115, TA0116, TA0117, TA0102, TA0103).
- ✅ **Key Metrics:** MTTD, MTTR, MTTC, DTRR, HCR, FPR, DHR, CC, RRI. Formulas must be memorized.
- ✅ **Industry Benchmarks:** MTTD target **< 24 hours**, MTTC target **< 7 days**, dwell time reduction **> 90%** in mature programs.
- ✅ **Five-Layer Defense-in-Depth:** Predictive → Preventive → Detective → Response → Recovery.
- ✅ **Seven Program Components:** TIP, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS, NDR, EDR/XDR, UEBA, Deception, SOAR.
- ✅ **Purdue Level 0/1 — PASSIVE monitoring only.** Never active scan PLCs or RTUs.
- ✅ **FPR ≠ Precision.** Precision = $TP/(TP+FP)$; FPR = $FP/(FP+TN)$. Always state the threshold value.
- ✅ **Real-world OT incidents to cite:** Stuxnet (2010), BlackEnergy (Ukraine 2015), Industroyer (Ukraine 2016), TRITON/Trisis (2017), Oldsmar Water (2021), Colonial Pipeline (2021).
- ✅ **Decoy assets (honeypot PLCs, honeytokens)** are passive — they generate alerts when *touched*, and do not disrupt production.
- ✅ **Hypothesis-driven hunting** is the most KTU-favored methodology — every 14-mark answer should center on it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recovery strategies and business continuity planning for integrated networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT634 - OT THREAT PREVENTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recovery strategies and business continuity planning for integrated networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Business Continuity Planning (BCP)** in the context of Operational Technology (OT) and integrated Industrial Control Systems (ICS) networks is defined by **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3** and **ISA/IEC 62443-2-1** as a holistic, management-driven process that develops a framework for implementing **resilience, redundancy, and rapid recovery** mechanisms to ensure the continuity of critical industrial processes during and after a cybersecurity incident, natural disaster, or system failure.

A **Recovery Strategy** is the predefined, documented set of technical, operational, and managerial procedures that an organization activates to restore disrupted OT/IT-integrated systems to their nominal operational state, while meeting pre-established **Recovery Time Objectives (RTO)** and **Recovery Point Objectives (RPO)**.

### Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Syllabus

| Term | Definition (KTU-Aligned) |
|------|-------------------------|
| **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** | Maximum acceptable downtime before unacceptable impact on safety or production |
| **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** | Maximum tolerable period in which data may be lost from an IT/OT service |
| **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** | Average time required to repair a failed OT component and restore service |
| **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | Predicted elapsed time between inherent failures of a system during operation |
| **BIA (Business Impact Analysis)** | Process of analyzing the impact of disruptions on business processes |
| **ICS/OT Integrated Network** | Converged network of SCADA, DCS, PLC, HMI, MES, and corporate IT systems |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Hospital Emergency Department Analogy 🏥**
>
> Imagine a city hospital as an **integrated OT/IT network**:
> - **IT systems** = Patient registration, billing, electronic health records
> - **OT systems** = ICU ventilators, MRI machines, blood bank refrigeration, oxygen supply
> - **A cyberattack (e.g., Ransomware)** = A sudden power failure + contamination event
> - **Recovery Strategy** = The hospital's **Disaster Manual**: backup generators, manual patient triage procedures, redundant oxygen cylinders, off-site paper records
> - **BCP** = The **entire operational plan** that ensures the hospital keeps saving lives even during the disaster
>
> Just as a hospital cannot shut down during a crisis, an oil refinery, power grid, or water treatment plant **cannot simply "go offline"** — they need layered, tested recovery mechanisms.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, this topic directly maps to:
> - **CO4 (Module 4)**: *"Design and evaluate recovery strategies and business continuity plans for integrated OT networks."*
> - **Cognitive Level**: Analyze (L4) → Evaluate (L5) → Create (L6)
> - **Engineer's Responsibility**: Modern OT engineers must design systems that are **safe, available, and resilient** — not just functional.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The BCP/DR Lifecycle for Integrated Networks

The complete Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery (BC/DR) lifecycle for OT-integrated networks follows a **six-phase methodology** as standardized by **ISO 22301:2019** and adopted in KTU's PECCT634 syllabus:

### Phase 1: Business Impact Analysis (BIA)

The foundation phase. It identifies:
- **Critical OT processes** (e.g., turbine control, reactor cooling, conveyor sequencing)
- **Dependencies** between IT and OT layers
- **Financial and safety impact** of downtime per hour
- **Regulatory compliance** requirements (e.g., NERC-CIP for power, IEC 62443 for industrial)

**Key Question Answered**: *"What processes MUST be restored first, and how fast?"*

### Phase 2: Risk Assessment (RA)

- Identify **threats** (ransomware, insider attacks, physical sabotage, natural disasters)
- Assess **vulnerabilities** in the Purdue Model layers (Levels 0-5)
- Calculate **risk score** using:
$$R = P \times I \times V_c$$
where:
- $P$ = Probability of threat occurrence
- $I$ = Impact severity
- $V_c$ = Vulnerability criticality (asset exposure factor)

### Phase 3: Recovery Strategy Development

Selection of the appropriate **recovery architecture** based on RTO/RPO:

| Recovery Strategy | Typical RTO | Typical RPO | Best Use Case in OT |
|-------------------|-------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Cold Site** | Days–Weeks | Hours | Non-critical office IT only |
| **Warm Site** | Hours | Minutes | MES, Historian backup |
| **Hot Site** | Seconds–Minutes | Near-zero | SCADA master, safety systems |
| **Active-Active Redundancy** | < 1 second | Zero (synchronous) | Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS), reactor control |
| **Active-Passive (Warm Standby)** | Minutes | Seconds | DCS servers, HMI databases |

### Phase 4: Plan Documentation (BCP/DRP)

Development of formal, version-controlled documents including:
- **Executive Policy Statement**
- **Emergency Response Procedures**
- **Crisis Communication Plan**
- **Technical Recovery Runbooks**
- **Roles & Responsibilities Matrix (RACI)**

### Phase 5: Testing & Validation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Favorite Question**: *"Why is testing critical in OT recovery planning?"*
>
> **Answer**: Unlike IT systems, OT systems cannot be casually rebooted or "tested live" because doing so may cause physical damage, environmental release, or safety incidents. Hence **tabletop exercises, simulation, and parallel-environment testing** are mandatory.

### Phase 6: Maintenance & Continuous Improvement

- Annual plan review
- Post-incident lessons learned
- Updates after any change in OT infrastructure

## 2.2 The Purdue Model & Recovery Zone Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Purdue Reference Model (ISA-99/IEC 62443)** segments industrial networks into logical zones. Each zone requires a **distinct recovery strategy** because they have different safety, availability, and performance constraints.

| Purdue Level | Function | Recovery Strategy Emphasis |
|--------------|----------|---------------------------|
| **Level 0** | Physical Process (Sensors, Actuators) | Mechanical fail-safes, manual override |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control (PLC, DCS Controllers) | Hot-standby PLCs, redundant I/O |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory (HMI, SCADA) | RAID servers, image-based backups |
| **Level 3** | Operations Management (MES, Historian) | Warm-site failover, database replication |
| **Level 3.5** | DMZ (Industrial Firewall Zone) | Stateless redundancy, secure data diode |
| **Level 4** | Business Planning (ERP) | Standard IT DR (cloud replication) |
| **Level 5** | Enterprise Network | Standard corporate IT DR |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE**: All mathematical notation uses `\vert` for absolute value to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Formula / Metric | LaTeX Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **RTO Calculation** | $\text{RTO} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{decide}} + T_{\text{restore}}$ | Total allowable downtime budget |
| **RPO Calculation** | $\text{RPO} = T_{\text{last\_backup}} - T_{\text{disaster}}$ | Maximum acceptable data loss window |
| **Availability (Tier)** | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$ | System uptime percentage |
| **Annual Downtime** | $D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ | Minutes of downtime per year |
| **Risk Score** | $R = P \times I \times V_c$ | Quantitative risk priority |
| **SLE (Single Loss Expectancy)** | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{EF}$ | Expected loss from one incident |
| **EF (Exposure Factor)** | $\text{EF} \in [0, 1]$ | Fraction of asset value lost |
| **ARO (Annualized Rate of Occurrence)** | $\text{ARO} \in [0, \infty)$ | Expected incidents per year |
| **ALE (Annual Loss Expectancy)** | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Expected annual financial loss |
| **Safety Integrity Level** | $\text{SIL} \in \lbrace 1, 2, 3, 4 \rbrace$ | IEC 61508 risk reduction measure |
| **Redundancy Factor** | $k\text{-of-}n \text{ (e.g., 2oo3)}$ | Voting logic for fault tolerance |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Recovery Strategy Application |
|----------------|------------------------------|
| **Power Generation** | N+1 turbine control redundancy, black-start capability |
| **Oil & Gas (Pipeline)** | Hot-standby SCADA, satellite backup communication |
| **Water Treatment** | PLC shadow pairing, manual chemical dosing fallback |
| **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing** | Batch record backup (FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliance) |
| **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)** | Edge computing failover, digital twin for recovery validation |
| **Nuclear Power** | 2oo3 SIL-3 voting, physically separated diverse backup systems |

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Frameworks

## 3.1 Derivation: RTO/RPO Budgeting for an OT Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Exam Standard)**: An integrated OT process has the following parameters:
> - Time to detect anomaly: $T_{\text{detect}} = 4$ minutes
> - Time for incident classification and decision: $T_{\text{decide}} = 11$ minutes
> - Time to restore from hot standby: $T_{\text{restore}} = 5$ minutes
>
> The process is backed up every 15 minutes. A disaster occurs at $T_{\text{disaster}} = 12{:}45$ hours, and the last successful backup was at $T_{\text{last\_backup}} = 12{:}30$ hours.
>
> **Find**: RTO, RPO, and evaluate if the recovery strategy is acceptable for a **Safety Instrumented System (SIL-2)** which mandates $\text{RTO} \le 30$ minutes and $\text{RPO} \le 5$ minutes.

### Step 1: Define Variables and Units

We need consistent units. All times will be expressed in **minutes**.

| Variable | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Detection time | $T_{\text{detect}}$ | 4 | minutes |
| Decision time | $T_{\text{decide}}$ | 11 | minutes |
| Restoration time | $T_{\text{restore}}$ | 5 | minutes |
| Disaster timestamp | $T_{\text{disaster}}$ | 12:45 | hh:mm |
| Last backup timestamp | $T_{\text{last\_backup}}$ | 12:30 | hh:mm |

### Step 2: Calculate RTO Using the Additive Model

The **Recovery Time Objective** is the sum of all time intervals from the moment the disaster strikes to the moment the system is fully operational:

$$\text{RTO} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{decide}} + T_{\text{restore}}$$

Substitute the numerical values:

$$\text{RTO} = 4 \text{ min} + 11 \text{ min} + 5 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{RTO} = 20 \text{ minutes}}$$

### Step 3: Calculate RPO Using the Time-Delta Method

The **Recovery Point Objective** is the time gap between the most recent successful backup and the moment of disaster:

$$\text{RPO} = \vert T_{\text{disaster}} - T_{\text{last\_backup}} \vert$$

Compute the difference in minutes:

$$T_{\text{disaster}} - T_{\text{last\_backup}} = 12{:}45 - 12{:}30 = 15 \text{ minutes}$$

$$\boxed{\text{RPO} = 15 \text{ minutes}}$$

### Step 4: Compare Against SIL-2 Mandate

| Metric | Calculated Value | SIL-2 Mandate | Compliance Status |
|--------|------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| RTO | 20 min | $\le 30$ min | ✅ **COMPLIANT** |
| RPO | 15 min | $\le 5$ min | ❌ **NON-COMPLIANT** |

### Step 5: Engineering Recommendation

> [!WARNING]
> **RPO exceeds the SIL-2 threshold by 10 minutes.** This means up to 15 minutes of safety-critical data could be lost. Recommended corrective actions:
>
> 1. **Increase backup frequency** to every 5 minutes ($\text{New RPO} = 5$ min) — direct fix.
> 2. **Implement synchronous data replication** to a hot-standby historian (RPO → 0).
> 3. **Add continuous data streaming** to an immutable write-once log (WORM) for forensic recovery.

### Step 6: Final Validated State

$$\text{RTO}_{\text{final}} = 20 \text{ min}, \quad \text{RPO}_{\text{final}} = 5 \text{ min}, \quad \text{SIL-2 Status} = \text{COMPLIANT} \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: System Availability and Annual Downtime

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement**: A SCADA master server has the following reliability parameters:
> - $\text{MTBF} = 8000$ hours
> - $\text{MTTR} = 2$ hours
>
> **Find**: (a) Availability $A$, (b) Annual downtime in minutes, (c) Tier classification per **TIA-942**.

### Step 1: Apply the Availability Formula

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$$

Substitute values:

$$A = \frac{8000}{8000 + 2} \times 100\%$$

$$A = \frac{8000}{8002} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 99.975\%}$$

### Step 2: Compute Annual Downtime

$$D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600 \text{ min/year}$$

$$D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - 0.99975) \times 525{,}600$$

$$D_{\text{annual}} = 0.00025 \times 525{,}600$$

$$\boxed{D_{\text{annual}} \approx 131.4 \text{ minutes/year}}$$

### Step 3: TIA-942 Tier Classification

| Tier | Availability | Annual Downtime |
|------|-------------|-----------------|
| Tier I | 99.671% | $\le 28.8$ hours |
| Tier II | 99.741% | $\le 22$ hours |
| **Tier III** | **99.982%** | **$\le 1.6$ hours (96 min)** |
| Tier IV | 99.995% | $\le 26.3$ minutes |

Since $A = 99.975\%$ and $D_{\text{annual}} \approx 131.4$ min, the system is **just below Tier III**. To achieve Tier III, MTTR must be reduced to $\le 1.45$ hours or MTBF increased to $\ge 11{,}000$ hours.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Framework: Recovery Strategy Selection Matrix

The decision tree for selecting an appropriate OT recovery strategy follows this logic chain:

$$\text{Impact Severity} \rightarrow \text{Safety Risk?} \rightarrow \text{Financial Loss/Hr} \rightarrow \text{Selected Strategy}$$

| Severity | Safety Risk | Financial Loss/Hr | Recommended Strategy |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Catastrophic | Yes (life-threatening) | Any | **Active-Active + Manual Fail-safe** |
| Critical | Moderate | $>\$500K$ | **Hot Site (synchronous replication)** |
| High | Low | $\$50K$–$\$500K$ | **Warm Site (asynchronous replication)** |
| Medium | None | $\$5K$–$\$50K$ | **Cold Site + Tape Backup** |
| Low | None | $<\$5K$ | **Standard Backup, Manual Rebuild** |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Q: Define Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO). How do they differ in the context of OT-integrated networks?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** *(1 mark)*: The maximum acceptable time duration within which an OT system or process must be restored to its operational state after a disaster, to avoid unacceptable consequences (safety, production, financial).
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** *(1 mark)*: The maximum tolerable time period during which data may be lost from an IT/OT service due to a major incident, measured backward from the disaster event.
- **Difference in OT Context** *(1 mark)*: In OT, RTO is dominated by **physical safety constraints** (e.g., reactor cooling must resume within minutes), while RPO is dominated by **process data continuity** (e.g., batch records, historian data). RTO addresses *time-to-recover*, RPO addresses *data-loss tolerance*.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
**Q: List and briefly explain any THREE recovery strategies used in integrated OT/IT networks.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Cold Site**: A backup facility with power and cooling but no pre-loaded data. RTO is days. Used for non-critical office IT.
2. **Warm Site**: Partially configured with periodic data replication. RTO is hours. Suitable for MES and Historian systems.
3. **Hot Site**: Fully mirrored real-time replication. RTO is minutes. Used for SCADA masters, safety-critical systems.
   *(Alternatives valid: Active-Active, Pilot Light, Backup-as-a-Service.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Module 4 questions of 14 marks allow internal choice. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as independent alternatives.

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper based on 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **six phases of the Business Continuity Planning (BCP) lifecycle** as applied to an **integrated OT/IT network**. Clearly differentiate between the roles of the **BIA** and the **Risk Assessment** phases. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A SCADA-controlled **chemical reactor** has the following recovery parameters:
- Time to detect a fault: 6 minutes
- Time for incident decision: 14 minutes
- Time to restore from hot standby: 10 minutes
- Last successful backup: 14:20 hours
- Disaster occurrence time: 14:38 hours

The reactor is classified as **SIL-3** with mandated RTO $\le 45$ min and RPO $\le 3$ min.
**Compute the RTO and RPO, and determine whether the current recovery strategy meets the SIL-3 mandate. If not, recommend a revised strategy.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The BCP lifecycle for integrated OT/IT networks consists of **six sequential phases**, each contributing to overall resilience:

1. **Phase 1 — Business Impact Analysis (BIA)** *(2 marks)*:
   - Identifies critical OT processes (e.g., reactor control, conveyor logic).
   - Maps dependencies between IT and OT layers (Purdue Levels 0–5).
   - Quantifies financial, safety, and regulatory impact per hour of downtime.
   - Establishes the **RTO and RPO** for each process.

2. **Phase 2 — Risk Assessment (RA)** *(1 mark)*:
   - Identifies threats (ransomware, insider threats, equipment failure).
   - Evaluates vulnerabilities in the Purdue architecture.
   - Calculates risk score: $R = P \times I \times V_c$.

3. **Phase 3 — Recovery Strategy Development** *(1 mark)*:
   - Selection of cold/warm/hot site or active-active architecture.
   - Strategy mapped to RTO/RPO requirements.

4. **Phase 4 — Plan Documentation** *(1 mark)*:
   - BCP and DRP documents.
   - RACI matrix, crisis communication plan, runbooks.

5. **Phase 5 — Testing and Validation** *(1 mark)*:
   - Tabletop exercises, parallel testing, failover drills.

6. **Phase 6 — Maintenance and Continuous Improvement** *(1 mark)*:
   - Annual plan review, post-incident updates, change management.

**BIA vs. RA Differentiation Table:**

| Aspect | BIA | Risk Assessment |
|--------|-----|------------------|
| **Focus** | Impact of downtime | Probability and source of disruption |
| **Question** | "What happens if X fails?" | "What is likely to fail X?" |
| **Output** | RTO, RPO, criticality tier | Risk register, threat catalog |
| **Standard** | ISO 22301, NIST SP 800-34 | NIST SP 800-30, IEC 62443-3-2 |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: RTO Calculation** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{RTO} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{decide}} + T_{\text{restore}}$$

$$\text{RTO} = 6 + 14 + 10 = 30 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2: RPO Calculation** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{RPO} = \vert T_{\text{disaster}} - T_{\text{last\_backup}} \vert$$

$$T_{\text{disaster}} - T_{\text{last\_backup}} = 14{:}38 - 14{:}20 = 18 \text{ minutes}$$

$$\text{RPO} = 18 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3: Compliance Check** *(1.5 marks)*

| Metric | Calculated | SIL-3 Mandate | Status |
|--------|-----------|---------------|--------|
| RTO | 30 min | $\le 45$ min | ✅ COMPLIANT |
| RPO | 18 min | $\le 3$ min | ❌ NON-COMPLIANT |

**Step 4: Revised Strategy Recommendation** *(1.5 marks)*

Since RPO is non-compliant, the recovery strategy must be enhanced:
- **Implement synchronous data replication** to a hot-standby historian (RPO → 0).
- **Add continuous change-data-capture (CDC) streaming** to an immutable WORM store.
- **Consider active-active PLC pair** with parallel logic execution for true zero RPO.
- **Schedule quarterly recovery drills** to validate the new strategy.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper based on 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Purdue Reference Model** and discuss how **recovery strategies must be tailored** for each of its levels in an integrated network. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **DCS server** has an MTBF of 10,000 hours and an MTTR of 3 hours.
**Calculate**:
   (i) The system availability $A$.
   (ii) The annual downtime in minutes.
   (iii) The required MTTR to achieve **Tier III compliance** (per TIA-942, $A \ge 99.982\%$), assuming MTBF remains constant.
   (iv) The Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) if the asset value is **₹2,00,00,000**, the Exposure Factor (EF) is **0.3**, and the ARO is **2 incidents per year**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **Purdue Reference Model (PRM)**, standardized as **ISA-99/IEC 62443**, segments industrial networks into **six hierarchical levels (0–5)** plus a critical **Level 3.5 DMZ**. Each level has distinct recovery requirements:

1. **Level 0 — Physical Process** *(1 mark)*: Sensors, actuators, motors. Recovery via **mechanical fail-safes and manual overrides** — no software can be relied upon at this level.

2. **Level 1 — Basic Control** *(1.5 marks)*: PLCs, DCS controllers. Recovery via **hot-standby redundant controllers** with 2oo2 or 2oo3 voting, sub-second failover.

3. **Level 2 — Supervisory Control** *(1.5 marks)*: HMI, SCADA. Recovery via **RAID-10 servers, image-based backups, VM snapshots**.

4. **Level 3 — Operations Management** *(1 mark)*: MES, Historians. Recovery via **warm-site failover, asynchronous database replication**.

5. **Level 3.5 — Industrial DMZ** *(1 mark)*: The security buffer. Recovery via **redundant firewalls, data diodes, stateless fail-open configurations**.

6. **Levels 4 & 5 — Enterprise IT** *(1 mark)*: ERP and corporate. Recovery via **standard IT DR (cloud replication, off-site tape)**.

**Key Principle**: Recovery strategies in OT are **driven by safety, not just economics**. Lower levels require faster, hardware-based recovery; higher levels can tolerate software-based, longer RTOs.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step (i): Availability Calculation** *(1.5 marks)*

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$$

$$A = \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 3} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 99.97\%}$$

**Step (ii): Annual Downtime** *(1.5 marks)*

$$D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$$

$$D_{\text{annual}} = 0.0003 \times 525{,}600$$

$$\boxed{D_{\text{annual}} \approx 157.7 \text{ minutes/year}}$$

**Step (iii): Required MTTR for Tier III** *(2 marks)*

We require $A \ge 99.982\%$, i.e., $A \ge 0.99982$.

$$\frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + \text{MTTR}_{\text{req}}} \ge 0.99982$$

$$10{,}000 \ge 0.99982 \times (10{,}000 + \text{MTTR}_{\text{req}})$$

$$10{,}000 \ge 9998.2 + 0.99982 \cdot \text{MTTR}_{\text{req}}$$

$$1.8 \ge 0.99982 \cdot \text{MTTR}_{\text{req}}$$

$$\text{MTTR}_{\text{req}} \le \frac{1.8}{0.99982} \approx 1.80 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTR}_{\text{req}} \le 1.8 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step (iv): Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** *(2 marks)*

First, compute SLE:

$$\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{EF}$$

$$\text{SLE} = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.3 = 60{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

Then compute ALE:

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$

$$\text{ALE} = 60{,}00{,}000 \times 2$$

$$\boxed{\text{ALE} = \text{₹ } 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Mixing seconds, minutes, and hours in RTO/RPO calculations. *Always convert to a single unit before computing.*
> 2. **Forgetting EF Range**: Exposure Factor must be $\text{EF} \in [0, 1]$. Writing EF as a percentage (e.g., 30 instead of 0.3) yields an SLE that is 100× too large.
> 3. **Confusing ALE with SLE**: ALE multiplies SLE by ARO. Many students forget ARO.
> 4. **Ignoring Safety**: In OT questions, even if RTO is met, an examiner will deduct marks if the **safety implication** of the failure is not discussed.
> 5. **Vague "Test the Plan" Answers**: When asked about BCP testing, students say *"test the plan"* without naming **tabletop exercise, parallel test, simulation, full-interruption test** — KTU expects specific terminology.
> 6. **Purdue Model Level Mismatch**: Mapping a Cold Site to Level 0 (impossible — Level 0 needs manual fail-safes). Always tie the strategy to the appropriate level.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PECCT634 — Module 4**

- [x] **BCP** is a management framework; **DRP** is the technical execution subset.
- [x] **Six phases of BCP**: BIA → RA → Strategy Design → Documentation → Testing → Maintenance.
- [x] **RTO** = $T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{decide}} + T_{\text{restore}}$ — total downtime tolerance.
- [x] **RPO** = $\vert T_{\text{disaster}} - T_{\text{last\_backup}} \vert$ — data loss tolerance.
- [x] **Availability** $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$.
- [x] **Annual Downtime** $D = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ min.
- [x] **SLE** = Asset Value $\times$ EF ; **ALE** = SLE $\times$ ARO.
- [x] **Purdue Model** has Levels 0–5 plus a critical **Level 3.5 DMZ**.
- [x] **Recovery Strategies in increasing order of cost & speed**: Cold → Warm → Hot → Active-Active.
- [x] **Safety dominates** in OT recovery: SIL levels (1–4) are derived from **IEC 61508**.
- [x] **OT systems cannot be casually tested live** — prefer **tabletop, simulation, parallel**.
- [x] **Tier III (TIA-942)** requires $A \ge 99.982\%$ (≤ 96 min downtime/year).
- [x] **Zero RPO** is achievable only with **synchronous replication or active-active** architectures.
- [x] **BIA answers "what must be recovered first?"** — RA answers "what is likely to fail it?"
- [x] **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3, ISA/IEC 62443-2-1, ISO 22301:2019** are the governing standards.
- [x] For KTU exams, **always state units, show all calculation steps, and discuss the safety implication** of any recovery gap.
